topics.title: Disassembling cylinder head, decarbonizing, valve timing setting including valve clearance adjustment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Disassembling cylinder head, decarbonizing, valve timing setting including valve clearance adjustment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Cylinder Head**
The cylinder head is the detachable uppermost component of an internal combustion engine that seals the combustion chamber from above. It houses the intake and exhaust valves, spark plug (SI engine) or injector (CI engine), and forms the upper half of the combustion chamber along with the piston crown and cylinder walls.

**Decarbonizing (Decarbonization / Decoking)**
The process of removing carbon deposits (also called carbonaceous residue or coke) from the combustion chamber components — piston crown, cylinder head, valve heads, valve seats, spark plug threads, and exhaust ports — using mechanical scraping, chemical solvents, or abrasive blasting.

**Valve Timing**
The precise angular relationship between the crankshaft position and the opening/closing instants of the intake and exhaust valves, expressed in degrees of crankshaft rotation before or after Top Dead Center (TDC) and Bottom Dead Center (BDC).

**Valve Clearance (Tappet Clearance / Lash)**
The small, deliberate gap between the valve stem tip (or rocker arm) and the cam lobe follower (tappet/pushrod) when the valve is in the fully closed position. It accommodates thermal expansion of valve train components during engine operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition**
> *Valve timing event = degrees of crankshaft rotation (NOT camshaft rotation) measured relative to TDC or BDC of the corresponding piston stroke.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **cylinder head as the "lid"** of a pressure cooker that traps the heat and pressure for cooking. Just like a pressure cooker lid has a safety valve that opens at the right moment to release steam, the cylinder head has intake valves (let fresh air-fuel mixture in) and exhaust valves (let burnt gases out). The exact moment these valves open and close is the **valve timing** — comparable to a perfectly choreographed dance.

**Decarbonizing** is like cleaning the burnt food residue from the inside of a pressure cooker after months of use — carbon builds up and reduces efficiency.

**Valve clearance** is like the small gap you leave in a metal pipe joint to allow for thermal expansion when hot water flows through — without it, the joint would crack or jam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Production Engine Valve Clearance (cold, typical)**:
> - Intake (SI petrol): **0.15 mm – 0.30 mm**
> - Exhaust (SI petrol): **0.25 mm – 0.45 mm**
> - CI diesel (typically larger due to higher thermal load): **0.20 mm – 0.40 mm**

### 1.3 Key Engine Components Referenced

| Component | Function | Material |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Cylinder Head | Seals combustion chamber, houses valves | Cast Iron / Aluminum Alloy |
| Intake Valve | Admits fresh charge | Stainless Steel / Inconel |
| Exhaust Valve | Releases burnt gases | Heat-Resistant Alloy (Silchrome) |
| Valve Seat | Sealing surface for valve face | Hardened Steel / Stellite |
| Valve Spring | Returns valve to closed position | Spring Steel (chrome-vanadium) |
| Rocker Arm | Transmits cam motion to valve | Hardened Steel |
| Tappet / Lifter | Follows cam profile | Hardened Steel |
| Camshaft | Drives valve actuation | Hardened Alloy Steel |
| Timing Belt/Chain | Synchronizes camshaft to crankshaft | Reinforced Rubber / Steel Chain |
| Piston Crown | Lower combustion chamber wall | Aluminum Alloy |
| Gasket (Head Gasket) | Seals head to block | Multi-layer Steel (MLS) / Composite |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — Disassembling the Cylinder Head

The cylinder head disassembly follows a strict reverse-engineering sequence. Each step must preserve component integrity for re-assembly and inspection.

**Pre-disassembly checks (Mandatory):**
- Engine must be **cold** (below 40 °C) to avoid warpage of aluminum heads.
- Battery disconnected, radiator drained, exhaust manifold bolts loosened.
- Camshaft position verified at TDC of compression stroke for #1 cylinder.

**Disassembly Logic Sequence:**

1. **De-pressurize auxiliary systems** — disconnect fuel lines, vacuum hoses, spark plug wires (SI) or fuel injection lines (CI).
2. **Remove valve cover (rocker cover)** — exposes rocker arm assembly and valve springs.
3. **Remove rocker arm shaft/assembly** — exposes pushrods (in OHV engines).
4. **Remove camshaft** — extract carefully from bearing journals to avoid scoring.
5. **Unbolt cylinder head** — follow reverse-tightening sequence (outside-to-inside) to prevent warping.
6. **Lift head off block** — use lifting eyes or two assistants for large cast-iron heads.
7. **Disassemble valves from head** — use a valve spring compressor tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip**: Always store removed bolts in their exact original positions. Different bolts have different torque values and lengths — interchanging them can cause thread damage or insufficient clamping load.

### 2.2 Decarbonizing — Theoretical Basis

Carbon deposits form due to:
- **Incomplete combustion** of fuel (especially rich mixtures)
- **Blow-by gases** carrying oil vapor into the combustion chamber
- **Fuel additives** leaving residue
- **High EGR rates** introducing particulates

**Effects of Excessive Carbon Build-up:**
- Increased compression ratio (knocking in SI, hard starting in CI)
- Reduced valve lift (especially exhaust valves → back-pressure)
- Hot spots causing pre-ignition/detonation
- Reduced heat transfer → overheating

**Decarbonizing Procedure (Step Logic):**

1. **Visual inspection** — quantify carbon layer thickness.
2. **Chemical soak** — use carbon-dissolving solvent (kerosene, diesel, or proprietary decarbonizer) for 15–30 minutes.
3. **Mechanical scraping** — use wooden/plastic scrapers (NEVER metal on aluminum to avoid gouging).
4. **Wire brushing** — for valve heads and piston crowns (rotary wire brush attachment).
5. **Sand/glass bead blasting** — industrial method for complete decarbonizing.
6. **Cleaning ports** — clear intake/exhaust port deposits.
7. **Lap valves to seats** — using valve grinding compound (fine abrasive paste).
8. **Final wash** — kerosene rinse and compressed air drying.

### 2.3 Valve Timing Setting — Deep Theory

Valve timing is specified by **four critical events** per valve per cycle (4-stroke engine):

| Event | Symbol | Typical Value (Petrol, example) |
|-------|--------|----------------------------------|
| Intake Valve Opens | IVO | 10°–20° Before TDC (BTDC) |
| Intake Valve Closes | IVC | 40°–70° After BDC (ABDC) |
| Exhaust Valve Opens | EVO | 40°–60° Before BDC (BBDC) |
| Exhaust Valve Closes | EVC | 10°–20° After TDC (ATDC) |

**Total Intake Event Duration (in degrees of crank rotation):**

$$\theta_{\text{intake}} = (180° + \text{IVO}_{\text{BTDC}}) + \text{IVC}_{\text{ABDC}}$$

**Total Exhaust Event Duration:**

$$\theta_{\text{exhaust}} = (180° + \text{EVO}_{\text{BBDC}}) + \text{EVC}_{\text{ATDC}}$$

**Overlap Period** (when both intake AND exhaust valves are simultaneously open):

$$\theta_{\text{overlap}} = \text{IVO}_{\text{BTDC}} + \text{EVC}_{\text{ATDC}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Overlap is INTENTIONALLY designed** in modern engines. It uses the momentum of exiting exhaust gases to draw in fresh charge (internal EGR effect), improving volumetric efficiency at mid-to-high RPM.

### 2.4 Valve Clearance Adjustment — Detailed Theory

**Why clearance is needed:**
- Metal components expand when heated.
- Valve stem elongates → if no clearance, valve cannot fully close when hot.
- Insufficient clearance (too tight) → burnt valves, loss of compression.
- Excessive clearance (too loose) → noisy operation, reduced valve lift, cam/tappet wear.

**Clearance Specification Format:**
Manufacturers specify **cold clearance** (engine at ambient temperature, ~20 °C) AND **hot clearance** (engine at operating temperature, ~80–90 °C). Hot clearance is typically **slightly less** than cold clearance.

**Adjustment Methods:**

1. **Screw-type tappet (OHV engines)** — loosen locknut, rotate adjusting screw, re-tighten locknut.
2. **Shim-type (bucket tappet, OHC engines)** — requires shim selection under bucket tappet. Clearance adjustment formula:

$$t_{\text{new shim}} = t_{\text{old shim}} + m_{\text{measured}} - m_{\text{target}}$$

Where $t_{\text{shim}}$ is shim thickness and $m$ is measured clearance.

3. **Hydraulic lash adjusters (HLA)** — self-adjusting; no manual clearance setting required.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|--------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| Crank-to-Cam Ratio | $\omega_{\text{cam}} = \frac{1}{2}\omega_{\text{crank}}$ | rad/s | 4-stroke engines |
| Intake Event Duration | $\theta_{\text{intake}} = 180° + \text{IVO}_{\text{BTDC}} + \text{IVC}_{\text{ABDC}}$ | degrees | Valve timing diagram |
| Exhaust Event Duration | $\theta_{\text{exhaust}} = 180° + \text{EVO}_{\text{BBDC}} + \text{EVC}_{\text{ATDC}}$ | degrees | Valve timing diagram |
| Valve Overlap | $\theta_{\text{overlap}} = \text{IVO}_{\text{BTDC}} + \text{EVC}_{\text{ATDC}}$ | degrees | Performance tuning |
| Shim Calculation | $t_{\text{new}} = t_{\text{old}} + (m_{\text{measured}} - m_{\text{target}})$ | mm | Bucket tappet adjustment |
| Cam Lift | $L_{\text{valve}} = L_{\text{cam}} \times R_{\text{rocker}}$ | mm | Valve train geometry |
| Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{V}_p = \frac{2 \cdot L_{\text{stroke}} \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | Engine speed limit |
| Valve Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | Clearance sizing |
| Compression Ratio | $r_c = \frac{V_d + V_c}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | Combustion chamber |
| Torque to Yield (Head Bolts) | $T = k \cdot d \cdot F_{\text{clamp}}$ | N·m | Re-assembly torque |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical**: All angular valve timing values are measured in **degrees of CRANKSHAFT rotation**, NOT camshaft rotation. Cam rotation is half of crank rotation in 4-stroke engines.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance tuning**: Adjusting valve timing (via VVT — Variable Valve Timing) is the primary method of modern engine optimization across the entire RPM range.
- **Emission control**: Proper overlap and timing reduces HC and CO emissions.
- **Fuel economy**: Correct clearance minimizes mechanical losses.
- **Engine longevity**: Proper decarbonizing restores compression and prevents pre-ignition, extending engine life by **30,000–50,000 km** in severely carboned engines.
- **Industrial relevance**: This experiment is foundational for automotive service technicians, engine re-builders, and aftermarket performance shops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Cam-to-Crank Angular Relationship (4-Stroke Engine)

A 4-stroke engine completes **one full working cycle** in **two crankshaft revolutions (720°)**. The camshaft, driven by a chain/belt at 1:2 ratio, completes **one revolution (360°)** in the same time.

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Crank revolution per cycle} = 2 \text{ revolutions} = 720°$$

$$\text{Cam revolution per cycle} = 1 \text{ revolution} = 360°$$

$$\therefore \frac{\theta_{\text{crank}}}{\theta_{\text{cam}}} = \frac{720°}{360°} = 2$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_{\text{cam}} = \frac{\theta_{\text{crank}}}{2}$$

**Example**: If IVO is specified as 12° BTDC (crank degrees), then the equivalent cam-shaft angle is:

$$\theta_{\text{cam, IVO}} = \frac{12°}{2} = 6° \text{ BTDC (cam degrees)}$$

This 2:1 ratio is fundamental when aligning timing marks on the camshaft sprocket vs crankshaft pulley.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Shim Thickness Calculation (Bucket Tappet Adjustment)

**Given:**
- Target intake clearance: $m_{\text{target}} = 0.20 \text{ mm}$ (cold)
- Measured clearance with old shim: $m_{\text{measured}} = 0.32 \text{ mm}$
- Old shim thickness: $t_{\text{old}} = 2.50 \text{ mm}$

**Logic**: If the measured clearance is **larger** than target, the tappet sits **higher**, meaning we need a **thicker** shim to push the tappet down and reduce clearance.

**Step 1: Identify the deviation**

$$\Delta m = m_{\text{measured}} - m_{\text{target}} = 0.32 - 0.20 = 0.12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Determine direction of correction**

Since $\Delta m > 0$ (clearance is too large), we must **increase** shim thickness by $\Delta m$.

**Step 3: Apply the shim formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{new shim}} &= t_{\text{old shim}} + \Delta m \\
t_{\text{new shim}} &= 2.50 \text{ mm} + 0.12 \text{ mm} \\
t_{\text{new shim}} &= 2.62 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result**: A shim of **2.62 mm** must be selected from the available shim kit (typically 2.40 mm to 3.00 mm in 0.02 mm increments).

**General formula:**

$$t_{\text{new shim}} = t_{\text{old shim}} + (m_{\text{measured}} - m_{\text{target}})$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Valve Thermal Expansion Compensation

**Given:**
- Valve stem length: $L_0 = 120 \text{ mm}$
- Coefficient of linear expansion (steel): $\alpha = 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$
- Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 80 - 20 = 60 \text{ °C}$

**Step 1: Apply linear thermal expansion formula**

$$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= (11.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 120 \times 60 \\
\Delta L &= 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 7200 \\
\Delta L &= 0.0828 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation**: The valve stem elongates by ~0.08 mm when hot. This is why intake valves (cooler, ~200–300 °C) need less clearance than exhaust valves (~600–800 °C). Exhaust valves need larger clearance because they expand more.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Timing Specifications

| Engine Type | IVO (BTDC) | IVC (ABDC) | EVO (BBDC) | EVC (ATDC) | Overlap |
|-------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|---------|
| Small Petrol (low RPM) | 10° | 45° | 45° | 10° | 20° |
| High-Performance Petrol | 25° | 70° | 65° | 25° | 50° |
| Diesel (Indirect Injection) | 8° | 35° | 35° | 8° | 16° |
| Diesel (Direct Injection) | 12° | 40° | 40° | 12° | 24° |
| Racing Engine (high overlap) | 40° | 80° | 80° | 40° | 80° |

**Analytical insight**: Racing engines use extreme overlap to maximize scavenging at high RPM, sacrificing low-end torque. Production engines balance overlap for drivability.

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Numerical — Valve Clearance Setting (Rocker Arm Type)

**Given engine**: 4-cylinder, OHV, petrol, firing order 1-3-4-2

**Target clearance** (cold):
- Intake: **0.20 mm**
- Exhaust: **0.30 mm**

**Step 1: Set cylinder #1 to TDC compression stroke**
Rotate crankshaft clockwise until TDC mark on pulley aligns with pointer. Both valves of #1 cylinder must be closed (rocker arms loose on cam lobes).

**Step 2: Adjust valves for cylinder #1 (intake and exhaust)**
Using feeler gauge, insert between rocker arm tip and valve stem. Adjust screw until drag is felt, then lock locknut while holding screw stationary.

**Step 3: Rotate crankshaft 180° clockwise**
Now cylinder #4 is at TDC compression. Adjust valves of cylinder #4.

**Step 4: Repeat for cylinders #3 and #2**

| Step | Rotation | Cylinder at TDC Compression | Valves Adjusted |
|------|----------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 0° | #1 | Intake #1, Exhaust #1 |
| 2 | 180° | #4 | Intake #4, Exhaust #4 |
| 3 | 360° | #3 | Intake #3, Exhaust #3 |
| 4 | 540° | #2 | Intake #2, Exhaust #2 |

**Alternative "1-4-3-2" method** allows adjustment of all valves with fewer crank rotations by exploiting the firing order — but the above method is the simplest for lab demonstration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cylinder Head Disassembly Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engine Cold, Battery Disconnected] --> B[Remove Engine Covers and Auxiliaries]
    B --> C[Drain Coolant and Oil from Head]
    C --> D[Disconnect Fuel Lines, Vacuum Hoses, Spark Plug Wires]
    D --> E[Remove Valve Rocker Cover]
    E --> F[Remove Rocker Arm Assembly or Camshaft]
    F --> G[Loosen Head Bolts in Reverse Sequence]
    G --> H[Lift Cylinder Head Off Block]
    H --> I[Place Head on Clean Workbench]
    I --> J[Use Valve Spring Compressor to Remove Valves]
    J --> K[Label and Store All Components]
    K --> L[Inspect Head for Warpage and Cracks]
    L --> M[End: Ready for Decarbonizing]
    
    stepA[Check Head Flatness with Straightedge] --> L
    stepB[Inspect Valve Seats and Guides] --> L
    stepC[Measure Valve Stem Diameter] --> L
```

### 4.2 Decarbonizing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Inspected Cylinder Head and Valves] --> P2[Soak in Decarbonizing Solvent]
    P2 --> P3[Wait 20 to 30 Minutes]
    P3 --> P4[Mechanical Scraping with Plastic Tool]
    P4 --> P5[Wire Brush Carbon from Valve Heads]
    P5 --> P6[Clean Combustion Chamber with Rotary Tool]
    P6 --> P7[Clean Intake and Exhaust Ports]
    P7 --> P8[Apply Valve Grinding Compound to Seat]
    P8 --> P9[Lap Valves Using Suction Cup Tool]
    P9 --> P10[Wash Components with Kerosene]
    P10 --> P11[Dry with Compressed Air]
    P11 --> P12[Inspect for Clean Surfaces]
    P12 --> P13{All Carbon Removed?}
    P13 -- No --> P4
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[End: Ready for Reassembly]
```

### 4.3 Valve Timing Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Crank_Position
        BDCA[BDC of Intake Stroke]
        TDCC[TDC at End of Compression]
        BDCB[BDC of Power Stroke]
        TDCE[TDC at End of Exhaust]
    end
    
    subgraph Intake_Valve_State
        IV_closed_a[Intake Valve Closed]
        IV_open[Intake Valve OPEN]
        IV_closed_b[Intake Valve Closed]
    end
    
    subgraph Exhaust_Valve_State
        EV_closed_a[Exhaust Valve Closed]
        EV_open[Exhaust Valve OPEN]
        EV_closed_b[Exhaust Valve Closed]
    end
    
    BDCA --> IV_closed_a
    IV_closed_a --> IV_open
    IV_open --> IV_closed_b
    IV_closed_b --> TDCC
    TDCC --> EV_closed_b
    EV_closed_b --> EV_open
    EV_open --> EV_closed_a
    EV_closed_a --> BDCB
    BDCB --> TDCE
    TDCE --> IV_closed_a
```

### 4.4 Valve Clearance Adjustment Procedure (OHV Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Engine Cold, TDC Cyl 1 Compression] --> Q2[Check Intake Clearance Cyl 1 with Feeler Gauge]
    Q2 --> Q3{Clearance Correct?}
    Q3 -- No --> Q4[Loosen Locknut and Adjust Screw]
    Q4 --> Q5[Tighten Locknut While Holding Screw]
    Q5 --> Q6[Reverify Clearance]
    Q6 --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q7[Check Exhaust Clearance Cyl 1]
    Q7 --> Q8{Clearance Correct?}
    Q8 -- No --> Q4
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Rotate Crank 180 Degrees]
    Q9 --> Q10[Adjust Valves Cyl 4]
    Q10 --> Q11[Rotate Crank 180 Degrees]
    Q11 --> Q12[Adjust Valves Cyl 3]
    Q12 --> Q13[Rotate Crank 180 Degrees]
    Q13 --> Q14[Adjust Valves Cyl 2]
    Q14 --> Q15[End: All Clearances Set]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Valve Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CR[Crankshaft] -->|1:2 ratio| CS[Camshaft]
    CS -->|Cam lobe lift| TA[Tappet / Lifter]
    TA -->|Push motion| PR[Pushrod OHV]
    PR --> RA[Rocker Arm]
    RA -->|Pivot and amplify| VS[Valve Stem Tip]
    VS --> VC[Valve Closes on Seat]
    
    CR2[Crankshaft Pulley] -->|Timing chain or belt| CS2[Camshaft Sprocket]
    CS2 --> CS
    
    style CR fill:#f9e,stroke:#333
    style CS fill:#bcf,stroke:#333
    style VC fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation**: This shows the kinematic chain from crankshaft rotation → cam lobe lift → tappet → pushrod → rocker arm pivot → valve stem → valve-seat contact. Clearance exists in this chain between rocker arm tip and valve stem tip (OHV) or between cam lobe and bucket tappet (OHC).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define valve timing. List the four timing events for an exhaust valve with typical angle values.

**Model Answer:**

Valve timing is the precise angular relationship between the crankshaft position and the opening or closing instant of the intake and exhaust valves, expressed in degrees of crankshaft rotation relative to TDC or BDC.

**Four exhaust valve events:**

| Event | Symbol | Typical Value |
|-------|--------|---------------|
| Exhaust Valve Opens | EVO | 40°–60° BBDC |
| Exhaust Valve Closes | EVC | 10°–20° ATDC |

*(Examiner expects the student to also mention: total exhaust event spans roughly 240°, that values are crank degrees, and that overlap exists between IVO and EVC.)*

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1 mark), Four events with symbols (1 mark), Typical values with units (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
What is valve clearance? Why is it necessary in an internal combustion engine?

**Model Answer:**

Valve clearance (also called tappet clearance or lash) is the small intentional gap between the valve stem tip and the rocker arm (OHV) or between the cam lobe and the bucket tappet (OHC) when the valve is fully closed.

**Necessity:**
1. To accommodate thermal expansion of valve train components (valve stem elongates when hot).
2. To ensure the valve seats fully and seals the combustion chamber when operating temperature is reached.
3. To prevent valve stem damage from being held partially open continuously.
4. To compensate for manufacturing tolerances and wear over service life.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often answer "to reduce noise" as the primary reason. This is a *consequence* of incorrect clearance, not the *primary engineering reason*. The correct primary reason is **thermal expansion compensation**.

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1 mark), Two valid reasons (1 mark each, 2 marks).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe with a neat procedure the step-by-step process of disassembling a cylinder head from a 4-cylinder petrol engine. Mention the tools used and safety precautions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the decarbonizing process in detail. List the tools, materials used, and the inspection criteria after decarbonizing.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cylinder Head Disassembly Procedure:**

1. **Preparation (1 mark)**
   - Ensure engine is cold (below 40 °C, especially aluminum heads).
   - Disconnect battery negative terminal.
   - Drain engine coolant from radiator and cylinder block drain plug.
   - Remove air cleaner assembly, fuel lines (depressurize first), and vacuum hoses.

2. **Auxiliary Component Removal (1 mark)**
   - Disconnect spark plug wires, label each for re-assembly.
   - Remove intake manifold and exhaust manifold bolts.
   - Remove thermostat housing if attached to head.

3. **Valve Cover and Rocker Arm Removal (1 mark)**
   - Remove valve (rocker) cover bolts in criss-cross pattern.
   - Lift off cover; inspect gasket for reuse.
   - For OHV engine: loosen rocker arm shaft bolts gradually and remove rocker arm assembly.
   - For OHC engine: remove camshaft cover, then remove camshaft.

4. **Pushrod and Head Bolt Removal (1.5 marks)**
   - Remove pushrods (OHV), label and store in order.
   - Loosen cylinder head bolts in **reverse tightening sequence** (outside-to-inside, in 2–3 stages) to prevent head warping.
   - Tools used: socket wrench set, torque wrench (for recording original torque), breaker bar.

5. **Head Lifting and Inspection (1.5 marks)**
   - Use lifting hooks or two-person lift for heavy cast-iron heads.
   - Place head on clean wooden surface (NEVER on concrete — scratches sealing face).
   - Inspect head gasket; replace with new MLS gasket.
   - Inspect head for cracks (dye-penetration test) and warpage (straightedge + feeler gauge; max allowable: 0.05 mm for aluminum, 0.10 mm for cast iron).

6. **Valve Removal (1 mark)**
   - Use valve spring compressor tool to compress valve spring.
   - Remove collets, spring retainer, spring, and valve.
   - Label each valve with cylinder number; inspect for burned seats, bent stems, and pitting.

**Safety Precautions (mention any 4 for full marks):**
- Wear safety glasses — eye injury from spring release is the #1 risk.
- Use proper lifting technique — heads weigh 15–30 kg.
- Never strike aluminum head with steel tools — use brass or wooden mallets.
- Keep work area clean — dropped valves can chip or bend.
- Store fasteners properly — never mix bolts of different grades.

**(b) Decarbonizing Process:**

1. **Purpose (1 mark)**
   Carbon deposits form from incomplete combustion, oil blow-by, and fuel additives. They cause knocking, increased compression, reduced valve lift, and overheating. Decarbonizing restores engine efficiency.

2. **Tools and Materials (1.5 marks)**
   - Decarbonizing solvent (kerosene, diesel, or proprietary carb-cleaner).
   - Wooden scrapers, plastic scrapers, wire brushes.
   - Rotary wire brush attachment for drill.
   - Valve grinding compound (fine grade, 120–240 grit).
   - Valve lapping tool (suction cup handle).
   - Compressed air source.
   - Clean lint-free rags.

3. **Procedure (3 marks)**
   - Soak carbon-affected parts in solvent for 20–30 minutes.
   - Scrape loose deposits with wooden/plastic scraper (avoid metal on aluminum).
   - Use rotary wire brush for stubborn deposits on piston crowns and valve faces.
   - For valve seats: apply thin layer of valve grinding compound to seat face, attach suction cup to valve head, rotate valve back-and-forth with light pressure for 1–2 minutes. **Lift valve periodically** to redistribute compound and prevent grooving.
   - Wipe away all compound residue with kerosene-soaked rag.
   - Blow dry with compressed air.

4. **Inspection Criteria (1.5 marks)**
   - Combustion chamber surfaces should be **uniformly clean**, no black carbon film.
   - Valve seat contact pattern should be a **continuous gray ring** 1.5–2.0 mm wide, centered on seat face.
   - No deep scratches, pitting, or erosion marks visible.
   - Valve stem and guide should be free of carbon and show no scoring.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain valve timing diagram for a 4-stroke petrol engine. Define valve overlap and state its significance.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the procedure for setting valve clearance in an OHV engine. Mention the standard clearance values for intake and exhaust valves.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Valve Timing Diagram — Description:**

The valve timing diagram is a circular plot showing the angular positions (in crank degrees) at which each valve opens and closes, plotted against the piston position (TDC and BDC of each stroke).

**Standard Events (Petrol Engine):**

| Event | Angle |
|-------|-------|
| Intake Valve Opens (IVO) | 10°–20° BTDC |
| Intake Valve Closes (IVC) | 40°–70° ABDC |
| Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO) | 40°–60° BBDC |
| Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC) | 10°–20° ATDC |

**Diagram Layout:** A circle representing 720° (2 crank revolutions) divided into four 180° quadrants — Intake stroke, Compression stroke, Power stroke, Exhaust stroke. The intake valve curve shows opening before TDC at end of exhaust and closing after BDC at end of intake. The exhaust valve curve shows opening before BDC of power stroke and closing after TDC at end of exhaust.

**Valve Overlap:**
The brief period (typically 10°–40° of crank rotation) at TDC between exhaust stroke end and intake stroke beginning, when **both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open**.

**Significance of Valve Overlap:**

1. **Scavenging effect**: Momentum of exiting exhaust gases creates a low-pressure region that helps draw fresh charge into the cylinder, improving volumetric efficiency.
2. **Internal EGR**: Small amount of residual exhaust gas mixes with fresh charge, reducing peak combustion temperature and NOx formation.
3. **Idle quality impact**: Excessive overlap causes rough idling; insufficient overlap reduces high-RPM power.
4. **Modern VVT systems**: Continuously vary overlap from low values at idle to high values at high RPM.

**(b) Valve Clearance Setting Procedure (OHV Engine):**

**Tools Required:**
- Feeler gauge set (0.05 mm to 1.00 mm range)
- Open-end/ring spanner (size as per locknut)
- Screwdriver (for adjuster slot)
- Workshop manual (for specific clearance values)

**Standard Clearance Values (Typical Petrol OHV):**

| Valve | Cold Clearance | Hot Clearance |
|-------|---------------|---------------|
| Intake | 0.15 mm (0.006 in) | 0.10 mm (0.004 in) |
| Exhaust | 0.25 mm (0.010 in) | 0.20 mm (0.008 in) |

**Procedure:**

1. **Set engine to TDC compression for cylinder #1** (1 mark)
   - Rotate crankshaft clockwise until TDC mark aligns with timing pointer.
   - Verify both valves of #1 cylinder are closed (rocker arms have clearance).

2. **Adjust intake valve of cylinder #1** (1 mark)
   - Insert feeler gauge between rocker arm tip and valve stem tip.
   - If gauge drags, clearance is correct. If loose, tighten adjuster screw; if too tight, loosen.
   - Hold adjuster screw stationary while tightening locknut.
   - Re-verify clearance after locknut tightening.

3. **Adjust exhaust valve of cylinder #1** (1 mark)
   - Repeat the same process with the appropriate exhaust feeler gauge.

4. **Rotate crankshaft 180° and adjust #4 cylinder** (1 mark)
   - This puts #4 at TDC compression.

5. **Rotate another 180° and adjust #3 cylinder** (1 mark)

6. **Rotate another 180° and adjust #2 cylinder** (1 mark)

7. **Final verification and re-check** (1 mark)
   - Re-rotate to TDC #1 and re-verify all settings.
   - Re-install valve cover with new gasket.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing cam degrees with crank degrees** — students often state timing values as cam degrees. KTU board strictly expects **crank degrees** unless explicitly specified otherwise.
> 2. **Incorrect sequence for setting valve clearance** — many students set all intake valves first, then all exhaust. KTU follows the **firing order method**: adjust both valves of one cylinder, rotate 180°, repeat.
> 3. **Forgetting thermal expansion reason for clearance** — answers must mention thermal expansion explicitly, not just "to reduce noise".
> 4. **Overlooking safety** — KTU deducts 1–2 marks if safety precautions are completely omitted in disassembly answers.
> 5. **Mixing up valve timing events** — IVO vs EVO is a frequent error. Memorize: **I**ntake **O**pens first chronologically (BTDC of intake stroke), **E**xhaust **C**loses last (ATDC of exhaust stroke).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] Cylinder head must be **completely cold** before removal (aluminum warps easily).
- [x] Head bolts loosened in **reverse sequence** (outside-to-inside) to prevent warping.
- [x] Always use a **new head gasket** on re-assembly; never reuse a compressed gasket.
- [x] Decarbonizing solvents take **20–30 minutes** to soften carbon; never use metal scrapers on aluminum.
- [x] Valve lapping creates a **uniform gray ring 1.5–2.0 mm wide** centered on the seat face.
- [x] Valve timing is measured in **degrees of crankshaft rotation** (not cam rotation).
- [x] **Cam-to-crank ratio** in 4-stroke engines is **1:2** (crank rotates twice per cam revolution).
- [x] **Overlap** = IVO (BTDC) + EVC (ATDC) — improves scavenging but reduces idle quality.
- [x] **Intake clearance** is **less** than exhaust clearance because exhaust valves run hotter.
- [x] **Shim formula**: $t_{\text{new}} = t_{\text{old}} + (m_{\text{measured}} - m_{\text{target}})$.
- [x] **Feeler gauge method**: drag-fit = correct clearance. Loose = too much gap. Cannot insert = too tight.
- [x] **Firing order method** (e.g., 1-3-4-2) for valve adjustment requires rotating crank **180°** between cylinders.
- [x] **Compression stroke identification**: Both valves closed + piston at TDC + air pressure felt when turning crank.
- [x] Safety first — **safety glasses mandatory** during spring compressor use.
- [x] **Valve spring compressor** is the only safe tool for valve removal from head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Weightage Tip**: This experiment typically carries **15–20 marks** in the lab internal assessment and is a frequent question in the **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** sections of the University ESE. Mastery of valve timing diagrams and clearance adjustment procedures is essential for scoring above 80%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression test of petrol and diesel engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Compression test of petrol and diesel engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Compression Test of a Petrol and Diesel Engine** is a fundamental laboratory experiment conducted in the *Vehicle Systems Lab* (PCAUL308) to determine the **Compression Ratio (CR)** of an internal combustion (IC) engine cylinder. The test is carried out by cranking the engine (without fuel ignition) using an external motor or starter, and observing the maximum air pressure built up inside the combustion chamber. The compression ratio is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of the **Total Cylinder Volume** to the **Clearance Volume**.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$CR = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{V_1}{V_c}$$

where $V_s$ is the **Swept Volume (Displacement)**, $V_c$ is the **Clearance Volume**, and $V_1$ is the **Total Cylinder Volume at BDC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAUL308 - Module 2):**
> The compression test is *NOT* a power test. It is a **diagnostic and geometric** experiment used to verify the geometric compression ratio of the engine, detect valve leakage, piston ring wear, and head gasket failures. KTU expects students to record both **theoretical CR** (from dimensions) and **actual CR** (from pressure gauge readings) and compare them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing air into a sealed plastic bottle with a small syringe pump. If you push the piston in slowly, the air compresses and you feel back-pressure on your hand. Now, if you use a *shorter* bottle (less clearance space), the same push of the piston produces a much higher back-pressure — because the air has less room to occupy.

- The **length of the syringe stroke** = Swept Volume ($V_s$).
- The **dead space at the top** (before the piston starts) = Clearance Volume ($V_c$).
- The **back-pressure you feel** = Indicated Pressure on the Compression Gauge.

A **diesel engine** is like the *shorter bottle* — it has very little dead space, so it squeezes air extremely hard (high compression), hot enough to ignite fuel without a spark plug. A **petrol engine** has comparatively more dead space, so it only squeezes moderately and relies on a spark plug.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Board Exam:**
> Petrol Engine CR ≈ **8 : 1 to 12 : 1**
> Diesel Engine CR ≈ **14 : 1 to 22 : 1**
> (Modern CRDI diesels may exceed 22:1)

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Bore (D)** — Cylinder inner diameter — typically **70 mm to 100 mm** for small engines.
- **Stroke (L)** — Piston travel distance — typically **80 mm to 120 mm**.
- **Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm)** — **1.01325 bar** or **101.325 kPa** (assumed standard).
- **Polytropic Index (n) for compression of air** — approximately **1.35** (during cranking, due to heat loss to walls).
- **Adiabatic Index (γ) for ideal air** — **1.4**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love testing the difference between **Isentropic (γ = 1.4)** and **Polytropic (n = 1.35)** compression — remember: the *actual cranking process* is closer to polytropic because the piston moves slowly and heat escapes through the cylinder walls.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Test is Performed

The compression test is performed for the following engineering purposes:

1. **Determination of Compression Ratio** — To compare the manufacturer's specified CR with the experimentally obtained CR.
2. **Condition Monitoring** — A low pressure reading indicates:
   - Worn-out piston rings (blow-by).
   - Leaking valves (intake or exhaust).
   - Blown head gasket.
   - Cracked cylinder head.
3. **Comparative Study** — To demonstrate why a diesel engine has nearly **twice** the compression ratio of a petrol engine.

## 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment proceeds in the following logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Disengage all valves from actuation.** The engine is brought to a state where it cannot start; only the starter motor turns the crankshaft.
- **Step 2 — Remove all spark plugs / injectors.** This prevents ignition and provides a port to insert the compression gauge.
- **Step 3 — Open the throttle fully (for petrol engines).** This ensures maximum air entry to simulate actual operating conditions.
- **Step 4 — Crank the engine using the starter motor.** The piston travels from Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) to Top Dead Centre (TDC), compressing the trapped air.
- **Step 5 — Record the maximum gauge pressure** $P_{max}$ indicated on the compression tester gauge.
- **Step 6 — Apply the polytropic relation** to back-calculate the actual compression ratio:

$$P_{max} \cdot V_c^{\,n} = P_{atm} \cdot V_1^{\,n}$$

- **Step 7 — Calculate CR** using dimensions and pressure readings.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept Volume | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L$ | m³ or cm³ | Geometric |
| 2 | Clearance Volume | $V_c = \frac{V_s}{(CR - 1)}$ | m³ or cm³ | From manufacturer / measured |
| 3 | Total Cylinder Volume | $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ | m³ or cm³ | At BDC |
| 4 | Compression Ratio (Geometric) | $CR = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{V_1}{V_c}$ | Dimensionless | Petrol: 8–12; Diesel: 14–22 |
| 5 | Polytropic Compression Pressure | $P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{\,n}$ | bar or kPa | $n \approx 1.35$ during cranking |
| 6 | Isentropic (Ideal) Compression Pressure | $P_{max,\,ideal} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{\,\gamma}$ | bar or kPa | $\gamma = 1.4$ |
| 7 | CR from Pressure (Polytropic) | $CR = \left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1/n}$ | Dimensionless | Actual CR |
| 8 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $P_{im} = \dfrac{P_{max} \cdot n}{n - 1} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_c}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | kPa | Rarely asked |
| 9 | % Error in CR | $\%\text{Error} = \left\vert \dfrac{CR_{theoretical} - CR_{actual}}{CR_{theoretical}} \right\vert \times 100$ | % | Always report |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The compression test is the *single most important on-vehicle diagnostic test* performed by automobile service technicians worldwide. It is included in the AIS-052 serviceability norms followed by Indian OEMs and is part of the **ASE A1 / A8 Engine Repair Certification** globally.

## 2.4 Why Petrol and Diesel Differ — Thermodynamic Justification

| Parameter | Petrol Engine (SI) | Diesel Engine (CI) |
|---|---|---|
| Ignition Method | Spark plug | Compression ignition |
| Required CR | 8–12 | 14–22 |
| Compression Temperature | ~400 °C (needs spark) | ~700–900 °C (auto-ignites) |
| Throttle During Test | Fully OPEN | Not applicable (no throttle) |
| Fuel Injection | Port / Direct | Direct injection |
| Polytropic n | 1.32–1.36 | 1.33–1.38 |

The diesel engine must achieve a temperature above the **auto-ignition temperature of diesel (~210 °C)** purely through compression, hence the high CR. The petrol engine controls power via throttle; hence it must not knock, so CR is limited.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Compression Ratio from Cylinder Dimensions

Given:
- Bore of cylinder = $D$ (in mm)
- Stroke of cylinder = $L$ (in mm)
- Clearance volume (from manufacturer / measured) = $V_c$

**Step 1:** Express the swept volume of a single cylinder.

$$V_s = \text{Area of piston} \times \text{Stroke}$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** Total cylinder volume when piston is at BDC = swept volume + clearance volume.

$$V_1 = V_s + V_c$$

**Step 3:** Compression ratio definition.

$$CR = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 3 to obtain an expression purely in terms of geometry (when $V_c$ is known).

$$CR = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4 V_c}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polytropic Compression Relation (PV^n = constant)

During the actual cranking process, the compression of air is **not perfectly isentropic** because:
1. The piston moves slowly.
2. Heat is lost to the cylinder walls.
3. Valve leakage may occur.

The process is best modelled as **polytropic**, where:

$$P \cdot V^{n} = \text{constant}$$

At state 1 (BDC): $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 = V_s + V_c$
At state 2 (TDC): $P_2 = P_{max}$, $V_2 = V_c$

Applying polytropic relation:

$$P_{atm} \cdot V_1^{\,n} = P_{max} \cdot V_c^{\,n}$$

**Rearranging** to solve for pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_c}\right)^{n} = (CR)^{n}$$

**Therefore the maximum compression pressure:**

$$P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{n}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Actual CR from Observed Pressure

Starting from the polytropic result:

$$P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{n}$$

Divide both sides by $P_{atm}$:

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}} = (CR)^{n}$$

Raise both sides to power $\tfrac{1}{n}$:

$$CR = \left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1/n}$$

This is the **formula used to back-calculate actual compression ratio** from the gauge reading.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical Problem)

**Given:**
- Petrol engine, single cylinder
- Bore $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- Stroke $L = 90$ mm $= 0.090$ m
- Clearance volume $V_c = 50$ cm³
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar
- Polytropic index $n = 1.35$

**Find:**
(a) Theoretical Compression Ratio
(b) Maximum pressure expected if engine is healthy
(c) Actual CR if pressure gauge reads 9.5 bar

### Solution (a) — Theoretical CR

**Step 1:** Calculate swept volume in cm³.

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (8.0)^{2} \cdot 9.0$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 64 \cdot 9.0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 576 = 452.39 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 2:** Apply CR formula.

$$CR_{theoretical} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{452.39 + 50}{50} = \frac{502.39}{50}$$

$$CR_{theoretical} = 10.05 \approx 10{:}1$$

This is a typical petrol engine compression ratio.

### Solution (b) — Expected Maximum Pressure

**Step 3:** Apply polytropic pressure equation.

$$P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{n} = 1.013 \cdot (10.05)^{1.35}$$

Compute $(10.05)^{1.35}$:

$$(10.05)^{1.35} \approx 21.41$$

**Step 4:** Final pressure.

$$P_{max} = 1.013 \times 21.41 \approx 21.69 \text{ bar}$$

A healthy petrol engine of CR 10:1 should read approximately **21.7 bar** on the compression gauge.

### Solution (c) — Actual CR from Gauge Reading

**Step 5:** Rearrange polytropic equation.

$$CR_{actual} = \left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{9.5}{1.013}\right)^{1/1.35}$$

$$CR_{actual} = (9.378)^{0.7407} \approx 5.81$$

**Step 6:** Error calculation.

$$\%\text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{10.05 - 5.81}{10.05} \right\vert \times 100 \approx 42.2\%$$

This large error (>15%) indicates a faulty engine — likely **worn piston rings or leaking valves**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Diagnosing Engine Health

| Observed $P_{max}$ vs. Expected | Likely Fault | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Within ±10% of expected | Healthy engine | None |
| 10–20% low | Mild ring wear | Top overhaul in 5000 km |
| 20–40% low | Severe ring/valve leak | Engine rebuild |
| >40% low | Head gasket blown / cracked block | Major repair |
| Gauge reads $P_{atm}$ only | Mechanical seizure or broken timing | Disassemble engine |
| Pressure rises then drops slowly | Valve seat leakage | Valve grinding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Practical Exam Viva:** Always state that a *healthy* petrol engine should not lose more than **10%** of its cranking pressure in the first 5 seconds after cranking stops. This is called the **Pressure Retention Test**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Compression Test Setup

The following Mermaid diagram maps the components and flow of the compression test apparatus — a Block-Level Functional Architecture rather than a physical drawing, ensuring accurate rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery 12V DC] --> B[Starter Motor]
    B --> C[Cranking Pulley]
    C --> D[Engine Crankshaft]
    D --> E[Piston Moves BDC to TDC]
    E --> F[Intake Valve Closed]
    E --> G[Exhaust Valve Closed]
    E --> H[Spark Plug Removed Port Open]
    H --> I[Compression Gauge Adapter]
    I --> J[Compression Gauge 0 to 30 bar]
    J --> K[Maximum Pressure Reading Pmax]
    K --> L[Comparison with Ptheoretical]
    L --> M{Healthy Engine?}
    M -- Yes --> N[CR within plus minus 10 percent]
    M -- No --> O[Diagnose Rings Valves Gasket]
    styleA fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    styleB fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#000
    styleC fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#000
    styleD fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#000
    styleE fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#000
    styleF fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#000
    styleG fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#000

    styleI fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#000
    styleJ fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000

    styleO fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#000
```

## 4.2 P-V Diagram Schematic (Mermaid Flow Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    St1[State 1 BDC P atm V total] -->|Polytropic Compression n 1.35| St2[State 2 TDC P max V clearance]
    St2 -->|Expansion on Release of Crank| St1
    St1 -.->|Heat Loss to Walls| QL[Q out to coolant]
    St2 -.->|Work Input from Starter| Win[W in from starter motor]
    styleSt1 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    styleSt2 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#000
    styleQL fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#000
    styleWin fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Stepwise Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Start engine to bring to operating temp] --> S2[Step 2 Remove all spark plugs and injectors]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Open throttle fully for petrol engines]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Insert compression gauge in cylinder 1 port]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Crank engine for 5 to 7 strokes via starter]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Record maximum gauge reading Pmax]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Repeat for all cylinders]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Calculate CR theoretical from dimensions]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Calculate CR actual from Pmax using polytropic formula]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Compare and tabulate percentage error]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Conclusion on engine condition]
    styleS1 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#000
    styleS2 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#000
    styleS3 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    styleS4 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#000
    styleS5 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#000
    styleS6 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    styleS7 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    styleS8 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#000
    styleS9 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#000
    styleS10 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#000
    styleS11 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Apparatus Required (Table Format)

| S.No | Apparatus | Specification | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Petrol engine test rig | 4-stroke, single cylinder | 1 |
| 2 | Diesel engine test rig | 4-stroke, single cylinder | 1 |
| 3 | Compression tester gauge | 0–30 bar, calibrated | 1 |
| 4 | Compression gauge adapter | Engine-specific rubber cone | 1 |
| 5 | Spark plug / injector remover | Standard service tool | 1 |
| 6 | Vernier caliper | 0.02 mm accuracy | 1 |
| 7 | Stop watch | 0.1 s accuracy | 1 |
| 8 | Tachometer (optional) | For cranking RPM | 1 |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define compression ratio. Why is the compression ratio of a diesel engine higher than that of a petrol engine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The compression ratio is the ratio of the total volume of the combustion chamber (when the piston is at BDC) to the clearance volume (when the piston is at TDC). It is expressed as $CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$. A diesel engine has a higher compression ratio (14:1 to 22:1) compared to a petrol engine (8:1 to 12:1) because in a diesel engine, air must be compressed to a temperature above the auto-ignition temperature of diesel (~210 °C), which requires high compression. A petrol engine relies on a spark plug for ignition, so its CR is limited to avoid knocking, where the air-fuel mixture auto-ignites prematurely.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the polytropic relation used in the compression test and explain why the index n is taken as 1.35 instead of 1.4.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The polytropic relation is $P V^{n} = \text{constant}$, where $P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{n}$. The index $n$ is taken as **1.35** (not 1.4) because the actual cranking process is not perfectly adiabatic — heat is lost to the cylinder walls, valves, and piston during the slow cranking stroke. The value 1.4 ($\gamma$ for air) corresponds to an ideal isentropic process with no heat loss, whereas 1.35 empirically accounts for real-world heat transfer during engine cranking.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always include units** in your final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (especially bar, cm³, mm).
> 2. **Never confuse** the *Polytropic Index (n)* with the *Specific Heat Ratio (γ)* — they look similar but have different physical meaning.
> 3. **Do not forget** to state assumptions: standard atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar), no valve leakage, dry air.
> 4. **Always tabulate** observations neatly with bore, stroke, $V_c$, $P_{atm}$, $P_{max}$, CR theoretical, CR actual, % error.
> 5. **Mention** that petrol engine CR is limited to avoid *knocking* — this is a favourite 1-mark sub-question in viva.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to conduct the compression test on a single-cylinder petrol engine. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 85 mm, a stroke of 95 mm, and a clearance volume of 45 cm³. During a compression test, the maximum gauge pressure recorded is 18 bar at a cranking speed of 200 RPM. Atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar. Take the polytropic index n = 1.35.
   (i) Calculate the theoretical compression ratio.
   (ii) Calculate the actual compression ratio from the pressure reading.
   (iii) Comment on the engine condition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Procedure & Apparatus (7 marks):**

1. **Apparatus (2 marks):** Petrol engine test rig, compression tester gauge (0–30 bar), gauge adapter, spark plug wrench, vernier caliper, stopwatch.

2. **Procedure (5 marks):**
   - Start the engine and bring it to normal operating temperature, then switch off.
   - Remove all spark plugs to prevent ignition and to relieve any compression.
   - For petrol engine, keep the throttle fully open; choke closed.
   - Insert the compression gauge firmly into the spark plug port of cylinder 1.
   - Crank the engine using the starter motor for 5–7 continuous strokes.
   - Record the maximum pressure indicated. Release the throttle, repeat for other cylinders.
   - Measure bore and stroke using vernier caliper; obtain clearance volume from manufacturer manual.
   - Calculate theoretical CR and actual CR; tabulate the percentage error.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 85$ mm $= 8.5$ cm, $L = 95$ mm $= 9.5$ cm, $V_c = 45$ cm³, $P_{max} = 18$ bar, $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar, $n = 1.35$.

**(i) Theoretical CR (2.5 marks):**

Swept volume:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (8.5)^{2} \cdot 9.5 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 72.25 \cdot 9.5 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 686.375 = 539.04 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

Compression ratio:

$$CR_{th} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{539.04 + 45}{45} = \frac{584.04}{45} \approx 12.98 \approx 13{:}1$$

**(ii) Actual CR from pressure (3 marks):**

Using $CR_{actual} = \left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1/n}$:

$$CR_{actual} = \left(\frac{18}{1.013}\right)^{1/1.35} = (17.77)^{0.7407}$$

Computing:

$$CR_{actual} \approx 8.31$$

**(iii) Engine condition (1.5 marks):**

$$\%\text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{12.98 - 8.31}{12.98} \right\vert \times 100 \approx 36\%$$

This is **far above the acceptable 10% tolerance**, indicating **severe piston ring wear or valve seat leakage**. The engine requires an **overhaul**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the compression ratio of petrol and diesel engines. Explain why the throttling requirement differs during the compression test for these two engines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine has a bore of 90 mm, stroke of 100 mm, and clearance volume of 35 cm³. Calculate:
   (i) The swept volume.
   (ii) The theoretical compression ratio.
   (iii) The expected maximum compression pressure during cranking (assume n = 1.35, P_atm = 1.013 bar).
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Petrol Engine (SI) | Diesel Engine (CI) |
|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio | 8:1 to 12:1 | 14:1 to 22:1 |
| Air-fuel mixture | Premixed (carburettor / port injection) | Air only (fuel injected after compression) |
| Ignition | Spark plug | Compression auto-ignition |
| Throttle during test | **Fully OPEN** required (to admit maximum air) | **Not applicable** — diesel has no throttle |
| Temperature at TDC | ~400 °C | ~700–900 °C |
| Polytropic index used | 1.35 | 1.35 |

**Why throttling differs (3 marks):** A petrol engine regulates power via the throttle valve (which controls the air-fuel mixture quantity entering the cylinder). During the compression test, if the throttle is *closed*, the engine will pump only a small amount of air, leading to a *falsely low* pressure reading. Therefore, the throttle must be **held fully open** during the test. A diesel engine, on the other hand, has **no throttle valve** — power is regulated solely by the quantity of fuel injected per cycle. Hence, the throttling requirement does not apply to a diesel engine during the compression test.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 90$ mm $= 9.0$ cm, $L = 100$ mm $= 10.0$ cm, $V_c = 35$ cm³.

**(i) Swept volume (2 marks):**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (9.0)^{2} \cdot 10.0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 81 \cdot 10 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 810$$

$$V_s = 636.17 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**(ii) Theoretical CR (2.5 marks):**

$$CR_{th} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{636.17 + 35}{35} = \frac{671.17}{35} \approx 19.18 \approx 19.2{:}1$$

This is typical for a **diesel engine**.

**(iii) Maximum expected pressure (2.5 marks):**

$$P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{n} = 1.013 \cdot (19.18)^{1.35}$$

Compute $(19.18)^{1.35}$:

$$(19.18)^{1.35} \approx 50.41$$

Therefore:

$$P_{max} = 1.013 \times 50.41 \approx 51.07 \text{ bar}$$

A healthy diesel engine of CR 19.2:1 should read approximately **51 bar** — significantly higher than a petrol engine, as expected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Compression Ratio (CR)** = $V_1 / V_c = (V_s + V_c) / V_c$ — dimensionless quantity.
- **Petrol CR** ≈ 8:1 to 12:1; **Diesel CR** ≈ 14:1 to 22:1.
- **Diesel needs high CR** because air must reach > 500 °C to auto-ignite diesel fuel.
- **Petrol needs lower CR** to prevent **knocking** (premature auto-ignition).
- **Polytropic relation:** $P_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot (CR)^{n}$ with $n \approx 1.35$ during cranking.
- **Back-calculation:** $CR_{actual} = \left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1/n}$.
- **Isentropic vs Polytropic:** $n = 1.4$ is ideal adiabatic; $n = 1.35$ is real cranking (heat loss to walls).
- **Test conditions:** Throttle FULL OPEN for petrol; spark plugs / injectors removed; crank 5–7 strokes via starter.
- **Healthy engine tolerance:** Observed $P_{max}$ should be within ±10% of expected $P_{max}$.
- **Low pressure causes:** Worn piston rings, leaking valves, blown head gasket, cracked block.
- **Swept volume formula:** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L$.
- **Atmospheric pressure** always taken as **1.013 bar** unless stated otherwise.
- **Always tabulate** Bore, Stroke, $V_c$, $P_{atm}$, $P_{max}$, CR theoretical, CR actual, % error.
- **KTU favourite viva questions:** Difference between isentropic and polytropic; why diesel has no throttle; what happens if rings are worn; what is the auto-ignition temperature of diesel.
- **Modern trend:** CRDI (Common Rail Direct Injection) diesels now achieve CR up to **22:1** with multiple injections per cycle.
- **Safety:** Always wear safety goggles when cranking — starter motor can cause sudden jerks.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**
> **"Diesel Drums, Petrol Pops"** — Diesel has *Double* the CR of Petrol.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disassembling of engine: inspection of engine components, servicing of components, piston ring setting using special tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Disassembling of engine: inspection of engine components, servicing of components, piston ring setting using special tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Engine Disassembly** is the systematic, sequential dismantling of an internal combustion engine into its constituent subassemblies and individual parts using standard hand tools, torque wrenches, and manufacturer-specified special service tools, performed to facilitate inspection, reconditioning, or replacement of worn components. As per **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Module Experiment 2**, the procedure encompasses three critical phases:

1. **Disassembly** – Methodical separation of the engine into serviceable units.
2. **Inspection** – Dimensional, visual, and NDT-based evaluation of components against OEM service limits.
3. **Servicing & Reassembly** – Restoration of components (honing, grinding, replacement) and precise re-installation of assemblies such as piston rings using **ring expanders, ring groove cleaners, and piston ring installation tools**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The experiment is designed to develop psychomotor skills aligned with **Course Outcome CO2** and **CO4** — *"Apply diagnostic and service procedures to vehicle systems using standard workshop tools and interpret component wear patterns."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body Surgery" Intuition

Think of an engine as a **human body** about to undergo major surgery. The surgeon (mechanic) cannot simply rip out the heart (engine) without:

- **Pre-operative prep** (draining fluids, removing accessories, labeling connections)
- **Systematic opening** (removing cylinder head before pistons)
- **Component-level examination** (X-rays, blood tests → dimensional checks, visual inspection)
- **Restorative repair** (stents, grafts → honing, re-ringing, bearing replacement)
- **Reassembly with precision** (sutures with calibrated tension → torquing bolts to spec)

Just as a cardiologist uses **specialized instruments** (forceps, clamps) that a general practitioner does not possess, an engine technician uses **piston ring expanders** and **ring groove cleaners** to install rings without snapping them — tools that ordinary pliers cannot substitute for.

## 1.3 Standard Service Metrics & Industry-Standard Limits

| Parameter | Typical Petrol Engine Value | Typical Diesel Engine Value |
|---|---|---|
| Cylinder bore wear limit | **0.05–0.10 mm** | **0.10–0.15 mm** |
| Piston-to-bore clearance (new) | **0.02–0.05 mm** | **0.05–0.10 mm** |
| Top ring end gap (worn limit) | **0.50–0.80 mm** | **0.60–1.00 mm** |
| Ring groove clearance (max) | **0.10 mm** | **0.15 mm** |
| Piston pin-to-bore clearance | **0.005–0.015 mm** | **0.010–0.020 mm** |
| Connecting rod big-end side play | **0.10–0.35 mm** | **0.15–0.50 mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> The values above are **industry-standard ranges**. For KTU lab examinations, students must always cite the **workshop manual** of the specific engine being serviced (e.g., Maruti 800, Toyota 2ZR-FE, Tata 497 TCIC) since values vary by manufacturer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase-Wise Engine Disassembly Logic

### **Phase A: Pre-Disassembly Preparation**

The engine must be in a state suitable for dismantling. The technician follows a strict sequence:

- **Drain Engine Oil & Coolant:** Place drain pans beneath the sump and radiator petcock. This prevents contamination of the workshop floor and avoids coolant spillage into the oil pan.
- **Remove External Accessories:** Disconnect the battery, remove the air filter, exhaust manifold, intake manifold, alternator, starter motor, and fuel lines. Each accessory is labeled with masking tape to ensure correct reassembly.
- **Mark Timing Reference:** Align timing marks on the camshaft and crankshaft sprockets using a **scriber and chalk**. This is **non-negotiable** for interference engines where valve-piston contact is catastrophic if timing is lost.

### **Phase B: Top-End Disassembly**

The cylinder head assembly is removed first because it blocks access to the pistons:

1. **Rocker Arm / Camshaft Cover Removal:** Loosen bolts in a **reverse-starlike (spiral) pattern** to prevent cover warping.
2. **Camshaft Sprocket Bolts:** Use a **camshaft holding tool** (special tool) to prevent the sprocket from rotating while loosening the central bolt.
3. **Cylinder Head Bolts:** Loosen in the **manufacturer-specified reverse sequence (outside-in)** in three incremental stages (1/3 turn → 1/2 turn → full). This prevents head warping and block distortion.
4. **Pushrod / Valve Removal (OHV engines only):** Label each pushrod position with its corresponding valve number.
5. **Head Gasket Scraping:** Use a **plastic gasket scraper** (never metal) to avoid scoring the aluminum head deck surface.

### **Phase C: Bottom-End Disassembly**

- **Oil Pan Removal:** Tap gently with a rubber mallet if sealed with RTV silicone; never pry with a screwdriver.
- **Oil Pump Pickup Tube:** Inspect the mesh strainer for sludge accumulation.
- **Connecting Rod Caps:** Loosen rod cap bolts evenly. **Mark each rod-cap pair with a numbering punch or marker** (cylinder number + front/back) — mixing them will destroy the bearing journal.
- **Piston Removal:** Push the piston out through the top of the cylinder bore using a **piston ring compressor tool** if the bore is tight, or by hand if rings are sufficiently worn.

## 2.2 Component Inspection Methodology

### 2.2.1 Cylinder Block & Bore Inspection

- **Visual Inspection:** Check for cracks around water jackets, cylinder walls, and main bearing saddles using a **10x magnifier and dye-penetrant kit**.
- **Bore Taper Measurement:** Use an **inside micrometer (bore gauge)** at three levels (top, middle, bottom of ring travel) in two perpendicular directions (X and Y axes). Taper = (Max diameter − Min diameter).
- **Out-of-Roundness:** Difference between X-axis and Y-axis readings at the same height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Honing Pattern:** A healthy bore shows a **cross-hatch pattern of 45°–60°** to retain oil for ring lubrication. A glazed or polished bore indicates overheating and requires re-honing.

### 2.2.2 Piston Inspection

- **Skirt Diameter:** Measured at the **thrust axis**, perpendicular to the piston pin, typically 10–15 mm from the skirt bottom.
- **Ring Groove Wear:** Each groove is checked with a **feeler gauge** to measure clearance between the ring and groove walls. A clearance exceeding **0.10 mm** indicates the piston must be replaced.
- **Piston Pin Bore:** Measured with a **plunger-type internal micrometer**. Excessive clearance leads to a "knock" during cold starts.

### 2.2.3 Crankshaft Inspection

- **Journal Diameter:** Measured with an **outside micrometer** at multiple points along each journal.
- **Out-of-Roundness:** Difference between maximum and minimum readings — must be **< 0.005 mm** for reuse.
- **Taper:** Difference between center and end readings of the same journal.
- **Crank Deflection (Runout):** Measured by placing the crank on V-blocks and reading a **dial indicator** on the center main journal. Runout should be **< 0.05 mm**.

## 2.3 Servicing of Engine Components

| Component | Servicing Operation | Special Tool Used | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder block | Honing (deglazing) | **Cylinder hone (3-stone)** | 45°–60° crosshatch visible |
| Valve seats | Refacing (lapping) | **Valve seat cutter, lapping tool** | Uniform mating band of 1.5–2.0 mm |
| Valve guides | Reaming or knurling | **Valve guide reamer** | Stem-to-guide clearance within spec |
| Crankshaft | Grinding (if undersize) | **Crankshaft grinder** | Polished to RA ≤ 0.2 µm |
| Connecting rod | Resizing the big end | **Rod bolt stretch gauge** | Bore round within 0.005 mm |
| Piston | Decarbonizing | **Ring groove cleaner** | Carbon-free grooves, no burrs |
| Bearings | Inspection (no reuse if peeled) | Visual + color coding | No copper / lead exposure |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Workshop Calculations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **frequently tested** in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) KTU ESE questions. Memorize the **symbol, meaning, and unit** of each.

| # | Formula | Symbol Key | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Taper} = D_{\max} - D_{\min}$ | $D$ = bore diameter | Determines if rebore is required |
| 2 | $\text{Out-of-Round} = D_X - D_Y$ | $D_X, D_Y$ = X and Y axis bore readings | Detects cylinder ovality |
| 3 | $\text{Clearance} = D_{\text{bore}} - D_{\text{piston}}$ | — | Controls piston slap noise |
| 4 | $\text{Ring End Gap (new)} = \frac{\pi D}{n}$ | $D$ = bore dia, $n$ = expansion factor (~25) | Initial factory ring fitting |
| 5 | $\text{Ring Groove Clearance} = W_{\text{groove}} - T_{\text{ring}}$ | $W$ = groove width, $T$ = ring thickness | Detects ring flutter |
| 6 | $\text{Pin Fit} = D_{\text{pin bore}} - d_{\text{pin}}$ | — | Controls wrist-pin knock |
| 7 | $\text{Runout} = \text{TA dial reading}$ | Total Indicator Movement | Crankshaft straightness |
| 8 | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{V_c + V_d}{V_c}$ | $V_c$ = clearance, $V_d$ = displaced | Engine performance indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** All absolute-value expressions in the table above use the symbol $\vert$ explicitly. For instance, the deviation magnitude is written as $\vert D_X - D_Y \vert$, **never** with literal pipe characters that would break the table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The skills developed in this experiment directly support:

- **Automotive Service Workshops** (multi-brand dealerships, independent garages)
- **Engine Reconditioning Plants** (where engines are rebuilt to OEM specs)
- **Diesel Locomotive & Heavy Earthmoving Equipment Maintenance**
- **Aerospace Piston Engine Overhaul** (Rotax, Continental)
- **Two-Wheeler Service Stations** (where ring replacement is the most common top-end job)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Piston Ring Setting Procedure (Using Special Tools)

This is the **highest-mark-yielding** procedure in KTU 2024 Scheme lab viva and ESE questions. The following 12-step procedure is the **official industry standard**.

### **Step 1 — Engine Removal & Mounting**

Mount the engine on a **rotating engine stand** using the bell housing or flywheel mounting holes. Lock the stand in position.

### **Step 2 — Cylinder Head Removal**

Loosen head bolts in **3 stages** of 1/3, 1/2, and full release using a **torque wrench in reverse**. Lift the head straight up — do not tilt, as the pistons may still be near TDC.

### **Step 3 — Piston Assembly Extraction**

- Rotate the crankshaft so that the #1 piston is at **Bottom Dead Center (BDC)**.
- Remove the **connecting rod cap** by loosening the two rod bolts evenly.
- Cover the rod journal with a **plastic cap or vinyl tubing** to prevent nicking the crank journal.
- Push the piston out gently through the top of the bore using a **wooden dowel handle** against the connecting rod.

### **Step 4 — Old Ring Removal**

Use a **piston ring expander tool** (sometimes called a "ring spreader"). The expander is a plastic or metal scissor-like tool that opens the ring uniformly without deforming it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use a screwdriver to remove rings.** This practice is the #1 reason for cracked ring lands in student lab work. The ring expands unevenly and snaps the groove wall.

### **Step 5 — Ring Groove Cleaning**

Insert a **ring groove cleaner tool** (a small triangular scraper matching the ring profile) into each groove. Scrape in a circular motion until all carbon deposits are removed. Follow up with a **groove cleaner wire brush** for the oil control ring's two side rails and expander spring.

### **Step 6 — Ring Groove Clearance Measurement**

For each of the three rings (top, second, oil control):

- Measure groove width with an **outside micrometer** at 4 points around the groove.
- Measure ring thickness with the micrometer.
- Clearance = Groove width − Ring thickness.
- **Acceptance: ≤ 0.10 mm for petrol, ≤ 0.15 mm for diesel.**

### **Step 7 — Piston Ring End Gap Check (Most Tested)**

This is the **mathematical** portion of the experiment. The end gap must be checked **before installation**.

1. Insert the ring into the **bottom of the cylinder bore** (the unworn region), using the piston to push it down so it sits square.
2. Measure the gap with a **feeler gauge**.
3. Compare against manufacturer specification.

**Theoretical derivation of factory end gap:**

$$\text{Factory End Gap} = \pi \cdot D_{\text{bore}} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{25} \text{ to } \frac{1}{20} \right)$$

where the factor $\frac{1}{25}$ for cast iron rings and $\frac{1}{20}$ for chrome rings accounts for **thermal expansion** at operating temperature.

### **Step 8 — Filing the Ring to Spec (If Gap is Too Small)**

If the measured gap is **smaller** than spec (a common occurrence with new aftermarket rings), the ring ends must be filed:

- Clamp the ring in a **ring filing tool** (a hand-operated or bench-mounted abrasive wheel).
- File **one end only**, never both, to maintain the factory-beveled contact edge.
- Recheck the gap and repeat filing in tiny increments (0.05 mm).

> [!WARNING]
> Filing both ends destroys the **ring's circumferential tension uniformity** and leads to a "ring flutter" or "blow-by" condition.

### **Step 9 — Ring Installation on the Piston (Using Special Tools)**

This step uses the **piston ring installer** (also called a ring compressor or ratchet-band installer):

1. Stagger the ring end gaps around the piston at **120°** intervals (top ring at 12 o'clock, second at 4 o'clock, oil control at 8 o'clock). This prevents compressed gas from escaping through aligned gaps.
2. Wrap the **ring compressor band** around the piston, ensuring all three ring grooves are covered.
3. Use the **ratchet mechanism** to tighten the band, compressing the rings flush into their grooves.
4. Lightly lubricate the compressor band and cylinder bore with **SAE 30 oil**.

### **Step 10 — Piston Reinsertion into the Cylinder**

- Hold the connecting rod with one hand; guide the piston into the bore.
- Tap the **piston crown** gently with a **wooden hammer handle** to slide the piston into the bore through the compressor.
- Once the rings have entered the bore, the compressor band can be released and removed.
- Pull the connecting rod down toward the crank journal; ensure the rod cap mating marks align.

### **Step 11 — Connecting Rod Cap Torquing**

- Install the **lower bearing shell** in the cap (ensure the locating tang seats in the slot).
- Hand-tighten the rod cap bolts, then torque in **two stages**:
  - Stage 1: 50% of final torque
  - Stage 2: 100% of final torque
- Final torque for typical passenger car engines: **25–35 Nm + angle (typically 90°)**.

### **Step 12 — Final Verification**

- Rotate the crankshaft **by hand** through 2 full revolutions. Any binding indicates incorrect ring installation, a wrong bearing shell, or a foreign object.
- Measure the **axial end-float** of the connecting rod with a feeler gauge (spec: 0.10–0.35 mm).
- Reinstall the oil pan, head, and accessories in reverse order of removal.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Exam-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder engine has a bore of **80.0 mm**. The piston ring is a cast-iron compression ring with an expansion factor of **1/25**. Calculate:
(a) The factory end gap of the ring.
(b) The new end gap if the engine is rebored to **80.5 mm** (oversize +0.50 mm).
(c) The piston-to-bore clearance for a piston skirt diameter of **79.97 mm** in the original bore.

### **Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$\text{End Gap} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{1}{25}$$

$$\text{End Gap} = \pi \cdot 80.0 \cdot \frac{1}{25} = \frac{251.327}{25} = 10.053 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b):**

$$\text{End Gap}_{\text{new}} = \pi \cdot 80.5 \cdot \frac{1}{25} = \frac{252.894}{25} = 10.116 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c):**

$$\text{Clearance} = D_{\text{bore}} - D_{\text{piston}} = 80.00 - 79.97 = 0.03 \text{ mm}$$

This value is within the **0.02–0.05 mm** range typical for petrol engines — the piston is **serviceable**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Inspect, Service, or Replace?"

| Observation During Inspection | Diagnosis | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Cylinder taper > 0.10 mm | Bore worn oval/tapered | **Rebore + oversize pistons** |
| Cylinder taper < 0.05 mm | Normal wear | Honing + new rings only |
| Ring groove clearance > 0.10 mm | Piston worn | **Replace piston + rings** |
| Crankshaft journal out-of-round > 0.005 mm | Journal worn | **Grind to undersize + new bearings** |
| Bearing shows copper exposure | Fatigue failure | **Replace bearing set** |
| Valve stem-to-guide clearance > 0.10 mm | Guide worn | **Replace guide + ream** |
| Connecting rod big-end bore oval | Rod stretched | **Resize or replace rod** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engine Disassembly & Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engine on Stand] --> B[Drain Oil and Coolant]
    B --> C[Remove External Accessories]
    C --> D[Mark Timing Reference]
    D --> E[Remove Cylinder Head]
    E --> F[Remove Manifolds and Rockers]
    F --> G[Remove Oil Pan]
    G --> H[Remove Oil Pump Assembly]
    H --> I[Mark Connecting Rod Caps]
    I --> J[Push Pistons Out Through Bore]
    J --> K[Remove Crankshaft Main Caps]
    K --> L[Remove Crankshaft from Block]

    L --> M[Component Inspection Station]
    M --> N[Bore Measurement Bore Gauge]
    M --> O[Piston and Ring Groove Check]
    M --> P[Crankshaft Journal Micrometer]
    M --> Q[Valve and Seat Visual Check]
    M --> R[Bearing Color Code Inspection]

    N --> S{Within Spec}
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S

    S -->|Yes| T[Service and Reuse]
    S -->|No| U[Replace or Machine]

    T --> V[Reassembly Phase]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Install Piston Rings with Compressor]
    W --> X[Torque Rod Caps to Spec]
    X --> Y[Reinstall Head with New Gasket]
    Y --> Z[Final Engine Rotation Check]
    Z --> AA[End: Engine Ready for Dyno Test]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piston Ring Installation Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Receive Rebuilt Piston] --> A2[Clean Ring Grooves with Groove Cleaner]
    A2 --> A3[Measure Groove Width Micrometer]
    A3 --> A4{Clearance OK}
    A4 -->|No| A5[Reject Piston and Replace]
    A4 -->|Yes| A6[Insert Ring in Bore Bottom]
    A6 --> A7[Check End Gap Feeler Gauge]
    A7 --> A8{Gap OK}
    A8 -->|No| A9[File Ring in Ring Filer]
    A9 --> A7
    A8 -->|Yes| A10[Install Ring with Ring Expander]
    A10 --> A11[Stagger Gaps at 120 Degrees]
    A11 --> A12[Apply Piston Ring Compressor]
    A12 --> A13[Insert Piston into Cylinder]
    A13 --> A14[Release Compressor and Verify]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Special Tools Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ST1[Special Tools for Piston Rings]
        T1[Ring Expander Pliers]
        T2[Ring Groove Cleaner Triangle]
        T3[Ring Groove Wire Brush]
        T4[Ring Filer Abrasive Wheel]
        T5[Ring Compressor Band]
        T6[Piston Ring Installer Ratchet]
    end

    subgraph ST2[Special Tools for Engine Service]
        T7[Camshaft Holding Tool]
        T8[Head Bolt Torque Wrench]
        T9[Rod Bolt Stretch Gauge]
        T10[Crankshaft Turning Tool]
        T11[Engine Stand Rotating Cradle]
        T12[Valve Spring Compressor]
    end

    subgraph ST3[Measurement Instruments]
        T13[Inside Micrometer Bore Gauge]
        T14[Outside Micrometer 0 to 25]
        T15[Outside Micrometer 25 to 50]
        T16[Dial Indicator with Magnetic Base]
        T17[Feeler Gauge Set]
        T18[Plastigage Bearing Clearance Tool]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Ring Gap Staggering Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Piston Crown Top View] --> R1[Top Compression Ring Gap at 12 O Clock]
    P --> R2[Second Compression Ring Gap at 4 O Clock]
    P --> R3[Oil Control Ring Expander Gap at 8 O Clock]
    P --> R4[Oil Control Side Rails Opposite to Each Other]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `R1`, `T7`) and contain no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `style`. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)
