### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define piston ring end gap. State the typical value for a 4-cylinder petrol engine of bore 78 mm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Piston ring end gap is the small clearance left between the two ends of a piston ring when it is fitted inside the cylinder bore. This gap accommodates the thermal expansion of the ring when the engine reaches operating temperature, preventing the ring ends from butting against each other and causing ring breakage or cylinder scoring.
- **Typical value (1 mark):** For a 78 mm bore petrol engine, the end gap is approximately **0.30 to 0.45 mm** (calculated as $\pi \cdot 78 \cdot \frac{1}{25} \approx 9.8 \text{ mm} / 25 \approx 0.39 \text{ mm}$). The actual factory spec must be verified from the workshop manual.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any three special tools used during piston ring installation and state their function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Piston Ring Expander:** Used to safely remove and install piston rings onto the piston without overstressing or snapping the ring material.
2. **Ring Groove Cleaner:** A triangular scraper that removes carbon deposits from piston ring grooves to ensure proper ring seating and gas sealing.
3. **Piston Ring Compressor (Ring Band):** Wraps around the piston to compress the rings flush into their grooves, allowing the piston to enter the cylinder bore without the rings catching on the bore edge.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the **ring expander** (for installation/removal) with the **ring compressor** (for bore insertion). Marks will be deducted if these are used interchangeably in the answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students answer **one full question** (with sub-parts) from a choice of two. Both choices are presented below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **step-by-step procedure for disassembling a 4-stroke multi-cylinder petrol engine** mounted on a workshop stand. Highlight the safety precautions and the use of special tools. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **method of inspecting a cylinder bore** for taper, out-of-roundness, and surface finish. Mention the instruments used and the service limits. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]:**

**1. Pre-Disassembly (1 mark):**
- Place engine on a **rotating engine stand**, lock in upright position.
- Drain engine oil through sump plug, drain coolant through radiator petcock.
- Disconnect battery, fuel lines, and external linkages.

**2. External Accessory Removal (1 mark):**
- Remove air cleaner, exhaust manifold, alternator, starter motor, distributor/ignition coils.
- Label every disconnected wire and hose with masking tape.

**3. Timing Reference Marking (1 mark):**
- Rotate crankshaft to align **camshaft and crankshaft timing marks** at TDC of #1 cylinder.
- Use a **camshaft holding tool** to fix the cam sprocket during bolt removal.

**4. Cylinder Head Removal (1 mark):**
- Loosen head bolts in **3 incremental stages** using a calibrated torque wrench in reverse.
- Lift head straight up; do not pry sideways.

**5. Oil Pan & Pump Removal (1 mark):**
- Tap with rubber mallet; never pry with metal tools to avoid pan warping.

**6. Piston and Rod Assembly Removal (1 mark):**
- Mark each connecting rod cap with cylinder number and front/back orientation.
- Cover rod journal with vinyl cap; push piston out through the top of the bore.

**7. Crankshaft Removal (1 mark):**
- Loosen main bearing caps in correct sequence; lift crank with both hands to avoid journal damage.
- Send crank for inspection or grinding if required.

**Safety Precautions** include: wearing safety goggles, draining fluids responsibly, using correct tools (no improvised tools), and organizing all fasteners in labeled trays.

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]:**

**1. Purpose of Bore Inspection (1 mark):**
The cylinder bore must be inspected for **taper**, **out-of-roundness (ovality)**, and **surface finish** to determine if reboring, honing, or simple deglazing is required.

**2. Instruments Used (2 marks):**
- **Inside Micrometer (Bore Gauge):** For diameter measurement.
- **Cylinder Bore Gauge with Dial Indicator:** For high-precision readings.
- **Surface Finish Comparator (RA Blocks):** For visual surface texture assessment.
- **Cylinder Hone (3-Stone):** For corrective surface finishing.

**3. Measurement Procedure (2 marks):**
- Measure bore at **three heights**: top (just below ring travel at TDC), middle, and bottom (just above ring travel at BDC).
- At each height, take readings in **two perpendicular directions** (X-axis = thrust axis, Y-axis = perpendicular).
- Taper = Max diameter − Min diameter across all heights.
- Out-of-round = $\vert D_X - D_Y \vert$ at the same height.

**4. Service Limits & Action (2 marks):**
- **Taper < 0.05 mm** → Hone and install new rings.
- **Taper 0.05–0.10 mm** → Rebore to +0.25 or +0.50 mm oversize, install oversize pistons.
- **Taper > 0.10 mm** → Mandatory rebore or block replacement.
- **Surface finish** should exhibit a **45°–60° crosshatch** pattern from honing; any glazing (mirror finish) requires immediate re-honing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe with neat sketches the **procedure for installing piston rings** on a piston using the special tools — ring expander, ring groove cleaner, and ring compressor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-cylinder diesel engine has a cylinder bore of **84.0 mm**. Calculate:
  (i) The factory end gap of the top compression ring.
  (ii) The clearance volume change if the cylinder is rebored by **+0.75 mm**.
  (iii) State two precautions observed during piston ring filing. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]:**

**1. Pre-Installation Cleaning (1 mark):**
- Wash piston in solvent; dry thoroughly.
- Use a **ring groove cleaner** (triangular scraper) to remove all carbon from each of the three grooves. Follow with a **wire brush** for the oil ring expander groove.

**2. Ring Groove Clearance Check (1 mark):**
- Measure each groove's width and each ring's thickness with an **outside micrometer**.
- Compute clearance = Groove − Ring thickness. **Acceptance: ≤ 0.10 mm petrol, ≤ 0.15 mm diesel.**

**3. End Gap Check (1 mark):**
- Insert each ring into the **bottom of the cylinder bore** (unworn area) using the piston to push it square.
- Measure gap with a **feeler gauge**. Compare to manual spec.

**4. Filing the Ring (1 mark):**
- If gap is too small, mount the ring in a **ring filer** and file **one end only** in small increments.
- Recheck and repeat until spec is achieved.

**5. Ring Installation on Piston (1 mark):**
- Use a **ring expander** to spread the ring and slide it over the piston crown into the correct groove.
- **Stagger the gaps at 120° intervals** to prevent gas leakage through aligned gaps.

**6. Piston Insertion into Bore (1 mark):**
- Wrap the **ring compressor band** around the piston, tighten via the ratchet until rings are flush.
- Lubricate the bore with SAE 30 oil.
- Insert piston into the bore, tap the crown gently with a wooden handle, then release the band once rings enter the bore.

**7. Final Verification (1 mark):**
- Rotate the crankshaft 2 full turns by hand. Any binding signals a fault.

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Factory End Gap Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$\text{End Gap} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{1}{25}$$

$$\text{End Gap} = \pi \cdot 84.0 \cdot \frac{1}{25}$$

$$\text{End Gap} = \frac{263.894}{25} = 10.556 \text{ mm}$$

The factory end gap is **approximately 10.55 mm**.

**(ii) New Bore Diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_{\text{new}} = 84.0 + 0.75 = 84.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Clearance Volume Change (approximate):**

$$\Delta V_c \approx \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_{\text{new}}^2 - D_{\text{old}}^2) \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the stroke length. If $L = 80 \text{ mm}$ (assumed for typical diesel):

$$\Delta V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (84.75^2 - 84.0^2) \cdot 80$$

$$\Delta V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (7182.56 - 7056.00) \cdot 80$$

$$\Delta V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 126.56 \cdot 80$$

$$\Delta V_c \approx 7949 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 7.95 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ increase in clearance volume per cylinder}$$

**(iii) Two Precautions During Ring Filing (2 marks):**

1. **File only one end** of the ring. Filing both ends destroys the factory-controlled tension and bevel geometry, causing ring flutter and blow-by.
2. **File in small increments (0.05 mm)** and re-measure the gap with a feeler gauge after each pass. Over-filing makes the gap too wide, allowing combustion gases to escape past the ring and reducing compression.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and cm in the same calculation (1 mark deduction).
> 2. **Forgetting the Pi Factor:** Writing "End Gap = D / 25" without $\pi$ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not Mentioning Stagger Angle:** Stating ring installation without specifying the **120° stagger** loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing Up Tools:** Calling the ring compressor a "ring expander" or vice versa loses 1 mark in tool identification questions.
> 5. **Skipping the Bore Cleanliness Note:** Failing to mention the cylinder bore must be **cleaned and oiled** before piston insertion is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Always drain fluids** before engine disassembly begins.
- [x] **Label every part** (rod caps, bearing shells, pushrods) for correct reassembly.
- [x] **Use a ring expander** to install/remove rings — **never a screwdriver**.
- [x] **Use a ring groove cleaner** to remove carbon from piston grooves.
- [x] **Stagger ring end gaps at 120°** to prevent gas leakage.
- [x] **Check ring end gap** in the unworn bottom of the bore using a feeler gauge.
- [x] **File only one end** of the ring when the gap is too tight.
- [x] **Use a ring compressor band** when inserting the piston into the bore.
- [x] **Loosen head bolts in 3 stages** in the manufacturer-specified reverse sequence.
- [x] **Torque rod cap bolts in 2 stages** — 50% then 100% of final torque.
- [x] **Measure cylinder bore** at 3 heights × 2 axes (6 readings per cylinder).
- [x] **Crosshatch pattern** from honing should be **45°–60°**.
- [x] **Cylinder taper > 0.10 mm** → rebore mandatory.
- [x] **Ring groove clearance > 0.10 mm** → replace piston.
- [x] **Crankshaft runout** should be measured on V-blocks with a dial indicator — limit **< 0.05 mm**.
- [x] The factory end gap formula is $\pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{1}{25}$ for cast iron rings.
- [x] **Always rotate the crank 2 full turns** after reassembly to verify free rotation.
- [x] Special tools are **non-negotiable** in the KTU lab — improvised tools (screwdrivers, pliers) will be marked down by the examiner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For the 14-mark questions, structure your answer in **numbered points with sub-headings**. Examiners allocate marks per point, not per paragraph. Use a **labeled sketch or block diagram** for any procedure-based question — it guarantees 1–2 extra marks for visual presentation, even if your written explanation is brief.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine valve reconditioning: valve grinding by using valve re-facing machine, valve seat grinding, lapping and valve angle cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Engine valve reconditioning: valve grinding by using valve re-facing machine, valve seat grinding, lapping and valve angle cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> **Engine Valve Reconditioning** is a systematic process of restoring the worn-out intake and exhaust valves, valve seats, and related contact surfaces of an internal combustion (IC) engine cylinder head to their original functional geometry. The process encompasses four primary machining operations: **(i) Valve Face Grinding (Re-facing)**, **(ii) Valve Seat Grinding/Cutting**, **(iii) Valve Lapping (Hand or Machine)**, and **(iv) Valve Angle Verification & Cutting**. Together, these operations re-establish the precise **sealing contact band (valve seat width)** required for efficient combustion chamber integrity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The PCAUL308 lab manual specifies that students must *physically perform* the operations using the **valve refacing machine (Kwik-Way Sunnen-type)** and the **valve seat cutting/grinding machine (Neway-type or Sioux-type)**. Recording observations such as initial run-out, final run-out, seat width, and contact pattern is **mandatory** for viva-voce and record evaluation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an engine valve like the **rubber stopper on a water bottle**. If the stopper's bottom surface becomes scratched, warped, or coated with mineral deposits, water leaks out. You cannot fix it by simply pressing harder — you must:
1. **Resurface the stopper** (grind off the scratches → *valve refacing*).
2. **Reshape the bottle's mouth** if it has been dented (*valve seat cutting*).
3. **Polish both surfaces together with a fine paste** so they mate perfectly (*lapping*).
4. **Verify the angle of contact** with a protractor so the fit is geometrically perfect (*angle verification*).

In an engine, this perfect mating — typically a **45° angle band** of width between **1.0 mm and 1.5 mm** for petrol engines and **1.5 mm to 2.5 mm** for diesel engines — is the difference between a healthy engine producing **full compression (12:1 to 18:1)** and a "tired" engine that burns oil and loses power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Standard Metrics Used:**
> - Standard Intake Valve Face Angle = **45°**
> - Standard Exhaust Valve Face Angle = **45°** (some heavy-duty = **30°**)
> - Valve Seat Three-Angle Cut: **15° (Top correction) → 45° (Seat) → 75° (Throat relief)**
> - Acceptable Valve Stem-to-Guide Clearance (Petrol): **0.02 mm – 0.06 mm**
> - Acceptable Run-out After Refacing: **≤ 0.03 mm** (TIR)
> - Acceptable Seat Width: **1.0 mm – 2.5 mm** (application dependent)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Valve Reconditioning Required?

During normal engine operation, the following wear mechanisms degrade the valve and seat:

| Wear Mechanism | Cause | Visible Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Fatigue / Burning** | High exhaust temps (700 °C–900 °C) | Pitting, cracks on valve face |
| **Carbon Deposits** | Incomplete combustion residues | Black coating, hot spots |
| **Impact Load Recession** | Repeated valve closing shock | Seat width increases over time |
| **Corrosion** | Lead from petrol, sulphur from diesel | Surface pitting, wash-out |
| **Mechanical Erosion** | High-velocity hot gases | Gutter formation (deep narrow grooves) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Practice:** Valves are usually refaced when the **valve margin thickness** falls below **0.5 mm–0.8 mm**, beyond which the valve head overheats and fails catastrophically (valve burn-through).

---

### 2.2 The Four Sub-Operations — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **(A) Valve Refacing (Grinding on Valve Refacing Machine)**

1. **Inspection First:** Measure valve stem diameter (micrometer), check stem straightness (V-block + dial indicator), measure valve head margin.
2. **Mounting:** Clamp the valve in the **chuck of the valve refacing machine** (e.g., Kwik-Way, Tobin-Arp, or Berco).
3. **Wheel Selection:** Use a **resin-bonded grinding wheel** of grit **60 (roughing)** followed by **80–100 (finishing)**. The wheel is dressed (true-cut) to the exact **45° (or 30°) angle**.
4. **Grinding:** Valve rotates automatically. Light, repeated traverses of the grinding wheel across the valve face. Coolant (water-soluble cutting fluid) is applied.
5. **Dressing Between Passes:** The grinding wheel is dressed with a **diamond dresser** to remove glaze and maintain sharpness.
6. **Stop Criteria:** Stop when the entire worn band has been removed, leaving a **continuous, clean, concentric grinding pattern** with no pits.
7. **Final Measurement:** Use a **DTI (Dial Test Indicator)** on a **concentricity gauge** to measure **Total Indicated Reading (TIR)**. The valve should run out **≤ 0.03 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Refacing:** Re-establishes a true geometric seating face. Without a flat/conical true face, sealing is impossible regardless of seat condition.

#### **(B) Valve Seat Cutting / Grinding**

The valve seat is cut using a **Neway cutter** (manual, three-angle system) or a **Sioux-type piloted grinding stone** (motorized, with a pilot matching the valve guide bore).

**The Three-Angle Principle:**

- **Top Angle (15° Correction / Upper Cut):** Creates a small **45° – 15° = 30° effective angle** at the top of the seat. This is the **transition (top correction) angle**. It reduces valve rotation tendency and improves airflow.
- **Seat Angle (45°):** The primary sealing surface.
- **Throat Angle (75° Relief / Bottom Cut):** Creates a **45° – 75° = –30° effective** undercut at the bottom, providing a sharp **scrape edge** and **clearance for hot gases** to flow unobstructed.

> **Rule of Thumb:** Use **15° stone first** to lower the seat, then **45° stone** to set seat width, then **75° stone** to lower the seat width and bring it down to the cylinder head face.

#### **(C) Valve Lapping**

Lapping is the **final precision fitting process** using **valve grinding compound** (a mix of **silicon carbide or aluminum oxide grit** with grease as the binder).

**Procedure:**
1. Apply a thin layer of **coarse compound (100–180 grit)** evenly on the valve face.
2. Insert the valve into its guide, attach a **lapping stick / suction tool**.
3. Rotate the valve back-and-forth with light downward pressure, lifting it occasionally to redistribute compound.
4. Wipe, inspect, repeat with **fine compound (280–400 grit)**.
5. Final lap with **mirror-finish compound (600+ grit)** until a **continuous matt-grey contact band** appears on both the valve face and the seat.

> **The "Why":** Lapping removes microscopic high spots left by refacing/seat cutting. A perfectly lapped valve shows a **uniform, unbroken, dull grey ring** with no bright interruptions.

#### **(D) Valve Angle Verification**

- **Valve Face Angle** is verified with a **bevel protractor / universal bevel protractor** placed on the refaced face.
- **Seat Angle** is verified with a **seat angle gauge (Neway gauge)** inserted into the seat.
- Both should match the design angle (**typically 45° ± 0.5°**).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Data Sheet (Exam-Critical Table)

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Application / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Valve Stem Run-out (TIR) | $\text{TIR} = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | mm (should be ≤ 0.03 mm) |
| Valve Margin Thickness | $t_m = t_{original} - \Delta t_{ground}$ | mm (limit ≥ 0.5–0.8 mm) |
| Seat Width | Measured with **seat width gauge** | mm (1.0–2.5 mm) |
| Effective Contact Band Position | Position = $\dfrac{D_{outer} - D_{inner}}{2}$ from center | mm from seat top |
| Contact Band Centering | Outer − Inner must be **symmetric** | Check with **Prussian Blue** test |
| Bevel Angle Verification | Read off **bevel protractor** | degrees (°), tolerance ± 0.5° |
| Stem-to-Guide Clearance | $C = D_{bore} - d_{stem}$ | mm (0.02–0.08 mm) |
| Three-Angle Cut Geometry | $15° / 45° / 75°$ | All ± 1° |
| Cylindricity (Conicity) of Stem | $\Delta D = D_{top} - D_{mid}$ | mm (≤ 0.005 mm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER** use the `|` character inside table cells — the markdown table will break. Always use $\vert$ or \mid for absolute values.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Workshops & Service Stations:** A "top-end overhaul" without valve reconditioning is incomplete. In diesel trucks, valve and seat reconditioning is done every **120,000–150,000 km**.
- **Emission Compliance:** A poorly sealed valve reduces compression → unburnt hydrocarbons (HC) and carbon monoxide (CO) rise → vehicle fails **BS-VI / Euro 6** emission tests.
- **Fuel Efficiency:** Properly seated valves restore volumetric efficiency (ηv) close to design value (~**85–95%**), saving **3–7% fuel**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Three-Angle Seat Cut Geometry

Consider the valve seat as a frustum (truncated cone). The seat lies between the combustion chamber (top) and the port (bottom). The total contact band width is $W$, and we wish to **reposition** it centrally.

Let the original seat have a single angle $\alpha_{seat} = 45°$.

When we use the **15° top correction stone**, the effective angle at the top of the seat becomes:

$$
\alpha_{top,eff} = \alpha_{seat} - \alpha_{top-stone} = 45° - 15° = 30°
$$

When we use the **75° throat relief stone**, the effective angle at the bottom becomes:

$$
\alpha_{bottom,eff} = \alpha_{bottom-stone} - \alpha_{seat} = 75° - 45° = 30° \text{ (relief)}
$$

> The two outer angles **narrow the seat width** and **centralize the contact band**. The 15° stone cuts the upper edge *inward* (lowers the seat top), and the 75° stone cuts the lower edge *outward* (raises the seat bottom) — both reducing $W$.

**Working Relationship:**

$$
W_{final} = W_{initial} - \Delta W_{15°} - \Delta W_{75°}
$$

$$
\text{Seat Position} \approx \frac{D_{bore} - D_{port}}{2} \pm \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the centering error (should be ≤ **0.5 mm** offset for proper heat transfer).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Correction

| # | Observed Defect | Probable Cause | Corrective Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bright spot only at top of seat** | Seat is too high (toward chamber) | Use **75° stone** to lower seat width |
| 2 | **Bright spot only at bottom of seat** | Seat is too low (toward port) | Use **15° stone** to raise seat width |
| 3 | **Seat width too wide (>2.5 mm)** | Insufficient use of 15°/75° | Apply both 15° and 75° stones to narrow |
| 4 | **Seat width too narrow (<1.0 mm)** | Over-correction with 15°/75° | Use **45° stone** alone to widen |
| 5 | **Valve margin < 0.5 mm** | Excessive grinding on face | **Reject valve**, replace |
| 6 | **Black streaks across contact band** | Compound contamination / oil | **Re-lap with clean compound** |
| 7 | **Concentricity TIR > 0.03 mm** | Valve stem bent or worn guide | **Replace guide**, true stem on lathe |
| 8 | **Pit marks not removed after refacing** | Grit too coarse / insufficient passes | Re-grind with **finer wheel + coolant** |
| 9 | **Edge of valve face chipped** | Burr from chuck jaws or careless dressing | **Dress wheel** carefully; use soft jaws |
| 10 | **Seating pattern not circular** | Valve stem/guide wear | **Replace guide**, then re-lap |

---

### 3.3 Sample Numerical — Run-out Calculation

A student measures the following readings on a refaced valve using a DTI on a concentricity fixture, rotating the valve 360°:

| Position (°) | DTI Reading (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 |
| 90 | 0.018 |
| 180 | 0.025 |
| 270 | 0.010 |

**Step 1: Identify Max and Min readings**

$$
R_{max} = 0.025 \text{ mm (at 180°)}
$$

$$
R_{min} = 0.000 \text{ mm (at 0°)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute TIR**

$$
\text{TIR} = R_{max} - R_{min} = 0.025 - 0.000 = 0.025 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with Limit**

$$
\text{TIR}_{measured} = 0.025 \text{ mm} < \text{TIR}_{limit} = 0.030 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The valve is **acceptable**. The refacing is geometrically true and the valve will seat concentrically.

> If TIR had been, say, **0.045 mm**, the valve would be **rejected** — likely due to a bent stem, worn guide, or improper chuck mounting (3-jaw self-centering chuck could not hold the valve true on its stem axis).

---

### 3.4 Sample Numerical — Valve Margin Calculation

A new exhaust valve has a margin thickness $t_{original} = 2.0$ mm. After refacing, the margin measures $t_{final} = 1.6$ mm. The KTU manual specification requires minimum margin $\geq 0.8$ mm for exhaust valves.

**Step 1: Compute material removed**

$$
\Delta t = t_{original} - t_{final} = 2.0 - 1.6 = 0.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Check against limit**

$$
t_{final} = 1.6 \text{ mm} > t_{min} = 0.8 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Valve is **reusable**. But a record must be kept — if a second refacing were attempted, the margin would drop to 1.2 mm, still safe but with reduced margin for any future operation.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Valve Re-Facing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Jaw Self-Centering Chuck] --> B[Valve Stem Clamping Zone]
    B --> C[Rotation Motor 0.75 to 1.5 HP 2800 RPM]
    C --> D[Drive Spindle with Live Center Support]
    D --> E[Valve Head Held at Working Height]
    E --> F[Grinding Wheel 45 deg Resin Bonded 60 to 100 Grit]
    F --> G[Diamond Dresser Tool Rest]
    G --> H[Coolant Nozzle and Splash Guard]
    H --> I[Work Rest with Angle Gauge]
    I --> J[Operator Hand Wheel for Traversing]
    J --> K[Finished Valve with True 45 deg Face]

    L[Cabinet Base] --> A
    L --> C
    L --> F
    L --> I

    M[Grinding Wheel Dresser Diamond Tip] --> F
    N[Coolant Pump Reservoir] --> H

    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,color:#000000
    style F fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF
    style K fill:#90EE90,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The valve is gripped by its stem in a 3-jaw chuck, supported at the head by a live center, and rotated. The grinding wheel is mounted on a slide that can be traversed horizontally and angled. The diamond dresser is used between passes to keep the wheel sharp and geometrically true.

---

### 4.2 Three-Angle Valve Seat Cut — Cross-Sectional Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion Chamber Side Top] --> B[15 deg Top Correction Stone]
    B --> C[45 deg Primary Seating Band]
    C --> D[75 deg Throat Relief Stone]
    D --> E[Port Side Bottom]

    F[Valve Face 45 deg] --> C
    G[Contact Band Width 1.0 to 2.5 mm] --> C

    style A fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000000
    style B fill:#FFA07A,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFD700,color:#000000
    style D fill:#87CEFA,color:#000000
    style E fill:#90EE90,color:#000000
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000000
    style G fill:#FFFFE0,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The 15° stone is applied first to "lower" the top of the seat. The 45° stone establishes the primary sealing band. The 75° stone is applied last to "raise" the bottom of the seat. The combination narrows and centralizes the contact band.

---

### 4.3 Process Flow of Complete Valve Reconditioning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Cylinder Head] --> B[Disassemble and Identify Valves]
    B --> C[Visual and Dimensional Inspection]
    C --> D{Valve OK to Reuse?}
    D -- No --> E[Replace with New Valve]
    D -- Yes --> F[Mount in Valve Refacing Machine]
    F --> G[Grind Valve Face with 60 Grit]
    G --> H[Dress Wheel]
    H --> I[Finish Grind with 100 Grit]
    I --> J[Measure TIR and Margin]
    J --> K{Within Limits?}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L[Mount in Valve Seat Cutting Machine]
    L --> M[Cut with 15 deg Stone]
    M --> N[Cut with 45 deg Stone]
    N --> O[Cut with 75 deg Stone]
    O --> P[Check Seat Width and Centering]
    P --> Q{Seat OK?}
    Q -- No --> M
    Q -- Yes --> R[Apply Coarse Lapping Compound]
    R --> S[Lap by Hand or Machine]
    S --> T[Inspect Prussian Blue Test]
    T --> U{Continuous Contact Band?}
    U -- No --> S
    U -- Yes --> V[Final Lapping with Fine Compound]
    V --> W[Clean and Reassemble]

    style A fill:#87CEEB,color:#000000
    style D fill:#FFD700,color:#000000
    style J fill:#FFA07A,color:#000000
    style P fill:#FFA07A,color:#000000
    style T fill:#90EE90,color:#000000
    style W fill:#90EE90,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The process is iterative — measurement and inspection gates control whether the operation must be repeated. This is the classic **Closed-Loop Quality Control** flow used in KTU-evaluated lab experiments.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List the four major operations performed during engine valve reconditioning. State the purpose of each in one line.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Valve Refacing** | Re-establishes true geometric face on worn valve |
| 2 | **Valve Seat Cutting** | Re-establishes true 45° seat with correct width and centering |
| 3 | **Lapping** | Achieieves perfect mating between valve face and seat via fine abrasive |
| 4 | **Angle Verification** | Confirms both face and seat angles match design (typically 45°) |

> **Marking Note (1 mark for correct listing, 2 marks for correct purpose statements).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a "three-angle" valve seat cut? Name the three angles used and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A three-angle valve seat cut uses three separate grinding/cutting stones at three different angles to **shape, narrow, and centralize** the seating band.

- **15° (Top Correction):** Lowers the top of the seat → narrows the contact band from above.
- **45° (Primary Seating):** Establishes the main contact band — the actual sealing surface.
- **75° (Throat Relief):** Raises the bottom of the seat → narrows contact band from below, leaving a sharp lower edge to scrape deposits.

> **One mark each for naming the angle and stating the role; 1 mark for the introductory definition.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe with the aid of a labeled sketch the construction and working of a **valve refacing machine**. List the safety precautions to be observed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the procedure of **valve seat cutting using a three-angle (Neway-type) cutter**. Show the role of the 15°, 45°, and 75° stones with a diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Bed/Cabinet:** Heavy cast-iron base absorbs vibration.
- **Three-jaw self-centering chuck:** Holds the valve by its stem; can be re-centered for tapered stems.
- **Rotation motor:** 0.75–1.5 HP, ~2800 RPM, with V-belt drive.
- **Spindle and live center:** Supports valve head, ensuring concentricity.
- **Grinding wheel head:** Swivels to 30° or 45° as required; carries the grinding wheel on a spindle.
- **Diamond dresser:** Mounted on a tool rest; used between passes to true the wheel.
- **Coolant system:** Tray + nozzle; flow of water-soluble coolant to the contact zone.
- **Work-rest and traverse handwheel:** Operator-controlled horizontal feed.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Valve is mounted in chuck; live center supports head; chuck rotates valve.
2. Wheel head is set to 45° (or 30° for heavy-duty exhaust).
3. Wheel is brought into contact with valve face; light pressure applied.
4. Hand-wheel traverses the wheel back and forth across the face.
5. Coolant flows continuously.
6. After rough pass, wheel is dressed; finishing pass with finer grit is done.
7. Valve is removed; TIR is measured on a concentricity gauge.

**Safety Precautions (1 mark — list any 4):**
- Wear **safety goggles** (wheel fragments can fly off).
- Ensure **wheel guards** are in place.
- Do not exceed the **rated RPM** of the grinding wheel.
- Do not grind with a **damaged or cracked wheel**.
- Keep hands clear of the rotating wheel and valve.
- Use proper **coolant flow** to prevent burns and dust inhalation.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Pilot Selection:** Choose a pilot diameter that matches the valve guide bore exactly. Insert the pilot into the guide; the cutter stones rotate around the pilot, ensuring concentricity.
2. **15° Stone (Top Correction):** Apply with light pressure. Its role is to **lower the upper edge** of the seat, narrowing the seat width from the top.
3. **45° Stone (Primary Seat):** Apply this next. It establishes the **main 45° sealing band**. The number of turns controls the seat width.
4. **75° Stone (Throat Relief):** Apply this last. It **raises the lower edge** of the seat, narrowing the seat width from the bottom and producing a sharp lower edge.
5. **Check Seat Width:** Use a seat width gauge. Target **1.0–2.5 mm** depending on application.
6. **Check Centering:** Apply **Prussian Blue** to the valve face; lightly insert and rotate; the transferred pattern should be **circular and centered**.

**Diagram (sketch described — 2 marks):**
A cross-section showing the cylinder head seat with three distinct angles labeled **15° at the top, 45° in the middle, 75° at the bottom**, with the **valve face (45°)** in contact with the **45° seat band**, and the contact band width labeled between the two edges.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is **valve lapping**? Explain the hand lapping procedure step-by-step. What is the **Prussian Blue Test** and why is it used? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A refaced exhaust valve has original margin $t_{orig} = 2.5$ mm. After refacing, the margin is $t_{final} = 1.5$ mm. The specified minimum margin is $0.8$ mm. Compute the material removed and state whether the valve is reusable. Also state what **Total Indicated Reading (TIR)** is and what value is acceptable. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Valve lapping is the final precision-fitting process where a **fine abrasive compound** is used between the valve face and the seat to remove microscopic high spots, achieving a **leak-proof gas-tight seal**.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Clean valve and seat thoroughly; remove all oil and old compound.
2. Apply a thin, even layer of **coarse lapping compound (100–180 grit silicon carbide in grease)** on the valve face.
3. Insert valve into its guide; attach the **lapping stick (suction-cup or split-lath type)** to the valve head.
4. Rotate the valve back-and-forth (~30° each side) with **light downward pressure**, lifting it occasionally to redistribute compound.
5. After ~2 minutes, remove, wipe, and **inspect the contact pattern** (a grey band should appear).
6. Repeat with **fine compound (280–400 grit)**.
7. Final polish with **mirror compound (600+ grit)** until a **uniform dull grey band** is visible on both valve and seat.
8. Wash off all compound with kerosene/solvent.

**Prussian Blue Test (2 marks):**
- **Purpose:** To check the **location, width, and centering** of the contact band.
- **Procedure:** Apply a thin film of **Prussian Blue (engineer's blue)** on the valve face; insert valve into seat; apply light pressure and rotate once; remove and inspect.
- **Reading:** A **uniform, continuous, circular blue band** indicates perfect contact. **Breaks** indicate high spots (re-lap). **Off-center** band indicates misalignment (re-cut the seat).

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Material Removed:**

$$
\Delta t = t_{orig} - t_{final} = 2.5 \text{ mm} - 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Check Minimum Margin:**

$$
t_{final} = 1.5 \text{ mm} > t_{min} = 0.8 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow \text{ SAFE to reuse.}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The valve has lost 1.0 mm of margin. It is still safely above the 0.8 mm limit and may be reused. However, a record must be maintained in the lab notebook. A **second refacing** would reduce margin to ~0.5 mm → unsafe → **valve to be replaced**.

**Step 4 — TIR Definition (2 marks):**

**Total Indicated Reading (TIR)** is the **difference between the maximum and minimum readings** of a Dial Test Indicator (DTI) as the valve is rotated 360° on a concentricity fixture (e.g., between centers or in a V-block with stem reference).

$$
\text{TIR} = R_{max} - R_{min}
$$

- **Acceptable limit for a refaced valve:** $\text{TIR} \leq 0.03$ **mm** (per KTU lab manual).
- **Higher TIR** indicates: bent valve stem, worn valve guide, or improper chuck mounting.
- **Practical significance:** High TIR → valve seats eccentrically → uneven contact band → localized hot spots → premature valve burn-out.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse refacing with lapping.** Refacing is *machine grinding*; lapping is *hand/machine polishing with compound*. Marks are deducted if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Always mention all three angles** (15°/45°/75°) in a seat-cutting question. Skipping even one angle is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Numerical answers must show units** (mm, °). A correct number without a unit loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Prussian Blue Test** is often misnamed as "Pressure Test" or "Penetrant Test" — these are different NDT methods. Use the correct term.
> 5. **TIR ≠ Run-out in some textbooks.** In KTU evaluation, "run-out" generally means TIR unless otherwise specified. State the formula clearly.
> 6. **Always wear PPE (goggles, gloves, apron)** in lab viva — students lose marks for omitting safety.
> 7. **Distinguish pilot diameters** for different engines. Universal pilots do not exist; mention pilot selection explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Valve reconditioning = **Refacing + Seat Cutting + Lapping + Angle Verification**.
- ✅ Refacing machine chuck **holds the stem**; **live center supports the head**.
- ✅ Grinding wheel is **resin-bonded**, angles 45°/30°, grits 60/80/100.
- ✅ Coolant **must be used** during grinding (prevents thermal cracks).
- ✅ **TIR ≤ 0.03 mm** is the geometric acceptance criterion for refacing.
- ✅ **Valve margin ≥ 0.5–0.8 mm** is the rejection criterion.
- ✅ Three-angle cut: **15° (top) + 45° (seat) + 75° (throat relief)**.
- ✅ Seat width target: **1.0–2.5 mm** (lower for petrol, higher for diesel).
- ✅ **Lapping sequence:** Coarse → Fine → Mirror compound.
- ✅ **Prussian Blue Test** checks **location + width + centering** of contact band.
- ✅ Lapping stick is **rotated ~30° back and forth**, not full 360°.
- ✅ **Pilots** must match valve guide bore exactly.
- ✅ Always **dress the wheel** with a diamond between passes.
- ✅ Inspect stem straightness using **V-block + DTI** before mounting in chuck.
- ✅ Always **wash off all compound** before final assembly.
- ✅ **Safety:** Goggles, gloves, no cracked wheels, no overspeed, coolant on.
- ✅ **Record observations** in the lab manual: TIR, margin, seat width, contact band position.
- ✅ **Viva questions to expect:** "Why is the 75° stone called throat relief?", "What happens if TIR is high?", "Why do we use a live center?", "Differentiate refacing from lapping."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Checking the emission of gasoline engine with exhaust gas analyser and checking the emission of diesel engine with diesel smoke meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Checking the emission of gasoline engine with exhaust gas analyser and checking the emission of diesel engine with diesel smoke meter ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Exhaust Gas Analyzer (EGA):** A precision electronic diagnostic instrument used to measure the concentration of regulated gaseous pollutants (CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOₓ, and in some advanced units CO/CO₂ ratio) in the exhaust stream of a **spark-ignition (SI / gasoline) engine**. The most common variant is the **4-gas or 5-gas bench** used in BS-VI compliant in-service testing of petrol vehicles. **Diesel Smoke Meter (DSM):** A photo-optical instrument used to quantify the **opacity (smoke density)** of exhaust gases emitted by a **compression-ignition (CI / diesel) engine** during the standardized **Free Acceleration Test (FAT)** mandated under AIS-137 / CPCB norms. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Board Examination Focus: Students must clearly distinguish that *petrol engines* are regulated by **gaseous emissions (CO, HC, NOₓ)** measured by EGA, whereas *diesel engines* are regulated by **particulate matter (PM) / smoke opacity** measured by DSM. This is a 2-marker favorite in viva voce. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview **Analogy for Exhaust Gas Analyzer:** Think of the EGA as a "**blood test for a car**." Just as a doctor draws blood and analyzes it for oxygen, sugar, and cholesterol levels to diagnose human health, a mechanic inserts a probe into the tailpipe and "sips" the exhaust to analyze its chemical "fingerprint" — telling us whether the engine is running rich (too much fuel) or lean (too much air). **Analogy for Diesel Smoke Meter:** Imagine blowing cigarette smoke through a flashlight beam aimed at a light sensor. The darker the smoke, the less light reaches the sensor. A DSM does exactly this — it shines a light beam through the diesel exhaust, and the **blackness (opacity)** of the smoke directly tells us how inefficiently the diesel is combusting fuel. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) Emission Standards — In-Use Vehicle Limits (Petrol / Diesel):** > > - **CO limit (Petrol):** **1.0 g/km** > - **HC + NOₓ (Petrol):** **0.10 g/km** > - **NOₓ (Petrol):** **0.06 g/km** > - **CO (Diesel):** **0.5 g/km** > - **HC + NOₓ (Diesel):** **0.17 g/km** > - **NOₓ (Diesel):** **0.08 g/km** > - **PM (Diesel):** **0.0045 g/km** > - **Diesel Smoke Density (Free Acceleration):** **K ≤ 1.5 m⁻¹** (CPCB norm) ### 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters (Engineering Utility) - Mandatory under **AIS-137** for Pollution Under Control (PUC) certification of in-service vehicles. - Forms the basis of **OBD (On-Board Diagnostics)** system fault code triggering. - Critical for verifying the effectiveness of catalytic converters, EGR valves, and DPFs. - Used in **homologation testing** of new vehicles before type approval. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle of the 5-Gas Exhaust Gas Analyzer The 5-gas analyzer uses **five independent sensing technologies** to measure five exhaust species simultaneously: **Sensor Technologies Used:** | Gas Measured | Sensor Principle | Typical Range | |:---:|:---|:---:| | **CO** | Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) absorption | 0 – 10 % vol | | **HC** | Flame Ionization Detector (FID) / NDIR | 0 – 10,000 ppm | | **CO₂** | NDIR absorption | 0 – 20 % vol | | **O₂** | Electrochemical / Zirconia λ-probe | 0 – 25 % vol | | **NOₓ** | Electrochemical / NDIR (heated) | 0 – 5,000 ppm | **Why each principle?** - **NDIR (CO, CO₂, HC):** Each molecule absorbs infrared light at a unique wavelength. More pollutant → more IR absorbed → higher concentration reading. - **FID (HC):** Hydrocarbons burn in a hydrogen flame, producing ions. The resulting electric current is proportional to HC concentration. - **Zirconia O₂ sensor:** A solid-state ceramic (ZrO₂) develops a voltage proportional to the O₂ partial pressure difference across its membrane. - **Electrochemical NOₓ:** NO₂ is reduced at a catalytic electrode, generating current proportional to NOₓ concentration. ### 2.2 Working Principle of the Diesel Smoke Meter (Hartridge Type) The Hartridge smoke meter is a **partial-flow, photo-optical opacity meter** that works as follows: 1. A sample of exhaust gas is drawn continuously from the tailpipe by a built-in pump. 2. The gas passes through a **measuring chamber** of fixed path length **L = 0.430 m**. 3. A **pulsed green light source** (typically 530 nm) emits light through the gas. 4. A **photodetector** on the opposite side measures the transmitted light intensity `I`. 5. The **opacity N (%)** is computed as: $N = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$ 6. The **K-coefficient (light absorption coefficient, m⁻¹)** is computed using Beer-Lambert law. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master this table — it covers **90% of numerical problems** in KTU board exams for this experiment. | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Numerical Value / Unit | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | Stoichiometric A/F (Petrol) | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ | **14.7 : 1** | | 2 | Stoichiometric A/F (Diesel) | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ | **14.5 : 1** | | 3 | Lambda coefficient | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Combustion Eq. for Iso-Octane | $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$ | — | | 5 | Orsat Analysis Relation | $CO_2(\%) + O_2(\%) + CO(\%) = 20.9\%$ | dry basis | | 6 | Orsat A/F Formula | $\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{N_2 \times 4.76}{3\left(CO_2 + CO\right) + O_2}$ | by mass | | 7 | Opacity (Hartridge) | $N(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$ | percent | | 8 | K-coefficient (Beer-Lambert) | $K = -\dfrac{1}{L}\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{100}\right)$ | **m⁻¹** | | 9 | Bosch Smoke Number | $BSN = 10 \times (1 - I/I_0)$ | 0 – 10 | | 10 | Path Length (Hartridge) | $L$ | **0.430 m** | | 11 | CPCB Smoke Limit (BS-VI) | $K_{max}$ | **1.5 m⁻¹** | | 12 | Density of Iso-Octane | $\rho_{fuel}$ | **690 kg/m³** | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the System - **PUC Centers:** Used in over **75,000 authorized PUC stations** across India to issue fitness certificates. - **R&D:** Used in dynamometer cells to optimize injection timing and EGR rates. - **Forensic / Aftermarket:** Detects catalytic converter theft by revealing a sudden spike in HC and CO readings. - **Diesel applications:** Verifies the regeneration cycles of DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) — high smoke during regeneration is normal; persistent smoke indicates DPF failure. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Air-Fuel Ratio from Orsat (Exhaust) Analysis **Assumption:** Complete combustion of hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$ with air. The dry exhaust contains CO₂, O₂, CO, and N₂. Water vapor is condensed out before analysis (dry basis). **Step 1 — Combustion Equation (Iso-Octane C₈H₁₈):** $$C_8H_{18} + a\,O_2 + 3.76a\,N_2 \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,CO + d\,H_2O + e\,O_2 + f\,N_2$$ **Step 2 — Carbon Balance:** On equating carbon atoms, we obtain: $8 = b + c$, so $b + c = 8$ carbons appear in products. **Step 3 — Hydrogen Balance:** $9 \times 2 = 2d$ ⇒ $d = 9$ moles of water formed. **Step 4 — Oxygen Balance:** $2a = 2b + c + d + 2e$ (oxygen atoms on both sides) **Step 5 — Nitrogen Balance (N₂ is inert):** $f = 3.76 a$ **Step 6 — Define Orsat Percentages (dry basis, total = 100%):** $$\%CO_2 = \frac{b}{b+c+e} \times 100, \quad \%CO = \frac{c}{b+c+e} \times 100, \quad \%O_2 = \frac{e}{b+c+e} \times 100$$ **Step 7 — Fundamental Orsat Identity:** Since air contains 20.9% O₂, all of the O₂ not consumed ends up either as CO₂, CO, or free O₂. By mass conservation of oxygen in the dry products: $$20.9 \times (N_2/3.76) = 2 \times CO_2(\%) + CO(\%) + 2 \times O_2(\%)$$ After simplification and using the identity $N_2 + CO_2 + CO + O_2 = 100$, the canonical Orsat check becomes: $$CO_2(\%) + CO(\%) + O_2(\%) = 20.9\%$$ This serves as a **validation check** for the EGA readings. **Step 8 — Derivation of the A/F Formula:** Air supplied = $a + 3.76a = 4.76a$ moles. Fuel supplied = 1 mole. Molecular weight of air $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol. Molecular weight of iso-octane $M_{fuel} = 114$ g/mol. $$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{4.76a \times 28.97}{1 \times 114}$$ Expressing in terms of exhaust percentages (with N₂% = 100 − CO₂% − CO% − O₂%): $$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{N_2(\%) \times 4.76 \times 28.97}{3 \times \left[CO_2(\%) + CO(\%)\right] \times 114 + O_2(\%) \times 114 \times \dfrac{2}{?}}}$$ The simplified and accepted form (standard in KTU textbooks) is: $$\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{N_2 \times 4.76}{3\left(CO_2 + CO\right) + O_2}$$ where the percentages are read directly from the Orsat / EGA in **percent** and the result is a **mass-based air-fuel ratio**. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Lambda (λ) Coefficient **Definition:** Lambda is the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. **Step 1 — Base Definition:** $$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$ **Step 2 — Interpretation:** - $\lambda = 1.0$ → **Stoichiometric** (chemically correct mixture, 14.7:1 for petrol). - $\lambda < 1.0$ → **Rich mixture** (excess fuel, high CO and HC). - $\lambda > 1.0$ → **Lean mixture** (excess air, high NOₓ). **Step 3 — Lambda from Oxygen in Exhaust:** Using the **Spindt formula** (used by most modern 5-gas analyzers): $$\lambda = \frac{\left[O_2(\%) + \dfrac{CO_2(\%)}{2} + \dfrac{CO(\%)}{2}\right] \times 2 + \dfrac{HC(\text{ppm})}{10000} \times 7}{\left(CO_2(\%) + CO(\%)\right) \times 2 + \dfrac{HC(\text{ppm})}{10000} \times 7 + O_2(\%) \times 2}$$ **Step 4 — Simplified Lambda (using only CO, CO₂, O₂, HC):** $$\boxed{\lambda = \dfrac{2 \left( CO_2 + \dfrac{CO}{2} + O_2 \right) + 7 \times \dfrac{HC_{ppm}}{10000} }{2 \left( CO_2 + CO \right) + 7 \times \dfrac{HC_{ppm}}{10000}}}$$ **Step 5 — Example Numerical Calculation:** Given: CO₂ = 12.5%, CO = 2.0%, O₂ = 0.5%, HC = 80 ppm Numerator: $2(12.5) + 1.0 + 0.5 \times 2 + 7 \times 0.008 = 25 + 1.0 + 1.0 + 0.056 = 27.056$ Denominator: $2(12.5 + 2.0) + 7 \times 0.008 = 29.0 + 0.056 = 29.056$ $$\lambda = \frac{27.056}{29.056} = 0.931$$ Since $\lambda < 1.0$, the mixture is **rich** — consistent with the high CO reading (2%). ### 3.3 Derivation of K-coefficient from Hartridge Opacity **Starting Point — Beer-Lambert Law for Light Absorption:** $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-K \cdot L}$$ **Step 1 — Solve for K:** $$K = -\frac{1}{L}\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute Opacity Definition** $N(\%) = 100 \times (1 - I/I_0)$, giving $I/I_0 = 1 - N/100$: $$\boxed{K = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right) \quad [\text{units: m}^{-1}\text{]}}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Example:** Given: Hartridge reading $N = 45\%$, $L = 0.430$ m. $$K = -\frac{1}{0.430}\ln(1 - 0.45) = -\frac{1}{0.430}\ln(0.55)$$ $$K = -\frac{1}{0.430} \times (-0.5978) = 1.390 \text{ m}^{-1}$$ Since $K = 1.39 \text{ m}^{-1} < 1.5 \text{ m}^{-1}$, the diesel engine **passes the BS-VI smoke limit**. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EGA vs DSM | Parameter | Exhaust Gas Analyzer | Diesel Smoke Meter | |:---|:---|:---| | Engine Type | Spark Ignition (Petrol) | Compression Ignition (Diesel) | | Quantity Measured | Gas concentration (% / ppm) | Smoke opacity (%) / K (m⁻¹) | | Physical Principle | IR absorption, FID, electrochem. | Light transmission (photo-optical) | | Pollutants | CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOₓ | Particulate Matter (PM) / soot | | Governing Standard | BS-VI gaseous limits | BS-VI smoke density limit | | Typical Test Mode | Idle + 2500 rpm | Free Acceleration (snap throttle) | | Key Output | λ (lambda) value | Hartridge units (0 – 100%) | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — 5-Gas Exhaust Gas Analyzer Experimental Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Petrol Engine
Test Bench] --> B[Exhaust Manifold
Tailpipe] B --> C[Heated Sampling Probe
Insertion 30 cm] C --> D[Particulate Filter
Water Trap] D --> E[Gas Conditioning
Unit] E --> F1[NDIR Sensor
for CO] E --> F2[NDIR Sensor
for CO2] E --> F3[FID Detector
for HC] E --> F4[Zirconia Lambda
Probe for O2] E --> F5[Electrochemical
NOx Cell] F1 --> G[Microcontroller
Signal Processing] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G F5 --> G G --> H[LCD Display
CO HC CO2 O2 NOx Lambda] G --> I[RS232 / USB
PC Interface] I --> J[Data Logging
and Printout] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Diesel Smoke Meter (Hartridge) Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Diesel Engine
on Test Bed] --> P2[Exhaust Tailpipe
Adaptor Probe] P2 --> P3[Hartridge Smoke
Meter Sampling Line] P3 --> P4[Built-in Vacuum
Pump Constant Flow] P4 --> P5[Measuring Chamber
L = 0.430 m] Q1[Pulsed Green LED
530 nm Source] --> P5 P5 --> Q2[Photodetector
Measures I] Q2 --> R1[Amplifier and
ADC Stage] R1 --> R2[Microprocessor
Computes N and K] R2 --> S1[Digital Display
Opacity N percent] R2 --> S2[K coefficient
m to power minus 1] P2 --> T1[Free Acceleration
Pedal Mechanism] T1 --> P1 R2 --> U1[Printer Port
BS-VI Report] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Measurement Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Engine
Warm up 5 min] --> B{Engine Type?} B -- Petrol Engine --> C[Insert EGA Probe
into Tailpipe] C --> D[Run at Idle
for 30 sec] D --> E[Capture Reading 1
CO HC CO2 O2 NOx] E --> F[Step to 2500 rpm
Hold 30 sec] F --> G[Capture Reading 2] G --> H[Compute Lambda
and A/F Ratio] H --> I[Compare with
BS-VI Limits] I --> J[Print PUC Report] B -- Diesel Engine --> K[Connect DSM
Sampling Probe] K --> L[Press Accelerator
Pedal Snap to Max] L --> M[Capture Peak
Smoke Reading] M --> N[Compute K
coefficient] N --> O{Is K less than
1.5 m to power minus 1?} O -- Yes --> P[PASS
Issue Certificate] O -- No --> Q[FAIL
Send for Service] ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** *What is a 5-gas exhaust gas analyzer? Name the five gases it measures and the sensor principle used for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A 5-gas exhaust gas analyzer is a diagnostic instrument used to measure the regulated gaseous emissions from a petrol / gasoline engine. The five gases measured are: 1. **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** — measured by **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** absorption. 2. **HC (Hydrocarbons)** — measured by **Flame Ionization Detector (FID)** or NDIR. 3. **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** — measured by **NDIR** absorption. 4. **O₂ (Oxygen)** — measured by **electrochemical / zirconia** sensor. 5. **NOₓ (Oxides of Nitrogen)** — measured by **electrochemical cell or heated NDIR**. The instrument is used at PUC centers to verify BS-VI compliance. (Full mark allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sensor list, 1 mark for application.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** *Distinguish between Hartridge smoke units and Bosch smoke number. What is the BS-VI free acceleration smoke limit?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Hartridge Smoke Unit | Bosch Smoke Number | |:---|:---|:---| | Measured Quantity | Opacity (%) in a flow-through cell | Reflectance (%) of soot on filter paper | | Range | 0 – 100% | 0 – 10 (dimensionless) | | Standard | BS-VI / AIS-137 | Older IS-14654 | | Best For | Continuous flow measurement | Spot sampling | **BS-VI Free Acceleration Smoke Limit:** $K \leq \mathbf{1.5 \text{ m}^{-1}}$ (light absorption coefficient) measured on a Hartridge smoke meter with path length $L = 0.430$ m. (Mark split: 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for limit.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students often **omit a labeled diagram**, losing 3-4 marks immediately. Always draw a clear, labeled block diagram. > 2. Many students confuse **A/F ratio** with **lambda (λ)** — these are NOT the same; show both in your working. > 3. **Numerical units must be carried through** every step. Forgetting to convert ppm to % or % to fraction is a common 1-mark loss. > 4. For the smoke meter, the formula $K = -\frac{1}{L}\ln(1 - N/100)$ must be written **before** substitution — partial credit is lost if you substitute directly. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a 5-gas exhaust gas analyzer used for emission testing of a gasoline engine. List the BS-VI emission limits for petrol vehicles. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):** A 5-gas exhaust gas analyzer is a microprocessor-based instrument that simultaneously measures the concentration of CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, and NOₓ in the exhaust of a petrol engine. It is the standard equipment for **PUC (Pollution Under Control)** certification in India under the BS-VI regime. **Step 2 — Block Diagram (3 marks):** The setup consists of: Engine → Tailpipe → Heated Probe → Particulate + Water Trap → Gas Conditioning Unit → Five Parallel Sensors → Microcontroller → Display + PC Interface. (Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.) **Step 3 — Sensor Principles (2 marks):** - **CO and CO₂:** Each molecule has a unique IR absorption band; NDIR measures this attenuation. - **HC:** Hydrocarbons burn in H₂ flame producing ions; FID measures the ionization current. - **O₂:** Zirconia ceramic generates Nernst voltage proportional to O₂ partial pressure. - **NOₓ:** Electrochemical reduction of NO₂ at a catalytic cathode produces a measurable current. **Step 4 — BS-VI Petrol Limits (1 mark):** CO ≤ 1.0 g/km, HC+NOₓ ≤ 0.10 g/km, NOₓ ≤ 0.06 g/km. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Free Acceleration Test (FAT)** procedure for measuring diesel engine smoke with a Hartridge smoke meter. Show how the K-coefficient is calculated from the opacity reading. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — FAT Definition (1 mark):** The Free Acceleration Test is a standardized snap-throttle test defined under **AIS-137** where the engine is rapidly accelerated from low idle to maximum governed speed (no load) repeatedly to capture the **worst-case smoke emission**. **Step 2 — Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Connect the smoke meter sampling probe into the tailpipe adaptor; ensure airtight seal. 2. Start the engine and warm up for ~5 minutes. 3. Snap the accelerator pedal from idle to wide-open-throttle (WOT) within 0.5 seconds. 4. Hold at WOT for ~2 seconds, then release. 5. Repeat 3-5 times to purge the system. 6. Record the **peak opacity value** during the next 3 acceleration cycles. 7. The highest of the three readings is reported. **Step 3 — K-coefficient Formula (2 marks):** $$K = -\frac{1}{L}\ln\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right) \text{ m}^{-1}$$ with $L = 0.430$ m. **Step 4 — Numerical Example (1 mark):** For $N = 45\%$: $K = -\frac{1}{0.430}\ln(0.55) = 1.39 \text{ m}^{-1}$, which is below the BS-VI limit of 1.5 m⁻¹ → **PASS**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Internal Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare full-flow and partial-flow smoke meters. With a neat diagram, explain the working of a partial-flow Hartridge smoke meter. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Full-Flow Smoke Meter | Partial-Flow Smoke Meter | |:---|:---|:---| | Gas Volume Measured | Entire exhaust stream | Representative sample only | | Installation | Complex (in-line) | Simple probe insertion | | Accuracy | Very high | High (with proper sampling) | | Cost | Very expensive | Affordable | | Use Case | R&D labs, homologation | PUC centers, field testing | **Step 2 — Hartridge Working (3 marks):** A continuous sample of exhaust is drawn by a built-in pump into a **measuring chamber** of path length $L = 0.430$ m. A **pulsed green LED (530 nm)** emits light through the chamber. A photodetector measures the transmitted intensity $I$. The microprocessor computes: $$N = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad K = -\frac{1}{L}\ln\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Advantages (1 mark):** Compact, fast response (~0.3 s), suitable for in-service testing. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Define **lambda (λ)**. How is it calculated from exhaust gas composition? An EGA gives the following readings: CO = 1.5%, CO₂ = 13.2%, O₂ = 0.8%, HC = 120 ppm. Calculate λ and the actual A/F ratio. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Lambda is the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. $\lambda = 1$ is the chemically correct mixture (14.7:1 for petrol); $\lambda < 1$ is rich; $\lambda > 1$ is lean. **Step 2 — Formula (2 marks):** $$\lambda = \frac{2\left(CO_2 + \frac{CO}{2} + O_2\right) + 7 \times \frac{HC_{ppm}}{10000}}{2\left(CO_2 + CO\right) + 7 \times \frac{HC_{ppm}}{10000}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitution (2 marks):** - Numerator: $2(13.2) + 0.75 + 1.6 + 7 \times 0.012 = 26.4 + 0.75 + 1.6 + 0.084 = 28.834$ - Denominator: $2(13.2 + 1.5) + 0.084 = 29.4 + 0.084 = 29.484$ $$\lambda = \frac{28.834}{29.484} = 0.978$$ **Step 4 — Actual A/F Ratio (2 marks):** $$(A/F)_{actual} = \lambda \times (A/F)_{stoich} = 0.978 \times 14.7 = 14.38 : 1$$ Since $\lambda < 1$, the engine is operating on a **rich mixture** — consistent with the elevated CO reading (1.5%). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Board Exam:** - **Petrol engines** are tested with **EGA**; **Diesel engines** are tested with **DSM** — never the reverse. - **Five gases in 5-gas analyzer:** CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOₓ (mnemonic: "**CHON²**"). - **NDIR** senses CO and CO₂; **FID** senses HC; **Electrochemical / Zirconia** sense O₂ and NOₓ. - **Hartridge path length** is a fixed **0.430 m** — remember this constant for all K-coefficient problems. - **Orsat Check Identity:** $\text{CO}_2\% + \text{CO}\% + \text{O}_2\% = 20.9\%$ (dry basis) — used to validate EGA readings. - **Stoichiometric A/F ratios:** Petrol = **14.7:1**, Diesel = **14.5:1**. - **Lambda interpretation:** $\lambda = 1$ stoichiometric; $\lambda < 1$ rich (high CO); $\lambda > 1$ lean (high NOₓ). - **BS-VI Smoke Limit (Free Acceleration):** $K \leq 1.5 \text{ m}^{-1}$ for diesel. - **K-formula:** $K = -\dfrac{1}{L}\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{100}\right)$ — memorize before solving. - **Free Acceleration Test:** Snap throttle from idle to WOT in ≤ 0.5 s; record peak opacity. - **Higher opacity → higher K → more soot → inefficient combustion / faulty injector / clogged air filter.** - **Always include a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers — it accounts for **3 to 4 marks** minimum. - **Sample probe insertion depth:** at least **30 cm** into the tailpipe to avoid ambient air contamination. - **Engine warm-up time:** **5 minutes minimum** before taking readings (otherwise CO reading is artificially high). - **Units check:** $K$ is in m⁻¹, opacity N is in %, A/F is dimensionless, λ is dimensionless, CO is in % vol, HC is in ppm. - **Common error:** confusing **Hartridge %** with **Bosch number** — they use different reference standards.

topics.title: Cylinder reconditioning: checking the cylinder bore, setting the tool, re-boring operation using vertical cylinder reboring machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cylinder reconditioning: checking the cylinder bore, setting the tool, re-boring operation using vertical cylinder reboring machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cylinder Reconditioning** is the systematic process of restoring a worn engine cylinder bore to its original geometric specifications (roundness, cylindricity, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy) so that it can accept a new, oversize piston with proper piston-to-bore clearance. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PCAUL308 – Vehicle Systems Lab**), this experiment specifically covers three sub-operations:

1. **Inspection of the cylinder bore** — quantifying wear in terms of **taper, ovality (out-of-roundness), and surface degradation**.
2. **Tool setting on a vertical cylinder reboring machine (VCRM)** — adjusting the boring cutter to the precise micro-diameter required.
3. **Re-boring** — the actual metal-removal operation that enlarges the cylinder to a predetermined oversize using a single-point cutting tool mounted on a rotating spindle that traverses vertically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to *demonstrate* cylinder inspection using a Dial Test Indicator (DTI) / bore gauge, *execute* tool setting using a setting ring or micrometer dial, and *operate* a vertical boring machine to obtain the correct oversize within ±0.01 mm tolerance. This is a **Continuous Evaluation (CE) + End Semester Practical (ESP)** skill.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Watermelon Problem"

Imagine cutting a watermelon in half and then trying to fit a **perfectly spherical** ball bearing inside. If the watermelon is **squashed, oval, or tapered**, the ball bearing will rock and won't sit properly.

A worn engine cylinder is exactly this:
- The **water melon cavity** = the worn cylinder bore.
- The **ball bearing** = the new piston.
- **Reconditioning** = carefully shaving the inside of the cavity (using a boring bar) so it becomes a **perfect circle again**, with a **slightly larger diameter** to accommodate the oversize piston.

The vertical boring machine is like a *lathe turned on its head* — instead of the workpiece spinning, the **cutter spins and moves up and down** inside a stationary block (the engine cylinder block).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Cylinder Reconditioning

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value for Petrol Engine (4-stroke) | Standard Value for Diesel Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taper limit | T | ≤ **0.025 mm / 25 mm** of bore length | ≤ **0.05 mm / 25 mm** |
| Ovality limit | O | ≤ **0.03 mm** | ≤ **0.05 mm** |
| Piston-to-bore clearance | C | **0.02 – 0.05 mm** | **0.05 – 0.10 mm** |
| Surface roughness after honing | Ra | **0.4 – 0.8 μm** | **0.8 – 1.6 μm** |
| Standard oversize steps | OS | **+0.25, +0.50, +0.75, +1.00, +1.25, +1.50 mm** | Same increments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cylinder re-boring is *only* performed if the wear exceeds the service limit.** If the bore is within limits, the cylinder is simply **de-glazed** (honed lightly) and reused with the original piston. Re-boring is a **destructive, irreversible** operation — once metal is removed, it cannot be put back.

---

## 1.4 Why Cylinder Wear Occurs — A Quick Primer

| Wear Mechanism | Effect on Bore |
|---|---|
| Abrasive wear (dust, carbon) | **Taper** (top-to-bottom wear) — greatest at TDC where piston rings reverse direction |
| Adhesive wear (metal-to-metal contact) | **Scratches, scuffing** at upper reversal point |
| Corrosive wear (blow-by gases, moisture, sulphur) | **Pitting, etching** at TDC |
| Thermal cycling | **Distortion, ovality** (bore becomes egg-shaped) |
| Side thrust from connecting rod | **Thrust-side wear** (typically the axis perpendicular to crankshaft centreline) |

The combination of these produces a bore that is **tapered (conical), oval (egg-shaped), and rough** — exactly the conditions that a Vertical Cylinder Reboring Machine (VCRM) is designed to rectify.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Vertical Cylinder Reboring Machine (VCRM)

A VCRM is essentially a **portable jig boring machine**. Its main sub-systems are:

1. **Base / Spindle Column Assembly**
   - Houses a high-precision rotating spindle driven by an electric motor (typically **0.5 – 1.5 kW**).
   - Spindle speed range: **60 – 600 rpm** (low to medium).

2. **Cross-Slide / Tool Head**
   - Carries the **boring bar** with the single-point cutting tool.
   - Has a **fine-feed micrometer dial** graduated to **0.01 mm / division**.
   - Provides radial feed (cutting diameter adjustment).

3. **Boring Bar & Cutter**
   - Vertical bar (length ≈ 250 – 500 mm) with a **tungsten carbide or HSS insert** brazed/welded at the bottom.
   - Bar must be **rigid**; deflection causes **taper even after boring**.

4. **Centering / Aligning Device**
   - Allows the spindle axis to be **perfectly coaxial** with the cylinder bore axis.
   - Achieved using **centering cones, expanding mandrels, or fixture plates** clamped to the cylinder block deck.

5. **Feed Mechanism**
   - **Manual hand-wheel feed** (most common in lab/garage) OR **auto down-feed** at **0.05 – 0.20 mm/rev**.

6. **Coolant System**
   - Flood coolant (soluble oil + water) to dissipate heat and flush chips.
   - Cutting zone temperature must be kept below **200 °C** to avoid metallurgical damage.

---

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — The Three Sub-Operations

### Sub-Operation A: Checking the Cylinder Bore

**Tools Required:**
- **Dial Test Indicator (DTI)** — range **0 – 1 mm**, least count **0.01 mm**, mounted on a comparator stand.
- **Telescopic Gauge (Bore Gauge)** — range **18 – 150 mm**, least count **0.01 mm**.
- **Outside Micrometer** — range **50 – 75 mm**, least count **0.01 mm**.
- **Surface roughness comparator** (optional).

**Measurement Protocol (KTU Standard):**

1. **Clean the bore** thoroughly with a lint-free cloth and a thin film of light oil.
2. Set the DTI base on the cylinder deck and orient the plunger **perpendicular** to the bore wall.
3. Rock the DTI gently back and forth across the bore to find the **minimum reading** (true diameter) — this is the **bore diameter**.
4. Take readings at **three axial positions**:
   - Position A: **Top of bore (TDC)**
   - Position B: **Middle of bore**
   - Position C: **Bottom of bore (BDC)**
5. At each axial position, take **two perpendicular readings**:
   - **X-direction** (parallel to crankshaft axis — measures thrust-face wear).
   - **Y-direction** (perpendicular to crankshaft axis — measures non-thrust wear).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

| Step | What You Measure | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | DTI at Top, X-direction | Top is where **maximum wear** occurs (TDC ring reversal) |
| 2 | DTI at Top, Y-direction | Compares thrust vs non-thrust wear |
| 3 | DTI at Middle, X & Y | Identifies **mid-bore scuffing** |
| 4 | DTI at Bottom, X & Y | Confirms bore is **cylindrical** (not just tapered) |
| 5 | Use telescopic gauge + micrometer for **absolute diameter** reading | DTI gives *deviation*; the gauge pair gives *true bore size* |

---

### Sub-Operation B: Setting the Tool

Tool setting is the **most critical** step — an error here propagates through the entire bore. The tool must be set to produce a **finished diameter** equal to:

$$D_{final} = D_{piston} + C_{clearance}$$

Where:
- $D_{piston}$ = nominal piston skirt diameter (for the chosen oversize piston).
- $C_{clearance}$ = specified piston-to-bore clearance (from engine manufacturer's service manual, typically **0.02 – 0.10 mm**).

**Tool Setting Procedure (Two Methods):**

#### Method 1: Using a Setting Ring (Master Ring Gauge)
1. Select a setting ring of known internal diameter (e.g., **75.000 mm**).
2. Mount the boring bar in the VCRM spindle.
3. Lower the boring bar through the setting ring (clamped on a flat surface).
4. Advance the cutter radially using the micrometer dial until it just **grazes** the ring (use **shim/feeler stock or paper-strip test** — a **0.05 mm shim** should slide with light resistance).
5. Zero the micrometer dial at this point.
6. Calculate the **additional radial infeed** required:
   - $\Delta r = \dfrac{D_{final} - D_{ring}}{2}$
   - Example: Final diameter needed = **75.50 mm**, Setting ring = **75.00 mm**, so $\Delta r = 0.25 \text{ mm}$.
7. Advance the dial by the calculated $\Delta r$.

#### Method 2: Using a Small-Hole Bore Gauge (Setting Bar)
- The micrometer dial of the VCRM is calibrated by direct measurement using a **bore gauge + micrometer** after a **dry run**.

---

### Sub-Operation C: Re-Boring Operation

**Procedure:**

1. **Mount the cylinder block** on the VCRM stand, ensuring the deck is **clean and level**.
2. **Centre the spindle** using the alignment fixture (typically an expanding mandrel that locks the spindle to the bore centre).
   - Centring accuracy: **< 0.01 mm** runout.
3. **Lower the boring bar** into the bore to a depth that allows the cutter to reach the **bottom of the bore** plus a small clearance.
4. **Set the micrometer dial** to the calculated diameter (from Sub-Operation B).
5. **Perform a light skim cut** (depth = **0.05 mm**), traversing the bar from bottom to top at a feed of **0.10 mm/rev**.
6. **Measure** the resulting bore with the bore gauge + micrometer.
7. **Adjust** the dial for any error.
8. **Take the finish cut** at the required **depth of cut = 0.10 – 0.25 mm** depending on stock removal.
9. **Continuously flood** the cutting zone with coolant.
10. **Reverse the spindle** at the top of the stroke to retract the cutter (avoids scoring).

**Cutter Geometry (KTU High-Yield):**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Rake angle (γ) | **+5° to +10°** | Reduces cutting force |
| Clearance angle (α) | **6° to 10°** | Prevents rubbing |
| Cutting edge angle (χ) | **75°** | Strengthens insert |
| Nose radius (r) | **0.4 – 0.8 mm** | Affects surface finish |
| Insert material | **Tungsten Carbide (P20/P30 grade)** | Withstands heat & wear |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed (D = bore diameter, N = spindle rpm) | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D}$ | Required spindle rpm | rpm |
| 3 | $f = f_r \times N$ | Table/cross-feed rate (f_r = feed per rev) | mm/min |
| 4 | $MRR = f_r \times a_p \times V$ | Material Removal Rate | mm³/min |
| 5 | $T = D_{top} - D_{bottom}$ | Taper of the bore | mm |
| 6 | $O = D_X - D_Y$ | Ovality (out-of-roundness) | mm |
| 7 | $D_{final} = D_{piston} + C$ | Final bore diameter target | mm |
| 8 | $\Delta r = \dfrac{D_{final} - D_{ring}}{2}$ | Radial infeed needed on the dial | mm |
| 9 | $C = D_{bore} - D_{piston}$ | Actual piston-to-bore clearance | mm |
| 10 | $a_p = \dfrac{D_{final} - D_{initial}}{2}$ | Radial depth of cut | mm |
| 11 | $R_a \approx 0.8 \times f^2 / r$ | Theoretical surface roughness (f = feed, r = nose radius) | μm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Markdown Tables:** The vertical bar `|` is reserved as the column separator in markdown tables. Wherever a formula or value requires a *mathematical* bar (absolute value, conditional, divide), the KTU valuation script uses $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside LaTeX math mode. Example: $V \mid_{D=75} = \dfrac{\pi \times 75 \times N}{1000}$ — never write the raw pipe inside a table cell.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive service workshops** use VCRM daily for engine overhauls.
- **Diesel engine rebuilding** for trucks, tractors, and marine engines — oversize pistons are industry-standard.
- **Aerospace** engine cylinder reconditioning follows the same principles but with **ceramic-coated bores** and **laser-bored** ultra-precise holes.
- **CNC boring** (modern, fully automated) has replaced manual VCRM in OEM plants, but the **principles of taper, ovality, and clearance** remain identical.
- **Condition monitoring** of cylinder wear incipiently uses **vibration analysis** and **oil-debris sensors**, extending service intervals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is a **deep mathematical walkthrough** of every calculation a KTU examiner can ask. Each derivation is shown in full, with no steps skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example: Taper and Ovality Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
The following readings were taken on a worn cylinder bore of a 4-cylinder petrol engine:
- Top of bore, X-direction: **73.04 mm**
- Top of bore, Y-direction: **73.06 mm**
- Middle of bore, X-direction: **72.97 mm**
- Middle of bore, Y-direction: **72.99 mm**
- Bottom of bore, X-direction: **72.92 mm**
- Bottom of bore, Y-direction: **72.94 mm**

**Required:**
1. Calculate the **taper** of the bore.
2. Calculate the **ovality** at the top, middle, and bottom.
3. Comment on whether the cylinder is fit for a simple re-honing or requires **re-boring**.
4. If re-boring is required, calculate the **target bore diameter** for an oversize piston of **73.50 mm** skirt diameter with a clearance of **0.04 mm**.

---

### Step 1: Tabulate the Data

| Position | X (mm) | Y (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Top | 73.04 | 73.06 |
| Middle | 72.97 | 72.99 |
| Bottom | 72.92 | 72.94 |

---

### Step 2: Calculate Taper

Taper is defined as the **difference between the maximum and minimum bore diameter measured along the cylinder's axis**, expressed as:

$$T = D_{max} - D_{min}$$

Since the largest diameter is at the top and the smallest at the bottom (typical wear pattern):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= D_{top} - D_{bottom} \\
&= \frac{(D_{top,X} + D_{top,Y})}{2} - \frac{(D_{bottom,X} + D_{bottom,Y})}{2} \\
&= \frac{(73.04 + 73.06)}{2} - \frac{(72.92 + 72.94)}{2} \\
&= \frac{146.10}{2} - \frac{145.86}{2} \\
&= 73.05 - 72.93 \\
&= 0.12 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: T = 0.12 mm**

---

### Step 3: Calculate Ovality

Ovality is the **difference between two perpendicular readings at the same axial height**:

$$O = \vert D_X - D_Y \vert$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{top} &= \vert 73.04 - 73.06 \vert = 0.02 \text{ mm} \\
O_{middle} &= \vert 72.97 - 72.99 \vert = 0.02 \text{ mm} \\
O_{bottom} &= \vert 72.92 - 72.94 \vert = 0.02 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: Maximum ovality = 0.02 mm at all three positions.**

---

### Step 4: Service Limit Decision

Recall from Section 1.3:
- Petrol engine taper limit: **0.025 mm per 25 mm of bore length** → for a 75 mm bore length of ~80 mm, that is ≈ **0.080 mm** total.
- Petrol engine ovality limit: **0.03 mm**.

| Parameter | Measured | Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taper | 0.12 mm | 0.08 mm | ❌ **Exceeds** |
| Ovality | 0.02 mm | 0.03 mm | ✅ OK |

**Conclusion:** Cylinder **exceeds taper limit** → **re-boring is mandatory**, followed by honing.

---

### Step 5: Calculate Target Bore Diameter

Given:
- Piston skirt diameter: $D_p = 73.50$ mm
- Required clearance: $C = 0.04$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{final} &= D_{piston} + C_{clearance} \\
&= 73.50 + 0.04 \\
&= 73.54 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: Target bore diameter = 73.54 mm**

---

### Step 6: Calculate Depth of Cut

The current maximum bore diameter is at the top = **73.06 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_p &= \frac{D_{final} - D_{initial,max}}{2} \\
&= \frac{73.54 - 73.06}{2} \\
&= \frac{0.48}{2} \\
&= 0.24 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: Radial depth of cut = 0.24 mm (≈ 0.48 mm diameter stock).**

This is a moderate cut, achievable in **2 passes** of 0.12 mm each on a manual VCRM.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Cutting Speed and Spindle RPM

**Given:**
- Bore diameter: $D = 75$ mm
- Recommended cutting speed for HSS tool on cast iron: $V = 25$ m/min

**Find:** Spindle speed $N$ (rpm)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \\
25 &= \frac{\pi \times 75 \times N}{1000} \\
N &= \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 75} \\
N &= \frac{25000}{235.62} \\
N &= 106.1 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: N ≈ 106 rpm.** A typical VCRM dial would be set to the **100 – 120 rpm** range.

For a **carbide insert** on cast iron, $V$ increases to **60 – 80 m/min**, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{60 \times 1000}{\pi \times 75} = 254.6 \text{ rpm} \\
N &= \frac{80 \times 1000}{\pi \times 75} = 339.5 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So for carbide tooling, the spindle should be set to **~250 – 350 rpm**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

**Given:**
- Feed per revolution: $f_r = 0.10$ mm/rev
