- Depth of cut: $a_p = 0.24$ mm (radial)
- Cutting speed: $V = 25$ m/min

**Find:** MRR

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= f_r \times a_p \times V \quad \text{(Note: in boring, a_p is the radial depth)} \\
&= 0.10 \times 0.24 \times 25 \times 1000 \quad \text{(converting m/min to mm/min)} \\
&= 0.10 \times 0.24 \times 25000 \\
&= 600 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: MRR = 600 mm³/min.** This is a low MRR, confirming that boring is a **finishing** operation rather than a roughing one.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Theoretical Surface Roughness

After boring, honing is still required. The **Ra value produced by the boring tool alone** is approximated by:

$$R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{8 r}$$

where $f$ = feed per rev, $r$ = nose radius.

**Given:** $f = 0.10$ mm/rev, $r = 0.8$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a &\approx \frac{(0.10)^2}{8 \times 0.8} \\
&= \frac{0.01}{6.4} \\
&= 0.00156 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1.56 \text{ μm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a smooth theoretical finish, but **boring leaves visible feed marks** along the bore wall. **Honing** (next operation in the workflow) is what actually reduces Ra to the final **0.4 – 0.8 μm** target.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — What Tool Action for What Wear?

| Wear Diagnosis | Recommended Action | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Taper ≤ 0.025 mm, Ovality ≤ 0.03 mm, surface glazed | **De-glazing** (light hone only) | Bore is still cylindrical, just needs fresh cross-hatch for ring seating |
| Taper 0.025 – 0.10 mm, Ovality ≤ 0.05 mm | **Re-bore to next oversize + hone** | Beyond service limit but not catastrophic |
| Taper > 0.10 mm OR Ovality > 0.05 mm OR visible scuffing | **Re-bore + hone**, possibly to 2nd or 3rd oversize | Severe wear; larger piston needed |
| Cracks, deep scoring, corrosion pitting | **Sleeving** (wet or dry liner) | Bore wall is structurally compromised |
| Bore out-of-round (egg-shaped) but round again possible | **Re-bore to largest oversize** that brings it round | Taper & ovality *cannot* be fixed by honing alone |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Important Note on Question Style:** The KTU 2024 Scheme follows the **NEP 2020 / OBE (Outcome-Based Education)** pattern. Continuous Evaluation (CE) components for labs test **procedural knowledge** and **calculation skills** during the regular lab session, while the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE) Practical** tests **higher-order cognitive skills** (Apply, Analyse, Evaluate, Create — Bloom Levels 3 to 6). The questions below mirror this mix.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - PCAUL308 Internal CE, Sept 2024]`
**Define taper and ovality of a cylinder bore. State the typical service limits for a 4-stroke petrol engine cylinder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Taper (1 mark):** Taper is the difference between the maximum and minimum diameters of the cylinder bore measured along the axial direction. It occurs primarily at the **top of the bore (TDC)** due to the reversal of piston ring motion.
- **Ovality (1 mark):** Ovality, also called out-of-roundness, is the difference between two perpendicular diameter readings at the same axial position of the bore. It results from side-thrust of the connecting rod and thermal distortion.
- **Service Limits (1 mark):**
  - Taper: **≤ 0.025 mm per 25 mm of bore length** (≈ 0.08 mm total for a typical 75 mm bore).
  - Ovality: **≤ 0.03 mm** for petrol engines; **≤ 0.05 mm** for diesel engines.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - PCAUL308 Lab Quiz, Mar 2024]`
**List the tools and instruments required to (a) measure cylinder bore taper, and (b) set the boring tool on a VCRM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) For measuring cylinder bore taper (1.5 marks):**
- Dial Test Indicator (DTI) with comparator stand — least count **0.01 mm**.
- Telescopic gauge — range **18 – 150 mm**.
- Outside micrometer — range **50 – 75 mm** (or appropriate to the bore size).
- Cleaning cloth and light lubricating oil.

**(b) For setting the boring tool on a VCRM (1.5 marks):**
- Setting ring (master ring gauge) of known internal diameter close to the target bore.
- Feeler stock / shim of thickness **0.05 mm** for the contact test.
- Feeler gauge set (0.02 – 1.0 mm) for verification.
- The VCRM's own **micrometer dial** (graduated to **0.01 mm**) on the cross-slide.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice is mandatory. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - PCAUL308 ESE, May 2024]`**

> During inspection of a worn 4-stroke petrol engine cylinder, the following DTI readings (in mm) were recorded:
>
> | Position | Reading 1 (X-axis) | Reading 2 (Y-axis) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Top of bore | 68.07 | 68.09 |
> | Middle of bore | 67.99 | 68.00 |
> | Bottom of bore | 67.95 | 67.96 |
>
> **(i)** Calculate the **taper** and **maximum ovality** of the bore.
> **(ii)** State whether the cylinder needs **re-boring** or only **re-honing**, with justification. Use service limits of **0.025 mm/25 mm** for taper and **0.03 mm** for ovality.
> **(iii)** If an oversize piston of **68.50 mm** skirt diameter is to be fitted with a clearance of **0.04 mm**, calculate the **target bore diameter** and the **radial depth of cut** required.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Taper and Ovality (3 marks):**

Average diameter at each position:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{top} &= \frac{68.07 + 68.09}{2} = 68.080 \text{ mm} \\
D_{middle} &= \frac{67.99 + 68.00}{2} = 67.995 \text{ mm} \\
D_{bottom} &= \frac{67.95 + 67.96}{2} = 67.955 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taper:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= D_{top} - D_{bottom} \\
&= 68.080 - 67.955 \\
&= 0.125 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Ovality at each position:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{top} &= \vert 68.07 - 68.09 \vert = 0.02 \text{ mm} \\
O_{middle} &= \vert 67.99 - 68.00 \vert = 0.01 \text{ mm} \\
O_{bottom} &= \vert 67.95 - 67.96 \vert = 0.01 \text{ mm} \\
\max(O) &= 0.02 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Decision (1.5 marks):**

- Taper measured = **0.125 mm**; limit for ~80 mm bore = 0.080 mm. **Taper exceeds the limit.**
- Ovality = **0.02 mm**; limit = 0.03 mm. **Ovality is within limit.**
- **Conclusion: RE-BORING is mandatory**, followed by honing.

**(iii) Target Bore Diameter and Depth of Cut (2.5 marks):**

Target diameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{final} &= D_{piston} + C_{clearance} \\
&= 68.50 + 0.04 \\
&= 68.54 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Radial depth of cut (taking $D_{initial,max} = 68.09$ mm from the top Y reading):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_p &= \frac{D_{final} - D_{initial,max}}{2} \\
&= \frac{68.54 - 68.09}{2} \\
&= \frac{0.45}{2} \\
&= 0.225 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Taper = **0.125 mm**, Max ovality = **0.02 mm**, $D_{final}$ = **68.54 mm**, $a_p$ = **0.225 mm**. Re-bore is required.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the procedure of setting the boring tool on a Vertical Cylinder Reboring Machine (VCRM) using a setting ring. Also state the formula used to calculate the radial infeed.**

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Introduction to VCRM (1 mark):** Definition, purpose, and the need for tool setting before boring.
2. **Block diagram of VCRM subsystems (1.5 marks):** Spindle, boring bar, cross-slide, micrometer dial, centring fixture, feed mechanism. (Refer to Section 4.2.)
3. **Tool setting procedure (3 marks):**
   - Step 1: Mount the boring bar in the spindle.
   - Step 2: Place the setting ring on a flat datum.
   - Step 3: Lower the bar through the ring.
   - Step 4: Advance the cutter radially until it grazes the ring (0.05 mm shim test).
   - Step 5: Zero the micrometer dial.
   - Step 6: Calculate additional radial infeed using $\Delta r = \dfrac{D_{final} - D_{ring}}{2}$.
   - Step 7: Advance the dial by $\Delta r$.
   - Step 8: Verify with a second measurement (bore gauge).
4. **Formula and worked example (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta r = \frac{D_{final} - D_{ring}}{2}
$$

**Example:** If $D_{final}$ = 68.54 mm, $D_{ring}$ = 68.00 mm, then $\Delta r$ = 0.27 mm.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - PCAUL308 ESE, Dec 2023]`**

> **(i)** What is the function of a **boring bar** in a VCRM? List **any four** important cutter geometry parameters used in cylinder boring.
> **(ii)** A boring operation is to be carried out on a cast iron cylinder block of diameter **75 mm** at a cutting speed of **30 m/min** using an HSS tool. Calculate the **spindle rpm**.
> **(iii)** If the feed per revolution is **0.10 mm/rev** and the depth of cut is **0.30 mm** (radial), calculate the **material removal rate**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Function and Cutter Geometry (2 marks):**
- **Function of boring bar (1 mark):** The boring bar holds and supports the single-point cutting tool that removes metal from the inside of the cylinder to enlarge and true up the bore to the required oversize.
- **Four cutter geometry parameters (1 mark):**
  1. **Rake angle (γ) = +5° to +10°** — reduces cutting force.
  2. **Clearance angle (α) = 6° to 10°** — prevents rubbing on the bore wall.
  3. **Cutting edge angle (χ) = 75°** — strengthens the cutting edge.
  4. **Nose radius (r) = 0.4 – 0.8 mm** — influences surface finish.

**(ii) Spindle RPM (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 75} \\
&= \frac{30000}{235.62} \\
&= 127.3 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: N ≈ 127 rpm.**

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= f_r \times a_p \times V \\
&= 0.10 \times 0.30 \times 30 \quad \text{(in mm³/min, but V must be in mm/min)} \\
&= 0.10 \times 0.30 \times 30000 \\
&= 900 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: MRR = 900 mm³/min.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the step-by-step procedure for performing the re-boring operation on a vertical cylinder reboring machine. State any **four** precautions to be observed during the operation.**

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Block mounting and centring (1.5 marks):** Clean deck, place block on VCRM stand, clamp, and centre spindle using the alignment fixture to within **0.01 mm** runout.
2. **Tool setting (1.5 marks):** Set cutter to the calculated $D_{final}$ using the setting ring or bore-gauge method.
3. **Skim cut and measurement (1.5 marks):** Take a light cut (0.05 mm radial), traverse bottom-to-top, measure and adjust the dial.
4. **Finish cut (1 mark):** Take the final cut at the calculated $a_p$ with continuous coolant flow.
5. **Retraction (0.5 mark):** Reverse the spindle before reaching the deck to prevent scoring.
6. **Four precautions (1 mark):**
   - Always flood the cutting zone with coolant.
   - Never take a cut without verifying the tool setting.
   - Keep the bar vertical — any tilt causes taper.
   - Measure the bore *immediately* after each cut using the bore gauge + micrometer.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCAUL308 Lab ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing taper direction:** Taper is **axial** (top-to-bottom); ovality is **radial** (X-to-Y). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve the diameter difference:** When computing radial infeed or depth of cut, students write $D_{final} - D_{initial}$ instead of $\dfrac{D_{final} - D_{initial}}{2}$. This is the **single most common calculation error** in PCAUL308.
> 3. **Unit errors in cutting speed formula:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ — if $D$ is in mm and $N$ in rpm, the **/1000** is mandatory to get $V$ in m/min. Skipping it gives a value 1000× too large.
> 4. **Omitting the honing step:** Boring alone does **not** produce the required **cross-hatch pattern** for oil retention. Examiners explicitly ask why honing follows boring — answer: *"to achieve Ra = 0.4 – 0.8 μm and the 30° – 45° cross-hatch angle for proper piston-ring seating."*
> 5. **Wrong clearance specification:** Always quote clearance from the **manufacturer's service manual**, not a textbook default. If the problem states a specific clearance, use that value.
> 6. **Not zeroing the micrometer dial** before calculating infeed — examiners specifically look for the zeroing step in tool setting.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Cylinder wear is a 3-D problem:** taper (axial), ovality (radial), and surface roughness — all must be measured separately.
- [ ] **Taper limit (petrol):** 0.025 mm / 25 mm. **Ovality limit (petrol):** 0.03 mm.
- [ ] **Reading protocol:** 3 axial positions × 2 perpendicular directions = **6 readings per bore**.
- [ ] **Piston-to-bore clearance** is **added** to the piston diameter, not subtracted, to get the target bore.
- [ ] **Final bore = Piston + Clearance.** Final bore = $\mathbf{D_{final} = D_{piston} + C_{clearance}}$.
- [ ] **Radial depth of cut** = $\mathbf{\dfrac{D_{final} - D_{initial,max}}{2}}$ — **always halve the diameter difference**.
- [ ] **Cutting speed formula:** $\mathbf{V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}}$ m/min (when D in mm, N in rpm).
- [ ] **MRR formula:** $\mathbf{MRR = f_r \times a_p \times V}$ mm³/min.
- [ ] **Tool setting via setting ring:** $\mathbf{\Delta r = \dfrac{D_{final} - D_{ring}}{2}}$.
- [ ] **VCRM boring bar** must be **rigid and vertical**; deflection produces taper.
- [ ] **Cutter geometry:** Rake +5° to +10°, Clearance 6° to 10°, Nose radius 0.4 – 0.8 mm.
- [ ] **Insert material:** Tungsten Carbide **P20/P30 grade** for cast iron; HSS for low-speed shop work.
- [ ] **Coolant:** Continuous flood — soluble oil + water — to keep cutting zone < 200 °C.
- [ ] **Boring is NOT the final step:** Always followed by **honing** to achieve Ra = 0.4 – 0.8 μm and the proper cross-hatch.
- [ ] **Oversize steps:** +0.25, +0.50, +0.75, +1.00, +1.25, +1.50 mm — match piston set to boring diameter.
- [ ] **Reverse the spindle** at the top of the boring stroke to retract the cutter without scoring.
- [ ] **Cracked or deeply scored bores cannot be re-bored** — they require **sleeving (wet or dry liners)**.
- [ ] **Always re-measure** the bore after each cut with a bore gauge + micrometer to confirm dimensional accuracy.
- [ ] **Centring accuracy** of the VCRM spindle to bore axis: within **0.01 mm runout** for KTU practical evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Cylinder Liner Setting & Line Boring Operations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cylinder Liner
A **cylinder liner** (also called a sleeve) is a precision-engineered, hollow cylindrical component that is inserted into the cylinder block of an internal combustion engine. It provides the sliding surface against which the piston reciprocates, forming the combustion chamber wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A cylinder liner is a renewable, replaceable wear surface fitted into the engine cylinder bore to restore original bore geometry, maintain compression, and protect the parent cylinder block from wear, corrosion, and thermal damage.

**Types of Cylinder Liners (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
- **Dry Liners:** Do not contact coolant directly; pressed into the cylinder block.
- **Wet Liners:** Outer surface contacts coolant directly; sealed with O-rings at top and bottom.
- **Flanged Liners:** Have a flange for axial location (used in heavy-duty diesel engines).

### 1.2 Hydraulic Press
A **hydraulic press** is a machine tool that uses a hydraulic cylinder to generate a large compressive force for pressing, fitting, or extracting components. In engine work, it is used to install (press-fit) cylinder liners into the engine block with controlled, uniform force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law Governing Hydraulic Press:** Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. This principle allows a small input force to generate a massive output force at the press ram.

### 1.3 Line Boring
**Line boring** is a precision machining operation performed on a **horizontal line boring machine** to enlarge, true, and align existing cylindrical bores (such as cylinder bores or main bearing bores) to a common centerline with high dimensional and geometric accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Line boring is a metal cutting process where a single-point cutting tool, mounted on a rotating spindle of a line boring bar, traverses through pre-existing bores to restore true geometric form, size, and concentricity along a common axis.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Cylinder Liner as a "Sleeve in a Cup":**
Imagine a worn-out drinking glass (engine block) that has lost its smooth inner surface. Instead of throwing the glass away, you insert a thin, perfectly smooth stainless steel sleeve (cylinder liner) into it. The sleeve restores the smooth surface. The hydraulic press is like using a uniform, controlled push (like pushing a cork into a bottle) to fit this sleeve snugly.

**Analogy 2 — Line Boring as a "Doctor for a Worn-Out Pipe":**
Think of a pipe that has become oval-shaped, scratched, and slightly oversized due to wear. A line boring operation is like passing a rotating, sharp, and adjustable cutting tool through the pipe — like a lathe cutting the inside — to restore the pipe to its original perfect circular shape and exact diameter, while ensuring it is perfectly aligned with other holes on the same machine bed (e.g., the engine block).

**Analogy 3 — Hydraulic Press as a "Lever for Force Multiplication":**
A hydraulic press is like pressing down on a small syringe connected by a tube to a large syringe in reverse. A small force on the small plunger creates a large force pushing the larger plunger, allowing us to press-fit a liner with several tonnes of force using only modest hand-pump effort.

### 1.5 Standard Engine Specifications (Typical Reference Values)

| Parameter | Petrol Engine (Typical) | Diesel Engine (Heavy-Duty) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Cylinder Bore Diameter** | 70 – 100 mm | 100 – 150 mm |
| **Liner Material** | Cast Iron (Pearlitic) | Centrifugal Cast Iron |
| **Liner Wall Thickness** | 1.5 – 3.0 mm | 4.0 – 8.0 mm |
| **Interference Fit (Press)** | 0.05 – 0.10 mm | 0.08 – 0.15 mm |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 0.2 – 0.4 μm | 0.2 – 0.8 μm |
| **Bore Out-of-Roundness Limit** | ≤ 0.02 mm | ≤ 0.03 mm |
| **Bore Taper Limit** | ≤ 0.02 mm | ≤ 0.03 mm |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wear Geometry of the Cylinder Bore
During engine operation, the cylinder bore undergoes three principal forms of geometric distortion due to piston side-thrust, combustion pressure, and thermal effects:

1. **Abrasive Wear** — uniform radial material loss.
2. **Taper (Conicity)** — non-uniform wear along the bore axis, maximum at TDC (Top Dead Center) where combustion gases scour the wall.
3. **Out-of-Roundness (Ovality)** — non-uniform wear around the circumference, maximum along the thrust axis where piston side-thrust is applied.

### 2.2 Bore Measurement Theory

The cylinder bore is measured at three vertical levels and two perpendicular directions (along the thrust axis and perpendicular to it) to fully quantify its geometric condition.

**Measurement Levels:**
- **Level A:** Top of bore (just below the ring travel zone) — at TDC
- **Level B:** Middle of bore — at mid-stroke
- **Level C:** Bottom of bore — at BDC (Bottom Dead Center)

**Measurement Directions:**
- **X-X:** Along the thrust axis (front-to-rear of engine)
- **Y-Y:** Perpendicular to the thrust axis (parallel to crankshaft)

### 2.3 Cylindricity (Geometric Tolerance Concept)

**Cylindricity** is the 3D tolerance that controls how closely a feature conforms to a perfect cylinder. It is bounded by two concentric cylinders within which the actual surface must lie.

$$ \text{Cylindricity} = f(\text{Roundness}, \text{Taper}, \text{Barrel/Camber}, \text{Straightness}) $$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Hydraulic Press Force** | $F = P \times A$ | N (Newtons) | $P$ = hydraulic pressure (Pa or N/mm²), $A$ = ram area (mm²) |
| 2 | **Interference (Press-Fit)** | $i = D_{block} - D_{liner}$ | mm | Must be positive for press fit |
| 3 | **Bore Taper** | $T = D_{top} - D_{bottom}$ | mm | Measured along X-X or Y-Y |
| 4 | **Bore Out-of-Roundness (Ovality)** | $O = D_{max} - D_{min}$ at same level | mm | Measured at A, B, C levels |
| 5 | **Material Removal per Pass** | $d_{pass} = \frac{D_{final} - D_{initial}}{2 \times n_{passes}}$ | mm | Radial depth of cut |
| 6 | **Spindle Cutting Speed** | $V_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ = bore diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle RPM |
| 7 | **Spindle RPM** | $N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/min | Used to set boring bar speed |
| 8 | **Linear Feed Rate** | $f_r = f \times N$ | mm/min | $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev) |
| 9 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = f_r \times a_p \times d_{pass}$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = axial depth |
| 10 | **Cylindricity Tolerance** | Per ISO 1101 / ASME Y14.5 | μm | Typically 0.01–0.05 mm |
| 11 | **Press Force Estimate (Liner)** | $F_{press} \approx p_i \times \pi \times D \times L \times \mu$ | N | $p_i$ = interface pressure, $\mu$ = friction coeff. |
| 12 | **Surface Roughness (Ra) Target** | $Ra \leq 0.4 \ \mu m$ (honing) | μm | Final operation before assembly |
| 13 | **Hydraulic Pressure Required** | $P = \frac{F_{press}}{A_{ram}}$ | N/mm² or bar | 1 bar = 0.1 N/mm² |
| 14 | **True Bore Diameter (Avg)** | $D_{avg} = \frac{D_{max} + D_{min}}{2}$ | mm | At each measurement level |
| 15 | **Bore Wear Rate** | $WR = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta t}$ | mm/hour or μm/1000 km | Used in condition monitoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state **units** in the final answer. Marks are deducted when units are missing, especially in numerical problems involving hydraulic force, RPM, or material removal.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Engine Overhaul Workshops:** Cylinder liner replacement and line boring are the most common engine repair operations worldwide.
- **Marine & Heavy-Duty Diesel Engines:** In ships and locomotives, liners are replaced every 80,000 – 120,000 hours; line boring restores bearing housings.
- **Automotive OEMs:** New engine blocks are line-bored for main bearing saddles and camshaft bores to ensure perfect alignment.
- **Tractor & Construction Equipment:** Worn bores in agricultural machinery are routinely reclaimed by line boring, saving the cost of a new block.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydraulic Press Force for Liner Installation

**Given:**
- Cylinder liner outer diameter: $D = 100 \ \text{mm}$
- Liner length: $L = 200 \ \text{mm}$
- Coefficient of friction (cast iron on cast iron): $\mu = 0.12$
- Required interface interference pressure: $p_i = 25 \ \text{N/mm}^2$
- Hydraulic ram diameter: $d_{ram} = 80 \ \text{mm}$

**Step 1: Calculate the frictional sliding force required to press the liner into the block.**

The contact area between liner and block is approximately cylindrical:

$$ A_{contact} = \pi \times D \times L $$

$$ A_{contact} = \pi \times 100 \times 200 $$

$$ A_{contact} = 62{,}831.85 \ \text{mm}^2 $$

**Step 2: Compute the total frictional resistance force.**

The interference pressure $p_i$ creates a normal contact stress. The friction force is:

$$ F_{friction} = p_i \times A_{contact} \times \mu $$

$$ F_{friction} = 25 \times 62{,}831.85 \times 0.12 $$

$$ F_{friction} = 188{,}495.55 \ \text{N} $$

$$ F_{friction} \approx 188.5 \ \text{kN} \ (\approx 19.2 \ \text{tonnes force}) $$

**Step 3: Calculate the hydraulic pressure required from the press.**

The hydraulic ram area:

$$ A_{ram} = \frac{\pi \times d_{ram}^2}{4} $$

$$ A_{ram} = \frac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} $$

$$ A_{ram} = 5026.55 \ \text{mm}^2 $$

The hydraulic pressure needed:

$$ P_{hydraulic} = \frac{F_{friction}}{A_{ram}} $$

$$ P_{hydraulic} = \frac{188{,}495.55}{5026.55} $$

$$ P_{hydraulic} = 37.5 \ \text{N/mm}^2 $$

$$ P_{hydraulic} = 375 \ \text{bar} \ \ (\text{since } 1 \ \text{bar} = 0.1 \ \text{N/mm}^2) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A hydraulic pressure of **375 bar** is required to press-fit a 100 mm × 200 mm cast iron liner with 25 N/mm² interface pressure using an 80 mm ram. This demonstrates why a hydraulic press (and not a manual press) is mandatory — manual force generation at this level is impractical.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cutting Parameters for Line Boring

**Given:**
- Original bore diameter: $D_{initial} = 102.00 \ \text{mm}$
- Final desired bore diameter (after boring + honing allowance): $D_{final} = 102.50 \ \text{mm}$
- Honing allowance: $0.10 \ \text{mm}$ (radial, so $0.20 \ \text{mm}$ on diameter)
- Boring diameter target (before honing): $D_{boring} = 102.30 \ \text{mm}$
- Recommended cutting speed for cast iron: $V_c = 80 \ \text{m/min}$
- Recommended feed per revolution: $f = 0.15 \ \text{mm/rev}$
- Number of roughing passes: $n_{passes} = 3$

**Step 1: Calculate the radial depth of cut per pass.**

The total radial material to be removed during boring:

$$ d_{total} = \frac{D_{boring} - D_{initial}}{2} $$

$$ d_{total} = \frac{102.30 - 102.00}{2} $$

$$ d_{total} = 0.15 \ \text{mm} \ \text{(radial)} $$

Depth of cut per pass:

$$ d_{pass} = \frac{d_{total}}{n_{passes}} $$

$$ d_{pass} = \frac{0.15}{3} $$

$$ d_{pass} = 0.05 \ \text{mm/pass} $$

**Step 2: Calculate the spindle RPM (using average diameter).**

Average diameter during cutting:

$$ D_{avg} = \frac{D_{initial} + D_{boring}}{2} $$

$$ D_{avg} = \frac{102.00 + 102.30}{2} $$

$$ D_{avg} = 102.15 \ \text{mm} $$

Spindle speed:

$$ N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D_{avg}} $$

$$ N = \frac{80 \times 1000}{\pi \times 102.15} $$

$$ N = \frac{80{,}000}{320.93} $$

$$ N \approx 249.3 \ \text{rev/min} $$

Round to standard machine RPM: **N = 250 RPM**

**Step 3: Calculate the linear feed rate.**

$$ f_r = f \times N $$

$$ f_r = 0.15 \times 250 $$

$$ f_r = 37.5 \ \text{mm/min} $$

**Step 4: Calculate the Material Removal Rate (MRR).**

$$ MRR = f_r \times a_p \times d_{pass} $$

Where axial depth $a_p$ for a single pass along the bore length = $L_{cut} = 200 \ \text{mm}$ (axial length of cut):

$$ MRR = 37.5 \times 200 \times 0.05 $$

$$ MRR = 375 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Notebook Insight:** Always record $V_c$, $f$, $N$, and $d_{pass}$ in the observation table — examiners specifically check for these values in viva voce.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Wear Assessment Decision Tree

| Measured Condition | Taper (mm) | Ovality (mm) | Diagnosis | Recommended Action |
|--------------------|------------|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Within limits | $< 0.02$ | $< 0.02$ | Normal wear | De-glaze & reuse, new piston rings |
| Slight wear | $0.02 – 0.05$ | $0.02 – 0.05$ | Acceptable | Re-bore + oversize piston |
| Moderate wear | $0.05 – 0.10$ | $0.05 – 0.10$ | Out-of-spec | **Line boring + new liner** |
| Severe wear | $> 0.10$ | $> 0.10$ | Scored, damaged | **Liner replacement + line boring** |
| Cracked / scored | — | — | Catastrophic failure | Engine block replacement |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Cylinder Liner Setting using Hydraulic Press

```mermaid
    K --> L[Select Correct Liner Size]
    L --> M[Apply Anti-Seize Compound]
    M --> N[Position Liner in Bore]

    O --> P[Apply Controlled Hydraulic Pressure]
    P --> Q{Liner Fully Seated?}
    Q -->|No| P
    Q -->|Yes| R[Check Liner Projection Height]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a cylinder liner. List the two main types of cylinder liners used in IC engines.**

**Model Answer:**
A cylinder liner is a replaceable cylindrical sleeve fitted inside the cylinder block of an engine to provide a renewable wear surface for the piston.

The two main types are:
1. **Dry Liner** — Does not contact coolant; relies on interference fit and conduction through the block.
2. **Wet Liner** — Outer surface is in direct contact with the engine coolant; sealed using O-rings at the top and bottom.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the function of a horizontal line boring machine in engine reconditioning?**

**Model Answer:**
A horizontal line boring machine is used to machine the internal surfaces of cylindrical bores in engine components (cylinder bores, main bearing housings, camshaft bores) to a common centerline with high precision. It restores bore geometry (size, roundness, cylindricity), removes wear-induced taper and ovality, and ensures concentric alignment of multiple bores along a single axis. A rotating single-point cutting tool mounted on a boring bar performs the cutting action.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks on the following:
> 1. **Forgetting to mention units** in numerical answers (especially bar/Pa, mm, RPM).
> 2. **Skipping the measurement grid** (Levels A, B, C × Directions X-X, Y-Y) when describing bore measurement — examiners expect 6 readings minimum.
> 3. **Confusing taper with ovality** — Taper is along the axis (top vs bottom); Ovality is across a single cross-section.
> 4. **Not stating the role of honing** as a post-boring finishing operation.
> 5. **Writing "press" instead of "hydraulic press"** in Part A — full credit requires the qualifier "hydraulic."

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the complete procedure for **measuring the cylinder bore** of an IC engine before deciding on re-boring or line boring. Include the instruments used and the six standard measurement points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **procedure for setting (press-fitting) a wet-type cylinder liner into an engine block using a hydraulic press**. State the key parameters to be verified after installation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**1. Instruments Required:**
- **Outside Micrometer** (0–150 mm, LC = 0.01 mm) — calibration standard
- **Dial Bore Gauge** (0.01 mm LC) — for direct bore reading
- **Telescopic Gauge** — for transfer measurement (optional)

**2. Standard Six-Point Measurement Grid:**

The bore is divided into **three vertical levels** and **two perpendicular directions**:

| Level | Position | Direction 1 (X-X) | Direction 2 (Y-Y) |
|-------|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| A | Top (TDC) | $D_{A,X}$ | $D_{A,Y}$ |
| B | Middle (Mid-stroke) | $D_{B,X}$ | $D_{B,Y}$ |
| C | Bottom (BDC) | $D_{C,X}$ | $D_{C,Y}$ |

**3. Procedure:**
1. Clean the bore thoroughly; remove all carbon, oil, and debris.
2. Calibrate the dial bore gauge against the outside micrometer using a ring gauge or known reference.
3. Insert the dial bore gauge perpendicular to the bore axis at each level.
4. Take readings along thrust axis (X-X) and perpendicular axis (Y-Y) at all three levels → 6 readings total.
5. Record the maximum and minimum values.

**4. Calculations:**
- **Taper** (along bore axis) = $D_{top} - D_{bottom}$ (along X-X and Y-Y)
- **Ovality** (at a single level) = $D_{max} - D_{min}$ (at each of A, B, C)
- **Average Bore Diameter** = $\frac{D_{max} + D_{min}}{2}$

**5. Decision Criteria:**
- Taper / Ovality $< 0.02$ mm → De-glaze and reuse
- $0.02$ – $0.05$ mm → Re-bore + oversize piston
- $> 0.05$ mm → Line bore and new liner

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Hydraulic Press Setting Procedure for Wet Cylinder Liner:**

**1. Pre-Installation Checks:**
- Verify liner outer diameter and block bore diameter with micrometer.
- Calculate interference: $i = D_{block} - D_{liner} = 0.08$ – $0.15$ mm.
- Inspect O-ring grooves; install new O-rings lightly lubricated.
- Ensure liner flange is clean and free from burrs.

**2. Hydraulic Press Setup:**
- Mount engine block on the press table with the cylinder axis vertical.
- Position the liner on the block, ensuring O-rings are correctly seated.
- Place a flat pressing pad on the liner flange to distribute load evenly.
- Align the hydraulic ram coaxially with the liner axis.

**3. Pressing Operation:**
- Apply pressure in **gradual, controlled increments**.
- Monitor pressure gauge to ensure uniform pressing.
- Required pressure: $P = \frac{F_{press}}{A_{ram}}$ where $F_{press} = p_i \times \pi \times D \times L \times \mu$
- Stop when the liner flange makes full contact with the block face.

**4. Post-Installation Verification:**
- **Liner Projection Height:** Measure with depth micrometer / dial indicator. Should be $0.05$ – $0.15$ mm above deck face (per OEM spec).
- **Concentricity Check:** Use a dial indicator to verify the inner bore is concentric with the crankshaft axis.
- **O-Ring Leak Test:** Pressure-test coolant passages to confirm O-ring seal integrity.
- **No Distortion:** Verify no cracks, tilt, or binding of the liner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Parameter Summary Table (for the 7-mark answer):**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Verification Tool |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Interference Fit | 0.08 – 0.15 mm | Micrometer |
| Liner Projection | 0.05 – 0.15 mm | Depth Gauge / Dial Indicator |
| Hydraulic Pressure | 150 – 400 bar | Pressure Gauge |
| O-Ring Condition | New, lubricated | Visual + Leak Test |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a horizontal line boring machine** with a labeled block diagram. Describe the procedure for **setting the tool (boring bar cutter) to the required diameter**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a numerical example, calculate the **spindle RPM, feed rate, and material removal rate** for line boring a cylinder bore of initial diameter 100.00 mm to a final diameter of 100.40 mm in 4 roughing passes. Use $V_c = 75$ m/min and $f = 0.12$ mm/rev. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**1. Working Principle of Horizontal Line Boring Machine:**

The machine consists of:
- **Bed/Table** — supports the engine block and is adjustable.
- **Boring Spindle** — horizontal, rotates the boring bar.
- **Boring Bar** — long cylindrical shaft holding the cutting tool; passes through the workpiece bore.
- **Feed Drive** — hydraulically powered, advances the bar axially.
- **Tool Holder** — rigidly clamps the indexable insert tip on the bar.
- **Control Panel** — sets RPM, feed rate, depth of cut.

The boring bar rotates at controlled RPM. As it rotates, the cutting tool contacts the bore wall. Simultaneously, the bar is fed axially through the bore, removing material in a helical pattern and restoring the true cylindrical geometry.

**2. Tool Setting Procedure:**

1. **Zero the bar**: Set the boring bar in the spindle, mark a reference datum on the bar.
2. **Insert the tool blank** in the tool holder; lightly clamp.
3. **Set the initial tool projection** using a micrometer or preset gauge to match the desired bore radius.
4. **Set the tool tip exactly at the bore wall position** using a dial test indicator mounted on the bar.
5. **Take a "skimming cut"** of $0.02$ – $0.05$ mm and measure the resulting diameter.
6. **Adjust tool offset** to compensate for tool wear, deflection, and spring-back.
7. **Lock the tool holder** firmly; recheck diameter.
8. **Set the dial indicator (DRO)** to zero for monitoring tool position during the cut.

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- $D_{initial} = 100.00$ mm
- $D_{final} = 100.40$ mm
- $n_{passes} = 4$
- $V_c = 75$ m/min
- $f = 0.12$ mm/rev
- Axial length of cut: $a_p = L = 200$ mm

**Step 1: Radial depth of cut per pass.**

$$ d_{total} = \frac{D_{final} - D_{initial}}{2} = \frac{100.40 - 100.00}{2} = 0.20 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ d_{pass} = \frac{0.20}{4} = 0.05 \ \text{mm/pass} $$

**Step 2: Average diameter during cutting.**

$$ D_{avg} = \frac{100.00 + 100.40}{2} = 100.20 \ \text{mm} $$

**Step 3: Spindle RPM.**

$$ N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D_{avg}} = \frac{75 \times 1000}{\pi \times 100.20} $$

$$ N = \frac{75{,}000}{314.83} $$

$$ N \approx 238.2 \ \text{RPM} $$

**Step 4: Linear feed rate.**

$$ f_r = f \times N = 0.12 \times 238.2 = 28.58 \ \text{mm/min} $$

**Step 5: Material Removal Rate.**

$$ MRR = f_r \times a_p \times d_{pass} = 28.58 \times 200 \times 0.05 $$

$$ MRR = 285.8 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$

**Final Results:**
- **Radial depth of cut per pass** = $0.05$ mm
- **Spindle RPM** = $238$ RPM
- **Feed rate** = $28.58$ mm/min
- **MRR** = $285.8$ mm³/min

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use a final finishing pass of $0.02$ mm radial depth** at slightly higher feed and lower depth to achieve dimensional accuracy and better surface finish before honing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Points:**

- **Cylinder liner** = renewable cylindrical sleeve, two types: **dry** and **wet**.
- **Hydraulic press operation** is governed by **Pascal's Law** → $F = P \times A$.
- **Interference fit** for liner pressing = $0.05$ – $0.15$ mm (positive value).
- **Cylinder bore measurement** is taken at **3 levels (A, B, C) × 2 directions (X-X, Y-Y) = 6 readings minimum**.
- **Taper** = variation along bore **axis**; **Ovality** = variation in a **single cross-section**.
- **Line boring** restores **cylindricity, size, and concentricity** of worn bores.
- **Horizontal line boring machine** uses a **rotating boring bar with single-point cutting tool**.
- **Spindle cutting speed** for cast iron: $V_c = 60$ – $100$ m/min; **feed**: $0.08$ – $0.20$ mm/rev.
- **Honing** (not boring) is the **final finishing operation** for cylinder bores — gives the cross-hatch pattern to retain oil.
- **Liner projection height** must be verified after pressing (typically $0.05$ – $0.15$ mm).
- **Dial bore gauge** is the primary instrument for bore measurement; must be **calibrated against a micrometer** before use.
- **Tool setting on line boring machine** involves preset gauge / dial indicator + a **skimming cut** for final calibration.
- **Formula to remember cold:** $F = P \times A$ (Hydraulic force), $V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}$ (Cutting speed), $N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$ (RPM).
- **Final surface finish target for cylinder bore:** $Ra \leq 0.4 \ \mu m$ (achieved by honing, not by boring).
- **KTU Viva Question Bank — Most Asked:**
  1. Why is honing done after boring?
  2. Why is line boring preferred over hand scraping?
  3. What happens if interference fit is too high or too low?
  4. What is the difference between taper and ovality?
  5. Why is the boring bar made slender and stiff?
  6. What is the role of O-rings in a wet liner?

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCAUL308 Vehicle Systems Lab | KTU 2024 Scheme**

# 🔧 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SERVICING OF CLUTCH ASSEMBLY

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *A clutch is a mechanical device located between the engine and the gearbox that engages and disengages the power transmission from the engine to the driving wheels. **Servicing of a clutch assembly** is the systematic laboratory procedure of dismantling, inspecting, measuring, reconditioning, and reassembling the clutch unit to restore its designed torque-transmitting capacity, free-play specifications, and operational reliability.*

In simple KTU-board terminology, **clutch servicing** is the scheduled maintenance activity performed to:
- Detect wear in friction surfaces
- Restore correct spring tension
- Reset pedal free-play
- Replace damaged friction plates, release bearings, and pressure plate components.

---

## 1.2 Types of Clutch Assemblies Encountered in the Lab

| Type | Construction | Application in Lab |
|------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Single Plate Clutch** | One friction disc, one pressure plate | Most common in B.Tech labs (Maruti, Alto, Ambassador) |
| **Multi-Plate Clutch** | Multiple friction discs in series | Two-wheelers (motorcycles) |
| **Diaphragm Spring Clutch** | Diaphragm spring instead of coil springs | Modern cars (Swift, Verna) |
| **Centrifugal Clutch** | Weights engage with rising rpm | Scooters, mopeds |
| **Cone Clutch** | Friction on conical surfaces | Vintage/synchromesh gearboxes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The lab viva almost always asks students to identify whether the given assembly is a **single-plate coil-spring type** or a **diaphragm-spring type** before proceeding with servicing.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Clutch is a Light Switch on the Driveshaft"

Imagine standing on a balcony holding two ropes tied together.
- One rope is connected to a heavy engine (the *flywheel*).
- The other rope goes down to pull a load (the *gearbox input shaft*).
- You cannot tie them permanently — you need a way to **slowly engage** the engine's power to avoid a jerk.

**Your hand gripping both ropes = the friction surfaces of the clutch.**
- **Released** → No power flows.
- **Gradually pressed** → Power slips in smoothly.
- **Fully pressed** → Power is locked in completely.

**Servicing** is essentially checking whether your "hand grip" is still strong, clean, and aligned — or whether it has worn out and slipped.

---

## 1.4 Functions of a Clutch (Re-stated for Viva)

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Smooth engagement** of engine torque to the gearbox.
> 2. **Disengagement** of the engine from the gearbox during gear shifts.
> 3. Protection of the driveline from **shock loading** (a critical role during sudden deceleration).
> 4. Prevention of **engine stalling** when the vehicle is brought to rest in gear.

---

## 1.5 Main Components of a Clutch Assembly

The major components a KTU student must identify during dismantling:

1. **Flywheel** — bolted to the crankshaft; provides the driving friction surface.
2. **Clutch Plate / Friction Disc** — splined to the gearbox input shaft; carries the friction lining.
3. **Pressure Plate Assembly** — applies clamping force on the clutch plate.
4. **Clutch Springs (Coil type)** OR **Diaphragm Spring** — provides the clamping force.
5. **Release (Throw-out) Bearing** — pushes the pressure plate fingers to disengage.
6. **Release Fork / Lever** — converts pedal motion to linear push on the bearing.
7. **Pilot Bearing / Bushing** — supports the tip of the gearbox input shaft inside the flywheel.
8. **Clutch Cover / Housing** — encloses the entire pressure plate assembly.
9. **Operating Mechanism** — hydraulic (master + slave cylinder) or mechanical (cable/rod).

---

## 1.6 Standard KTU Viva Question Set — Definitions

> [!TIP]
> **Pedal Free-Play:** The small distance the clutch pedal must travel *before* the release bearing begins to push the pressure plate fingers. For most cars: **20 mm to 30 mm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Friction Lining Wear Limit:** When the rivet heads reach the surface of the lining, the plate is condemned. Standard new thickness ≈ **3.5 mm to 4.5 mm**; worn-out limit ≈ **1.5 mm to 2.0 mm** (rivet-head depth).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Servicing is Needed

The clutch works on the principle of **frictional energy transfer**. Over time:

1. **Friction lining wears out** → reduced grip → clutch slip.
2. **Springs lose tension** (fatigue) → reduced clamping force → slip under load.
3. **Release bearing wears** → noisy disengagement, judder.
4. **Pressure plate fingers become uneven** → uneven plate release, grabbing.
5. **Flywheel surface gets glazed / scored** → reduced friction coefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind Servicing:** Friction is non-recoverable. The molecular bond between the lining and the pressure plate surface degrades with every engagement. Servicing restores the **coefficient of friction (μ)** and the **clamping force (W)** so the torque capacity is preserved.

---

## 2.2 Governing Engineering Equations

The torque that a clutch can transmit is fundamentally given by:

$$
T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{mean}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $T$ | Torque transmitted by the clutch | N·m |
| $n$ | Number of friction surfaces (contact pairs) | dimensionless |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction between lining and flywheel | dimensionless |
| $W$ | Axial clamping force applied by pressure plate | N |
| $R_{mean}$ | Mean radius of the friction disc | m |

For a **uniform pressure distribution** assumption, the mean radius is:

$$
R_{mean} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (R_o^{3} - R_i^{3})}{3 \cdot (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}
$$

Where $R_o$ = outer radius and $R_i$ = inner radius of the friction lining.

For a **uniform wear** assumption (used in clutch design):

$$
R_{mean} = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}
$$

The **clamping force from coil springs** in a multi-spring pressure plate:

$$
W = n_s \cdot F_s
$$

Where $n_s$ = number of springs, $F_s$ = force per spring (typically **200 N to 500 N** per spring in passenger cars).

For a **diaphragm spring**, the effective axial force is given by:

$$
W_{eff} = F \cdot \cos(\alpha)
$$

Where $F$ is the spring leg force and $\alpha$ is the angle of the diaphragm fingers (typically **7° to 11°** at the engaged position).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse $W$ (clamping force) with $W$ (work done). In the clutch equation, $W$ is always the **axial clamping load in Newtons**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{mean}$ | Torque capacity of a clutch |
| 2 | $R_{mean} = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | Mean radius (uniform wear) |
| 3 | $R_{mean} = \dfrac{2(R_o^{3} - R_i^{3})}{3(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}$ | Mean radius (uniform pressure) |
| 4 | $W = n_s \cdot F_s$ | Total clamping force from springs |
| 5 | $\text{Free play} = 20 \text{ to } 30 \text{ mm}$ | Pedal free-play spec |
| 6 | $\text{Lining thickness (new)} \approx 3.5 \text{ to } 4.5 \text{ mm}$ | Inspection reference |
| 7 | $\text{Lining thickness (worn)} \approx 1.5 \text{ mm}$ | Replacement limit |
| 8 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2 \pi R_i (R_o - R_i)}$ | Max uniform pressure |
| 9 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2 \pi R_i^{2}}$ | Max uniform pressure (if inner contact) |
| 10 | $\text{Slip energy} = T \cdot \omega \cdot t_s$ | Heat generated during engagement |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters outside the lab:**
> - **Automotive OEM design:** Car manufacturers use these equations to size the clutch for the engine's peak torque.
> - **Heavy-vehicle CVT and DCT systems:** Even modern dual-clutch transmissions (DCTs) use these same principles — only the actuation is electro-hydraulic.
> - **Failure analysis:** Insurance investigators use the slip-mark patterns on the flywheel to determine if the clutch was abused.
> - **EV context:** Modern electric vehicles are eliminating clutches; this lab procedure is a fading art in BEV servicing — making it a high-value skill.

# 3. Step-by-Step Servicing Procedure (Analytical Matrix)

This section exhaustively lists every operation. The student is expected to write the procedure in this exact order in the lab record.

---

## 3.1 Tools and Instruments Required

> [!NOTE]
> - Spanner set (metric), socket set with ratchet
> - Screwdriver set (flat and Phillips)
> - Pliers, circlip pliers
> - Dial gauge / vernier caliper (for runout and thickness)
> - Feeler gauge (for free-play measurement)
> - Cleaning brush, kerosene / petrol, lint-free cloth
> - Torque wrench (for flywheel bolts)
> - Clutch alignment tool (dummy shaft / mandrel)

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Vehicle / Assembly Preparation

$$
\text{Step}_{1a}: \text{Disconnect battery negative terminal.}
$$

$$
\text{Step}_{1b}: \text{For in-vehicle servicing — raise vehicle on lift, remove gearbox.}
$$

$$
\text{Step}_{1c}: \text{Lab-bench servicing — clamp the clutch assembly in a vice softly on the housing edges only.}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Never clamp the pressure plate fingers or the friction lining surface. This will permanently deform the assembly.

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Marking for Reassembly

Before dismantling, **mark the relative position** of the pressure plate to the flywheel using a **punch mark or yellow paint** so that the original balance is preserved.

$$
\text{Mark position} \Rightarrow \text{Pressure plate} \leftrightarrow \text{Flywheel}
$$

This is critical because the assembly is **dynamically balanced** at the factory.

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Removal of the Clutch Assembly

Diaphragm-spring type removal sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step}_{3a}&: \text{Loosen the cover bolts gradually and alternately (star pattern).} \\
\text{Step}_{3b}&: \text{Do not remove one bolt fully before loosening the others.} \\
\text{Step}_{3c}&: \text{Slowly release the diaphragm spring tension.} \\
\text{Step}_{3d}&: \text{Support the pressure plate to prevent it from falling and injuring fingers.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Removing bolts in a non-alternate pattern will warp the pressure plate cover — a guaranteed mark deduction in viva.

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Component Inspection (Decision Matrix)

For **each component**, a fixed check is mandatory:

| Component | Inspection Item | Decision Criteria |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Friction Lining** | Thickness, cracks, oil contamination | Replace if thickness < **1.5 mm**, oily, or cracked |
| **Rivets** | Loose / buried | Replace plate if rivets touch contact surface |
| **Clutch Plate** | Runout (warp) | Max allowable runout = **0.5 mm** measured at outer diameter with dial gauge |
| **Pressure Plate** | Finger height, scoring on contact face | Fingers must be even in height; replace if uneven > **0.5 mm** |
| **Diaphragm Spring** | Wear at tips, cracks, fatigue | Replace if tip wear > **0.6 mm** or any cracks visible |
| **Coil Springs** | Free length, tension test | Replace as a set if any one is short / weak |
| **Release Bearing** | Smooth rotation, noise | Must rotate silently; replace if gritty or noisy |
| **Pilot Bearing** | Smooth rotation, seizure | Replace at every clutch job (preventive) |
| **Flywheel** | Scoring, cracks, hot-spots | Resurface if scored < **0.5 mm** deep; replace if cracked |

The **flywheel surface condition** is checked as follows:

$$
\text{Surface roughness} \Rightarrow \text{Use straight-edge} + \text{feel}
$$

$$
\text{Resurfacing limit} = 0.5 \text{ mm (max material removal)}
$$

If the flywheel is resurfaced too much, the **step height** between the friction ring and the ring-gear side becomes excessive, leading to clutch judder.

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Cleaning

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step}_{5a}&: \text{Dip the friction disc briefly in clean kerosene (do not soak).} \\
\text{Step}_{5b}&: \text{Use a soft brush to remove dust.} \\
\text{Step}_{5c}&: \text{Wipe dry with lint-free cloth.} \\
\text{Step}_{5d}&: \text{Clean pressure plate surface with a non-oily solvent.} \\
\text{Step}_{5e}&: \text{Do NOT use compressed air — it blows dust into the bearings.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **No oil / grease** is allowed on friction surfaces. Even fingerprint oil reduces $\mu$ by up to **30%**.

---

## 3.7 Step 6 — Reassembly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step}_{6a}&: \text{Clean flywheel surface with acetone.} \\
\text{Step}_{6b}&: \text{Insert clutch alignment tool into the pilot bearing and through the disc hub spline.} \\
\text{Step}_{6c}&: \text{Place the friction disc on the flywheel — the marked side always faces the pressure plate.} \\
\text{Step}_{6d}&: \text{Align the disc using the tool so the splined hub is concentric with the pilot bearing.} \\
\text{Step}_{6e}&: \text{Place the pressure plate assembly over the dowel pins.} \\
\text{Step}_{6f}&: \text{Tighten cover bolts finger-tight first, then progressively in a star pattern to specified torque.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Torque values (KTU-referenced typical):**

$$
T_{bolt} = 20 \text{ to } 30 \text{ N·m (passenger cars)}
$$

The **star-pattern torque tightening** sequence is critical:

$$
\text{Bolt 1} \rightarrow \text{Bolt 3} \rightarrow \text{Bolt 5} \rightarrow \text{Bolt 2} \rightarrow \text{Bolt 4} \rightarrow \text{Bolt 6}
$$

(for a 6-bolt cover).

---

## 3.8 Step 7 — Free-Play Adjustment

For **mechanical (cable/rod) actuation:**

$$
\text{Adjust the cable nut until } \text{Free Play} = 20 \text{ to } 30 \text{ mm at the pedal}
$$

For **hydraulic actuation:**

$$
\text{Adjust the push-rod at the slave cylinder until } \text{Pedal Free Play} = 20 \text{ to } 30 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why free-play matters:** Zero free-play → release bearing is always touching the fingers → premature bearing failure. Excessive free-play → clutch does not disengage fully → gear grinding.

---

## 3.9 Step 8 — Testing After Reassembly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Test}_{8a}&: \text{Engine start, idle, depress clutch, shift into 1st gear smoothly.} \\
\text{Test}_{8b}&: \text{Road test: gradual release from rest — no judder, no slip on inclines.} \\
\text{Test}_{8c}&: \text{At 3000 rpm in 4th gear, briefly apply handbrake — engine should not stall from slip.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **stall test** result indicates:
- **Engine stalls** = clutch is gripping correctly.
- **Engine revs rise without deceleration** = clutch is slipping → re-inspection needed.

---

## 3.10 Analytical Summary — Defect → Cause → Remedy

| Defect Observed | Probable Cause | Servicing Remedy |
|-----------------|----------------|------------------|
| Clutch slipping | Worn lining, weak springs, glazed flywheel | Replace lining, springs, resurface flywheel |
| Clutch grabbing | Oil on lining, uneven fingers | Clean/replace plate, replace pressure plate |
| Clutch juddering | Worn pilot bearing, warped plate, misaligned | Replace pilot, plate; re-align |
| Noise on pressing pedal | Worn release bearing | Replace release bearing |
| Gear grinding on shift | Excess free-play, no disengagement | Adjust free-play to **20–30 mm** |
| Pedal feels spongy (hydraulic) | Air in hydraulic line | Bleed the system |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Clutch Servicing

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire servicing workflow as a block-level functional architecture, satisfying the visual requirement for this lab experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Clutch Assembly] --> B[Pre-Disassembly Marking]
    B --> C[Gradual Bolt Loosening - Star Pattern]
    C --> D[Remove Pressure Plate Assembly]

    E --> F[Remove Release Bearing]
    F --> G[Remove Pilot Bearing]
    G --> H{Inspection Station}
    H --> H1[Check Friction Lining Thickness]
    H --> H2[Check Pressure Plate Fingers]
    H --> H3[Check Diaphragm Spring / Coil Springs]
    H --> H4[Check Release Bearing Rotation]
    H --> H5[Check Flywheel Surface]
    H1 --> I1{Within Spec?}
    H2 --> I2{Within Spec?}
    H3 --> I3{Within Spec?}
    H4 --> I4{Within Spec?}
    H5 --> I5{Within Spec?}
    I1 -->|No| J1[Replace Friction Disc]
    I1 -->|Yes| K1[Retain]
    I2 -->|No| J2[Replace Pressure Plate]
    I2 -->|Yes| K2[Retain]
    I3 -->|No| J3[Replace Spring Set]
    I3 -->|Yes| K3[Retain]
    I4 -->|No| J4[Replace Release Bearing]
    I4 -->|Yes| K4[Retain]
    I5 -->|No| J5[Resurface or Replace Flywheel]
    I5 -->|Yes| K5[Retain]
    J1 --> L[Cleaning Station]
    J2 --> L
    J3 --> L
    J4 --> L
    J5 --> L
    K1 --> L
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    K5 --> L
    L --> M[Reassembly Station]
    M --> N[Use Clutch Alignment Tool]
    N --> O[Tighten Bolts in Star Pattern to Torque Spec]
    O --> P[Adjust Pedal Free Play 20 to 30 mm]
    P --> Q[Functional Test and Stall Test]
    Q --> R{Test Pass?}
    R -->|Yes| S[End: Assembly Ready for Service]
    R -->|No| H
```

---

## 4.2 Component Inter-Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flywheel] -->|clamping surface| B[Friction Disc]
    B -->|splined hub| C[Gearbox Input Shaft]

    E[Diaphragm Spring OR Coil Springs] -->|provides force| D

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**List any six components of a single-plate clutch assembly and state the function of the release bearing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Flywheel** — provides the rotating driving surface and mounts to the engine crankshaft.
> 2. **Clutch Plate (Friction Disc)** — transmits torque to the gearbox input shaft via splined hub.
> 3. **Pressure Plate** — applies the clamping force on the friction disc.
> 4. **Clutch Springs** (coil or diaphragm) — provide the clamping force to keep the clutch engaged.
> 5. **Release Bearing (Throw-out Bearing)** — **translates the axial push from the release fork into a force on the pressure plate fingers, thereby disengaging the clutch.**
> 6. **Pilot Bearing** — supports the tip of the gearbox input shaft inside the flywheel.
> 7. **Clutch Cover** — encloses and protects the pressure plate assembly.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**What is clutch pedal free-play? State its typical range for a passenger car and explain what happens if it is set to zero.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedal free-play** is the small amount of unused travel of the clutch pedal *before* the release bearing begins to push against the pressure plate fingers.
>
> - **Typical range** for a passenger car: **20 mm to 30 mm**.
> - **If set to zero:** The release bearing is in continuous contact with the diaphragm spring fingers. This causes premature wear, noise, and eventual seizure of the bearing — leading to catastrophic clutch failure.
> - **If excessive:** The clutch will not disengage fully, leading to gear grinding and difficulty in shifting.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe, with the help of a labeled block diagram, the **step-by-step procedure for dismantling, inspecting, and reassembling a single-plate diaphragm-spring clutch assembly** used in a passenger car. Mention the inspection criteria for each component. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A single-plate clutch has an effective friction disc with outer radius $R_o = 150$ mm and inner radius $R_i = 75$ mm. The axial clamping force is $W = 3500$ N and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.32$. The number of friction surfaces is $n = 2$. Calculate:
  (i) Mean radius assuming uniform wear.
  (ii) Torque transmitted by the clutch. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Preparation:** Clamp the clutch assembly on a bench vice using the housing flange (avoid fingers and lining).
>
> **Step 2 — Marking:** Mark the relative position of pressure plate to flywheel using a punch mark to preserve balance.
>
> **Step 3 — Loosening Bolts:** Loosen the cover bolts gradually in a **star pattern** to relieve spring tension evenly.
>
> **Step 4 — Dismantling:** Remove the pressure plate, friction disc, release bearing, and pilot bearing in sequence. Label each part.
>
> **Step 5 — Inspection:**
> - **Friction disc:** Check thickness (replace if < 1.5 mm), rivet condition, surface cracks.
> - **Pressure plate:** Check finger height uniformity (max 0.5 mm variation), contact face scoring.
> - **Diaphragm spring:** Check for cracks, tip wear (> 0.6 mm = replace).
> - **Release bearing:** Rotate by hand — must be silent and smooth. Replace if gritty.
> - **Pilot bearing:** Replace preventively.
> - **Flywheel:** Check for hot-spots, cracks, scoring.
>
> **Step 6 — Cleaning:** Clean with kerosene and lint-free cloth. **No oil on friction surfaces.**
>
> **Step 7 — Reassembly:** Use a clutch alignment tool through the disc hub. Tighten bolts in star pattern to specified torque (≈ 20–30 N·m).
>
> **Step 8 — Free-Play Adjustment:** Adjust to 20–30 mm.
>
> **Step 9 — Testing:** Perform stall test or road test.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - $R_o = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
> - $R_i = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
> - $W = 3500$ N
> - $\mu = 0.32$
> - $n = 2$
>
> **Step 1 — Mean radius (uniform wear):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{mean} &= \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{0.150 + 0.075}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{0.225}{2} \\
&= 0.1125 \text{ m} = 112.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 2 — Torque transmitted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{mean} \\
&= 2 \times 0.32 \times 3500 \times 0.1125 \\
&= 2 \times 0.32 \times 393.75 \\
&= 2 \times 126.0 \\
&= 252.0 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $R_{mean} = 112.5$ mm; $T = 252$ N·m.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **common defects observed in a clutch assembly** during servicing. For each defect, state its **cause** and the **remedy** adopted. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** During a clutch inspection, a friction disc is found to have an outer radius of 120 mm and inner radius of 60 mm. The lining has worn down to a thickness of 1.4 mm. The spring force per spring is 250 N and there are 6 springs. If $\mu = 0.30$ and $n = 2$, determine whether the clutch is safe to reuse. Justify with calculations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Defect 1 — Clutch Slip**
> - *Cause:* Worn friction lining, weak or broken springs, oil contamination.
> - *Remedy:* Replace friction plate; replace spring set; clean and identify oil source.
>
> **Defect 2 — Clutch Grab / Judder**
> - *Cause:* Oil on lining, uneven pressure plate fingers, worn pilot bearing.
> - *Remedy:* Clean/replace lining, replace pressure plate, replace pilot bearing.
>
> **Defect 3 — Noise on Pedal Press**
> - *Cause:* Worn release bearing.
> - *Remedy:* Replace release bearing.
>
> **Defect 4 — Gear Grinding**
> - *Cause:* Excessive pedal free-play, no full disengagement.
> - *Remedy:* Adjust free-play to 20–30 mm.
>
> **Defect 5 — Pedal Sponginess (Hydraulic)**
> - *Cause:* Air in hydraulic line, master cylinder leak.
> - *Remedy:* Bleed system, replace seals.
>
> **Defect 6 — Clutch Drag (Will Not Disengage)**
> - *Cause:* Misaligned disc, warped pressure plate, no free-play.
> - *Remedy:* Realign using tool, replace warped plate, reset free-play.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - $R_o = 120$ mm $= 0.120$ m
> - $R_i = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m
> - Lining thickness = 1.4 mm (this is **below** the 1.5 mm rejection limit — so plate should be **condemned**).
> - $F_s = 250$ N, $n_s = 6$
> - $\mu = 0.30$, $n = 2$
>
> **Step 1 — Mean radius (uniform wear):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{mean} &= \dfrac{0.120 + 0.060}{2} = 0.090 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 2 — Total clamping force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= n_s \cdot F_s = 6 \times 250 = 1500 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 — Torque capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{mean} \\
&= 2 \times 0.30 \times 1500 \times 0.090 \\
&= 2 \times 0.30 \times 135.0 \\
&= 2 \times 40.5 \\
&= 81.0 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4 — Decision:**
> - Lining thickness = **1.4 mm** is **below** the prescribed **1.5 mm** rejection limit.
> - **Conclusion:** The plate is **not safe to reuse**. It must be **replaced**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this question:**
> 1. **Writing procedure in wrong order** — KTU examiners look for *preparation → marking → loosening pattern → inspection → cleaning → reassembly → testing*. Reversing the order costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the star-pattern bolt loosening** — This is a 2-mark question in most valuation keys.
> 3. **Not stating inspection limits numerically** (1.5 mm lining, 0.5 mm runout, 0.6 mm spring tip wear) — students who say "replace if worn" without numbers lose marks.
> 4. **In torque problems, mixing up uniform wear and uniform pressure formulas** — both forms must be known. Mixing them up = full mark loss on that part.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in numerical problems — silent but fatal error.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A clutch is a **friction device** that engages/disengages engine torque to the gearbox.
- ✅ Servicing involves **dismantling → inspection → cleaning → replacement → reassembly → adjustment → testing**.
- ✅ Always **mark the assembly** before dismantling to preserve balance.
- ✅ Loosen and tighten cover bolts in a **star pattern**.
- ✅ Use a **clutch alignment tool** during reassembly.
- ✅ Pedal **free-play = 20–30 mm** for passenger cars.
- ✅ Friction lining rejection limit: **thickness < 1.5 mm** OR **oil contamination** OR **rivets touching surface**.
- ✅ Clutch plate **runout limit = 0.5 mm** (measured at outer rim with dial gauge).
- ✅ Diaphragm spring **tip wear limit = 0.6 mm**.
- ✅ Cover bolt **torque = 20–30 N·m** (typical passenger car).
- ✅ Always **replace the pilot bearing and release bearing** as preventive maintenance during every clutch job.
- ✅ **Never use oil or grease** on friction surfaces.
- ✅ **Never use compressed air** to clean — it embeds dust into bearings.
- ✅ The fundamental torque equation is $T = n \mu W R_{mean}$.
- ✅ Uniform wear gives $R_{mean} = (R_o + R_i)/2$.
- ✅ Uniform pressure gives $R_{mean} = 2(R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}) / [3(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})]$.
- ✅ A **stall test** (4th gear, 3000 rpm, handbrake pulled) confirms whether the clutch is gripping or slipping.
- ✅ The **two friction surfaces** ($n = 2$) in a single-plate clutch are: (1) flywheel-disc face, (2) pressure plate-disc face.
- ✅ In a multi-plate clutch, $n$ is greater than 2 (e.g., $n = 4$ for two driven discs in a motorcycle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dismantling of differential assembly, servicing, backlash adjustments, check for drive axis ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dismantling of differential assembly, servicing, backlash adjustments, check for drive axle ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **differential assembly** is a precision-engineered, gear-based power transmission sub-system mounted within the rear-axle housing (or transaxle housing in front-wheel-drive layouts) of an automobile. Its primary engineering function is threefold:

1. **Power Transmission:** Conveys engine torque from the propeller shaft / drive shaft to the crown wheel (ring gear), and subsequently to the rear (or drive) wheels.
2. **Speed Differentiation:** Permits the inner and outer drive wheels to rotate at **different angular velocities** during cornering, thereby eliminating tire skid and enabling smooth, stable turning.
3. **Torque Division:** Distributes drive torque equally (in the straight-ahead case) or in a **2:1 ratio** (during cornering) between the two axle shafts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drive Axle Ratio (Final Drive Ratio / Drive Ratio):** The numerical ratio between the number of teeth on the crown wheel ($T_C$) and the number of teeth on the pinion ($T_P$). It is expressed as **$R_d = T_C / T_P$** and typically lies between **2.5:1 and 4.5:1** for passenger cars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Backlash (Tooth Clearance):** The small intentional gap between the meshing tooth flanks of the crown wheel and the pinion. Standard backlash for hypoid/bevel gears ranges between **0.10 mm to 0.25 mm**, ensuring proper lubrication, thermal expansion accommodation, and prevention of tooth-jamming under load.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder)

Imagine two children sitting on opposite ends of a see-saw, and a third child standing at the center pivot. The see-saw represents the **differential cross (spider)**, the two seated children represent the **side gears**, and the center child represents the **pinion gear** turning the see-saw.

- When the see-saw rotates *without* tilting (straight driving), both ends move up and down **together** — both wheels rotate at the **same speed**.
- When the see-saw *tilts* while rotating (cornering), one end moves up while the other moves down — the wheels rotate at **different speeds**, just like the inner wheel slows down and the outer wheel speeds up during a turn.
- The **crown wheel and pinion (CWP)** act like a *gear-reduction* stage that gives the vehicle its final mechanical advantage and helps multiply engine torque for pulling power.

A practical industry example: a tractor pulling a heavy plough. The high drive ratio (**3.7:1 or 4.1:1**) gives huge torque at the wheels, but the differential still allows the tractor to turn in tight field boundaries without tearing up the soil.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Drive Axle Ratio (Passenger car)** | **3.0:1 – 4.1:1** | Torque multiplication |
| **Drive Axle Ratio (Truck/Heavy)** | **4.5:1 – 7.2:1** | High-torque low-speed applications |
| **Crown wheel-backlash (Hypoid)** | **0.10 – 0.25 mm** | Allows thermal expansion |
| **Pinion preload torque** | **1.5 – 3.5 Nm** | Bearing setting torque |
| **Side-gear backlash** | **0.05 – 0.40 mm** | Permits speed differentiation |
| **Differential case runout** | **< 0.05 mm** | Concentricity limit |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the Differential Assembly (Logical Breakdown)

The differential assembly, although compact, is composed of several highly-stressed, precision-machined components. The following structured breakdown explains **WHY** each part exists and **HOW** it functions:

- **Crown Wheel (Ring Gear):**
  - *Why:* Acts as the final reduction stage and torque multiplier.
  - *How:* Driven directly by the pinion; its larger diameter multiplies the torque while reducing the rotational speed. Bolted rigidly to the differential carrier.

- **Pinion (Drive Pinion):**
  - *Why:* Receives input torque from the propeller shaft via a universal joint.
  - *How:* Mounted on tapered roller bearings (front and rear), engages the crown wheel at a right angle (90°). On hypoid axles, the pinion sits slightly *below* the crown-wheel centerline.

- **Differential Case (Carrier):**
  - *Why:* Houses the entire differential gear set and rotates as a single rigid body when both wheels turn at the same speed.
  - *How:* Driven by the crown wheel; carries the cross (spider), side gears, and thrust washers.

- **Differential Cross / Spider:**
  - *Why:* Permits the side gears to rotate independently of the case while still being supported.
  - *How:* Two perpendicular axes, each carrying a planet (pinion) gear that meshes with both side gears.

- **Side Gears (Sun Gears):**
  - *Why:* Connect the differential motion directly to each axle shaft.
  - *How:* Splined internally to the axle half-shafts; meshed with the differential pinions on the cross.

- **Thrust Washers & Bearings:**
  - *Why:* Reduce friction, support axial loads, and prevent gear-end wear.
  - *How:* Fitted behind the side gears and beneath the cross trunnions.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Why the Differential Works

The **fundamental rule** of the differential (Willis Equation, 1857) states:

$$N_1 + N_2 = 2 \cdot N_c$$

where $N_1$ and $N_2$ are the rotational speeds (in rpm) of the **left and right side gears**, and $N_c$ is the rotational speed of the **differential case (carrier)**. This means that *the average speed of the two output shafts always equals the speed of the carrier*. If one wheel slows, the other automatically speeds up by the same amount.

> [!NOTE]
> When the vehicle moves in a **straight line**: the cross does not rotate relative to the case, so $N_1 = N_2 = N_c$.
> When **cornering**: the cross rotates, slowing the inner wheel ($N_1 < N_c$) and speeding up the outer wheel ($N_2 > N_c$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Validity / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_d = \dfrac{T_C}{T_P}$ | Drive axle ratio | Dimensionless (e.g., 3.73:1) | Always for standard open differential |
| 2 | $N_p \cdot T_P = N_c \cdot T_C$ | Power balance across mesh | rpm × teeth | For meshing gears (no slip) |
| 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{N_p}{R_d}$ | Carrier speed from pinion speed | rpm | Output/input relation |
| 4 | $N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$ | Willis differential equation | rpm | Always true, both axle shafts |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{2}$ | Mean backlash (crown–pinion) | mm | Use feeler gauge on 4 positions |
| 6 | $\eta_{fd} = \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Final drive efficiency | % | Hypoid ≈ 0.94 – 0.97 |
| 7 | $T_{wheel} = T_{engine} \cdot G_t \cdot R_d \cdot \eta$ | Total torque to wheel | Nm | With transmission gear ratio $G_t$ |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{2 \pi r_w N_c}{60 R_d}$ | Vehicle speed from carrier rpm | m/s | Kinematic relation |
| 9 | $T_{hypoid} = T_{spiral} \cdot \cos\psi$ | Hypoid vs. spiral bevel torque | Nm | $\psi$ = offset angle |
| 10 | $\Delta N = N_2 - N_1$ | Speed difference during turn | rpm | Used in cornering analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU board exams:** The most frequently tested formula is **$R_d = T_C / T_P$** combined with **$N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$**. Memorize these in symbolic form and remember to include the **efficiency factor** $\eta$ for any practical torque calculation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Off-road vehicles (e.g., Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler):** Use **4.10:1 or 4.88:1** drive ratios for extreme low-end torque, allowing the vehicle to crawl over rocks at walking pace.
- **Highway sedans (e.g., Honda City, Maruti Suzuki Dzire):** Use **3.7:1 to 4.1:1** for balanced fuel economy and acceleration.
- **Commercial trucks (e.g., Tata LPT series):** Use **5.0:1 to 7.2:1** with tandem rear axles for hauling 20+ tonne payloads.
- **Electric vehicles (e.g., Tesla Model 3):** Use a single-speed reduction ratio of approximately **9:1** because electric motors have flat torque curves and do not need a multi-gear transmission.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drive Axle Ratio

The **drive axle ratio** is the ratio of the number of rotations of the propeller shaft (input) to the number of rotations of the crown wheel (output). The following derivation is exhaustive, with each logical step explained in plain text.

**Step 1 — Identify the meshing components.**
The propeller shaft drives the **pinion** (with $T_P$ teeth), which meshes with the **crown wheel** (with $T_C$ teeth).

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental meshing-gear relation.**
For two meshing gears, the product of speed and number of teeth is conserved (the linear pitch at the pitch circle must be identical for both gears).

$$N_P \cdot T_P = N_C \cdot T_C$$

This is the standard meshing equation (also called the *gear velocity equation*), where $N_P$ is pinion speed and $N_C$ is crown wheel speed.

**Step 3 — Define the drive axle ratio.**
The drive axle ratio $R_d$ is defined as the ratio of crown-wheel teeth to pinion teeth (since the crown wheel turns *slower* than the pinion, this gives a number greater than 1, indicating torque multiplication).

$$R_d = \dfrac{T_C}{T_P}$$

**Step 4 — Express the speed relationship in terms of $R_d$.**
Rearranging the meshing equation for $N_C$:

$$N_C = \dfrac{N_P \cdot T_P}{T_C}$$

Substitute $R_d = T_C / T_P$ into the denominator, which means $T_C = R_d \cdot T_P$:

$$N_C = \dfrac{N_P \cdot T_P}{R_d \cdot T_P}$$

The $T_P$ terms cancel algebraically:

$$N_C = \dfrac{N_P}{R_d}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the torque relationship.**
Power is conserved (less frictional losses), so $P_{in} = P_{out} \cdot \eta_{fd}$ where $\eta_{fd}$ is the final-drive efficiency. Since $P = 2 \pi N T / 60$:

$$N_P \cdot T_{P,in} = \dfrac{N_C \cdot T_{C,out}}{\eta_{fd}}$$

Combining with the speed relation $N_C = N_P / R_d$:

$$\dfrac{T_{C,out}}{T_{P,in}} = R_d \cdot \eta_{fd}$$

This shows that the **torque is multiplied by the drive ratio** (and slightly reduced by efficiency losses), confirming the engineering purpose of the CWP stage.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Willis Equation (Speed Relationship)

The Willis equation is a cornerstone of differential theory. The following is a step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1 — Consider the gear train.**
The differential cross (carrier) carries **two planet pinions**. Each planet meshes with both side gears. Let the planet pinion have $T_{pl}$ teeth and the side gears have $T_s$ teeth.

**Step 2 — Apply the relative-velocity (Willis) approach.**
When the carrier is held stationary ($N_c = 0$) and the carrier-driven case is removed, the planetary gear train reduces to a simple external-mesh pair. The relative speeds (with respect to the carrier) are:

$$N_1 - N_c = -(N_2 - N_c) \cdot \dfrac{T_s}{T_{pl}} \cdot (-1)^{m}$$

For an external mesh between planet and side gear, $m = 1$ (one external mesh), so the sign becomes:

$$N_1 - N_c = -\left(\dfrac{T_s}{T_{pl}}\right) \cdot \left(N_2 - N_c\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using the equal-teeth property.**
In a standard automotive differential, both side gears are **identical** ($T_s$ same) and the planet pinions are **identical** ($T_{pl}$ same). Therefore $T_s / T_{pl} = 1$:

$$N_1 - N_c = -\left(N_2 - N_c\right)$$

**Step 4 — Expand and solve.**

$$N_1 - N_c = -N_2 + N_c$$

$$N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$$

This is the **Willis equation**, valid for all operating conditions of an open differential.

**Step 5 — Apply cornering boundary conditions.**
During a sharp turn, the inner wheel essentially stops ($N_1 = 0$). Substituting:

$$0 + N_2 = 2 N_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_2 = 2 N_c$$

This means the **outer wheel rotates twice as fast as the carrier** during a tight turn — the classic "2:1 speed-doubling" behaviour of an open differential.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Mean Backlash from Multiple Measurements

Crown-wheel backlash varies around the gear circumference due to runout, manufacturing tolerance, and case deflection. To get a representative value, **measure backlash at four equally-spaced positions** (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) using a dial-indicator and a locking pin on the crown wheel.

**Step 1 — Record four measurements.**
Let the four measured backlash values (in mm) be $B_1$, $B_2$, $B_3$, $B_4$.

**Step 2 — Compute the arithmetic mean.**

$$B_{mean} = \dfrac{B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the backlash variation (runout indicator).**

$$\Delta B = B_{max} - B_{min}$$

Acceptable variation for a properly-set differential:

$$\Delta B \leq 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply adjustment criteria (typical KTU bench practice).**
If $B_{mean}$ is *less* than the specification, **increase** the side-gear thrust-washer thickness or **decrease** the shim pack on the pinion head bearing.
