If $B_{mean}$ is *greater* than the specification, perform the reverse action. Always re-tighten the pinion nut to the correct preload torque after adjustment.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Problem:** A vehicle has a pinion with 9 teeth and a crown wheel with 41 teeth. The engine delivers 240 Nm of torque at 3500 rpm through a first-gear transmission ratio of 3.42:1. The final-drive efficiency is 0.95. Calculate: (a) the drive axle ratio, (b) the crown-wheel speed, and (c) the torque at each drive wheel (assume straight-line motion).

**Step 1 — Drive axle ratio.**

$$R_d = \dfrac{T_C}{T_P} = \dfrac{41}{9} = 4.556 \approx 4.56:1$$

**Step 2 — Pinion speed (input to final drive).**
The transmission multiplies torque, not speed, in the *same* ratio. Therefore:

$$N_P = N_{engine} \cdot G_t = 3500 \times 3.42 = 11{,}970 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Crown-wheel speed (carrier speed).**

$$N_C = \dfrac{N_P}{R_d} = \dfrac{11{,}970}{4.556} = 2628 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Torque at the crown wheel (after efficiency loss).**

$$T_C = T_P \cdot R_d \cdot \eta_{fd} = 240 \times 4.556 \times 0.95 = 1038.7 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 5 — Torque at each drive wheel (straight-line case).**
In straight motion, the differential splits the torque equally between the two side gears. Since both side gears are identical and the planet pinions are idling:

$$T_{wheel} = \dfrac{T_C}{2} = \dfrac{1038.7}{2} = 519.4 \text{ Nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-exam tip:** Always state the assumption "straight-line motion" or "open differential in straight-ahead running" explicitly. Without this, the examiner cannot award full marks for the torque-division step.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Fault Diagnosis by Symptom

| Observed Symptom | Probable Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Humming / whining noise at constant speed** | Incorrect crown-pinion backlash (too tight or too loose) | Re-shim pinion bearings; re-set backlash to 0.15 – 0.20 mm |
| **Clunk on gear engagement** | Excessive side-gear backlash; worn thrust washers | Replace thrust washers; check side-gear splines |
| **Tire scuffing on turns** | Differential locked or cross seized | Strip differential; inspect cross trunnions; replace cross |
| **Oil leak at axle shaft** | Worn oil seal at axle housing | Replace oil seal; check seal runout |
| **Vibration at high speed** | Worn universal joint / bent propeller shaft | Check UJ; balance propeller shaft |
| **Differential oil becomes milky** | Water ingress from failed breather / seal | Replace breather; flush with fresh 90-grade gear oil |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Differential Assembly Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the power-flow and functional relationships inside a rear-axle differential assembly, including the major sub-components, lubrication paths, and adjustment points.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Propeller Shaft Input] --> B[Universal Joint Yoke]
    B --> C[Pinion Shaft Assembly]
    C --> D[Tapered Roller Bearing Front]
    C --> E[Tapered Roller Bearing Rear]
    C --> F[Pinion Gear 9 to 13 Teeth]
    F --> G[Crown Wheel Ring Gear 39 to 49 Teeth]
    G --> H[Differential Case Carrier]
    H --> I[Differential Cross Spider]
    I --> J[Planet Pinion Gears Two Pieces]
    J --> K[Side Gear Left]
    J --> L[Side Gear Right]
    K --> M[Left Axle Half Shaft]
    L --> N[Right Axle Half Shaft]
    M --> O[Left Drive Wheel]
    N --> P[Right Drive Wheel]
    Q[Thrust Washer Pack] --> H
    R[Pinion Shim Pack] --> C
    S[Hypoid Gear Oil 90 Grade] -.->|Lubrication| F
    S -.->|Lubrication| G
    S -.->|Lubrication| J
    T[Breather Vent] --> H
    U[Drain Plug] --> H
    V[Filler Plug] --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, **solid arrows** represent mechanical power flow, **dotted arrows** represent lubrication flow, and the **adjustment components** (thrust washers, shim pack) are shown as separate input nodes since they do not transmit power but control clearances.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Chart — Dismantling Procedure (Lab Sequence)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Mount Axle on Stand] --> S2[Step 2 Drain Hypoid Oil]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Remove Rear Cover Bolts]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Tap Cover and Lift Off]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Mark Crown Wheel to Case Position]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Remove Crown Wheel Bolts]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Lift Crown Wheel Off Case]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Lift Out Complete Differential Case]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Hold Cross with Soft Jaws]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Drive Out Cross Roll Pin]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Remove Cross and Planet Pinions]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Remove Side Gears and Thrust Washers]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13 Inspect All Components]
    S13 --> S14{Parts Serviceable}
    S14 -->|Yes| S15[Clean and Reassemble]
    S14 -->|No| S16[Replace Worn Components]
    S15 --> S17[Set Backlash and Reassemble]
    S16 --> S17
    S17 --> S18[Refill with 90 Grade Gear Oil]
    S18 --> S19[Road Test for Noise and Leaks]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bevel Gear Geometry and Tooth Contact

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Engine Torque Input] --> B[Transmission Output]
    B --> C[Propeller Shaft]
    C --> D[Hypoid Pinion Below Centerline]
    D --> E[Sliding Contact Zone]
    E --> F[Crown Wheel Above Pinion]
    F --> G[Differential Case]
    G --> H[Cross Spider]
    H --> I[Planet Pinion Mesh]
    I --> J[Side Gear Left]
    I --> K[Side Gear Right]
    L[Hypoid Offset Angle 7 to 12 deg] --> D
    M[Pressure Angle 20 to 22.5 deg] --> F
    N[Tooth Contact Pattern Check] --> F
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Backlash Measurement Set-up

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Crown Wheel Locked by Locking Pin] --> B[Dial Indicator Plunger]
    B --> C[Perpendicular to Tooth Flank]
    C --> D[Measure at Four Positions 0 90 180 270]
    D --> E[Compute Mean Backlash]
    E --> F{Within 0.10 to 0.25 mm}
    F -->|No| G[Adjust Pinion Shims or Side Gear Washers]
    G --> D
    F -->|Yes| H[Reassemble and Test]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Tip for KTU Practical Exam:** Always lock the crown wheel using the special locking tool (drilled bolt) before attempting to rotate it. Never apply force to the side gears or axle shafts during measurement, as this will give a falsely high backlash reading.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define "Backlash" in a differential assembly. Mention the standard range of backlash for a hypoid gear pair and explain why backlash must be provided.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (1 mark):* Backlash is the small clearance or gap between the meshing tooth flanks of the crown wheel and the pinion gear, measured along the common tangent at the pitch circle.
- *Standard range (1 mark):* For hypoid bevel gears used in automotive differentials, the standard backlash is **0.10 mm to 0.25 mm**, varying slightly with the application.
- *Why it must be provided (1 mark):* Backlash is essential to (i) prevent tooth jamming due to thermal expansion of the gears under load, (ii) provide space for the lubricating oil film to form, and (iii) accommodate minor manufacturing and assembly tolerances without causing gear-tooth binding.

---

### Question A2: What is meant by "Drive Axle Ratio"? How is it determined experimentally in a vehicle systems lab?

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (1 mark):* The drive axle ratio (also called the final drive ratio) is the ratio of the number of teeth on the crown wheel to the number of teeth on the pinion, $R_d = T_C / T_P$.
- *Practical determination (2 marks):* In the lab, the propeller shaft is rotated a known number of revolutions (say, 10) using a manual handle or a hand drill, and the number of rotations of the crown wheel (and hence the differential case) is counted. The drive axle ratio is calculated as:
  $$R_d = \dfrac{\text{Number of propeller shaft rotations}}{\text{Number of crown wheel rotations}}$$
  This can also be cross-verified by physical tooth counting on the disassembled crown wheel and pinion gears.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice (A): Differential Dismantling and Servicing

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat labelled sketch, the step-by-step procedure for **dismantling a rear-axle differential assembly** in a vehicle systems laboratory. List the tools and safety precautions required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Tools required (1 mark):*
- Torque wrench, socket set (metric), soft-jaw bench vice, brass drift and mallet, dial indicator (0.01 mm least count), feeler gauge set (0.05 – 1.0 mm), circlip pliers, bearing puller, pinion pre-load sleeve, crown-wheel locking tool, and a clean parts tray.

*Step-by-step dismantling procedure (5 marks):*
1. **Mount the axle housing** securely on the rotating workshop stand with the rear cover facing up.
2. **Drain the hypoid gear oil** by removing the drain plug into a designated oil tray; dispose of used oil as per workshop norms.
3. **Mark the relative position** of the crown wheel to the differential case using a paint marker or scriber — this is critical for reassembly with the same factory-set pattern.
4. **Remove the rear cover bolts** in a cross-pattern (opposite bolts first) to avoid warping the cover; tap gently with a rubber mallet to break the gasket seal.
5. **Lift off the rear cover** carefully; do not damage the gasket surface.
6. **Remove the crown wheel bolts** and lift the crown wheel off the differential case, supporting it with both hands to avoid dropping.
7. **Withdraw the complete differential case** assembly from the housing using the lifting handle or brass drift.
8. **Hold the cross in soft jaws** of a bench vice and drive out the cross roll pin with a suitable drift.
9. **Remove the cross and the two planet pinions**, noting their orientation.
10. **Remove the side gears and thrust washers**, keeping all shims and washers in labelled bags.

*Safety precautions (1 mark):*
- Wear safety goggles and gloves at all times.
- Use a parts tray to avoid losing small components.
- Never use excessive force — if a part resists, investigate the cause.

**(b)** Explain the **inspection and servicing procedure** for the dismantled components. What are the rejection criteria for a hypoid crown wheel and pinion? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Inspection procedure (4 marks):*
1. **Visual inspection** of every gear tooth for pitting, spalling, chipping, scoring, or heat-cracks. Any of these requires replacement.
2. **Bearing inspection** — rotate each tapered roller bearing by hand; roughness, brinelling, or discoloration indicates replacement.
3. **Thrust-washer thickness** measured with a micrometer; replace if worn beyond 0.05 mm of the original thickness.
4. **Side-gear spline wear** checked with a new half-shaft — any axial play beyond 0.10 mm requires replacement.
5. **Case runout** measured on a surface plate with a dial indicator; maximum permissible runout is 0.05 mm.
6. **Hypoid oil** sampled and tested for metal particles; presence of brass-coloured sludge indicates impending failure.

*Rejection criteria for CWP (3 marks):*
- **Pinion:** more than 5% of teeth chipped or broken; tooth-flank pitting exceeding 0.5 mm depth; any crack on the pinion head.
- **Crown wheel:** tooth-spacing error > 0.04 mm; runout > 0.05 mm; pitted area exceeding 15% of the total tooth-flank area; any tooth broken.
- **Bearings:** roughness, discoloration, axial play > 0.05 mm, or any brinelling marks.
- **Case and cross:** cracks, worn trunnion bores, or deformed splines.

---

### Question B1 — Choice (B): Backlash Adjustment and Drive Axle Ratio

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is **backlash** in a crown wheel and pinion pair? Describe the **method of measuring and adjusting backlash** in a differential assembly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Definition and significance (2 marks):*
Backlash is the clearance between the non-working flanks of the meshing teeth of the crown wheel and pinion. It is necessary to prevent gear-tooth jamming, to accommodate thermal expansion, and to allow an oil film to form at the contact zone. The recommended backlash for a hypoid pair in a passenger car differential is **0.10 – 0.25 mm**.

*Measurement method (3 marks):*
1. Reassemble the crown wheel and pinion into the axle housing; tighten all bearing nuts to the specified preload torque.
2. Mount a **dial indicator** on the housing with its plunger perpendicular to a crown-wheel tooth flank.
3. Lock the crown wheel using the locking pin provided in the axle housing.
4. **Rock the pinion** back and forth by hand; the dial indicator shows the backlash.
5. **Measure at four equally-spaced positions** (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°).
6. Compute the **mean backlash** as $B = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4)/4$ and the **variation** as $\Delta B = B_{max} - B_{min}$.

*Adjustment method (2 marks):*
- To **increase** backlash: add shims on the pinion head bearing or move the pinion away from the crown-wheel centre.
- To **decrease** backlash: remove shims from the pinion head bearing or move the pinion closer.
- Final adjustment is verified by checking the **tooth-contact pattern** using marking compound; the pattern should be centred on the tooth, slightly towards the toe on the drive side and towards the heel on the coast side.

**(b)** Define **drive axle ratio**. A vehicle has a pinion with 11 teeth and a crown wheel with 43 teeth. The engine delivers 220 Nm at 3000 rpm in third gear ($G_t = 1.42:1$). The final-drive efficiency is 0.96. Calculate: (i) drive axle ratio, (ii) crown-wheel speed, (iii) torque at each drive wheel in straight motion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Definitions (1 mark):* Drive axle ratio is the ratio of crown-wheel teeth to pinion teeth.

*(i) Drive axle ratio (1 mark):*

$$R_d = \dfrac{T_C}{T_P} = \dfrac{43}{11} = 3.91:1$$

*(ii) Crown-wheel speed (2 marks):*
Pinion speed = engine speed × transmission gear ratio:
$$N_P = 3000 \times 1.42 = 4260 \text{ rpm}$$
Crown-wheel speed:
$$N_C = \dfrac{N_P}{R_d} = \dfrac{4260}{3.91} = 1089.5 \text{ rpm}$$

*(iii) Torque at each drive wheel in straight motion (3 marks):*
Crown-wheel (case) torque:
$$T_C = T_P \times R_d \times \eta_{fd} = 220 \times 3.91 \times 0.96 = 826.8 \text{ Nm}$$
In straight motion, the differential splits the torque equally:
$$T_{wheel} = \dfrac{T_C}{2} = \dfrac{826.8}{2} = 413.4 \text{ Nm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor ($\eta_{fd}$):** Many students write $T_C = T_P \times R_d$ without including $\eta_{fd}$. The hypoid gear mesh is a sliding-contact system, so efficiency is typically 0.94 – 0.97 and MUST be included. Deduct up to 1 mark if missed.
> 2. **Wrong direction in speed relationship:** Some students write $N_C = N_P \times R_d$ instead of $N_C = N_P / R_d$. Remember: the crown wheel is *larger* and therefore turns *slower* than the pinion.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption of straight-line motion:** Without this assumption, the torque split is undefined. Always declare it.
> 4. **Confusing transmission ratio with drive ratio:** The transmission ratio $G_t$ is applied first (to engine torque), and the drive ratio $R_d$ is applied second. Multiplying them in the wrong order leads to an inverted answer.
> 5. **Skipping the unit:** Always write Nm, rpm, and mm. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Differential assembly** is a torque-multiplying, speed-differentiating gear unit in the drive axle.
- [x] **Drive Axle Ratio** $R_d = T_C / T_P$ — typically **3:1 to 4.5:1** for passenger cars.
- [x] **Willis Equation** $N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$ — always true, governs speed differentiation.
- [x] **Backlash** must be **0.10 – 0.25 mm** for hypoid pairs; measured at 4 positions and averaged.
- [x] **Pinion preload torque** must be set before measuring backlash (typical 1.5 – 3.5 Nm).
- [x] **Hypoid oil** is a special high-pressure gear oil (GL-5, 90 grade) — not regular engine oil.
- [x] **Dismantling sequence:** drain → mark → cover off → CWP off → case out → cross out → side gears.
- [x] **Servicing** involves visual, dimensional, and surface-finish checks; defective parts replaced as matched sets.
- [x] **Adjusting backlash:** pinion shims move pinion axially; side-gear shims move case axially.
- [x] **Tooth-contact pattern** must be checked with marking compound after every backlash adjustment.
- [x] **Efficiency factor** $\eta_{fd} \approx 0.94 – 0.97$ — must be included in all torque calculations.
- [x] **Torque split** is **equal** in straight motion, **2:1** during sharp turning (open differential).
- [x] **Always state the assumption** (straight-line motion, open differential) before computing torque split.
- [x] **Mandatory safety:** safety goggles, gloves, parts tray, no force, no improvisation.
- [x] **Reassembly is the *reverse* of dismantling** with proper shim selection and torque tightening.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dismantling of gear box, inspecting components, servicing, checking the gear ratios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dismantling of gear box, inspecting components, servicing, checking the gear ratios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **gearbox** (also termed a **transmission gearbox**) is a mechanical assembly comprising a set of gears, shafts, bearings, synchronizing devices, and shift mechanisms enclosed in a housing, whose primary function is to **transmit torque from the engine to the driving wheels** while providing **variable speed-torque ratios** as demanded by the vehicle's operating conditions.

In the context of KTU's **PCAUL308 – Vehicle Systems Lab**, **Module Experiment 2** focuses on the **systematic dismantling, inspection, servicing, reassembly, and gear-ratio verification** of an automotive manual gearbox. This lab experiment develops the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO2:** Identify and inspect the components of vehicle sub-systems.
- **CO3:** Apply standard service procedures for vehicle sub-assemblies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> "A manual gearbox is a fixed-ratio stepped transmission in which gear ratio changes are achieved by the driver manually selecting appropriate gear pairs through a shift linkage, with the synchronizer unit ensuring smooth engagement."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a vehicle gearbox as the **"bicycle analogy on steroids"**:

Imagine riding a bicycle uphill versus on a flat road. On flat roads you use the big chainring (front gear) — high speed, low torque. While climbing, you shift to the small chainring — low speed, high torque. A **car gearbox does exactly this**, but with multiple gear pairs stacked inside a sealed aluminum housing, switched by synchronizers instead of your hand.

The gear ratio behaves like a **mechanical lever**:

- **Numerically HIGH ratio (e.g., 4.1:1 in 1st gear)** → Engine spins **fast**, wheels turn **slow** with **enormous torque** (used for climbing/starting).
- **Numerically LOW ratio (e.g., 1:1 in 4th gear or 0.8:1 in overdrive)** → Engine and wheels rotate at **similar speeds**, delivering **fuel-efficient cruising**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** A standard 5-speed manual passenger car gearbox typically provides overall ratios from **4.2:1 (1st gear)** down to **0.8:1 (5th/overdrive)**, with reverse around **3.5:1 to 4.5:1**. Final drive (differential) ratios are usually in the range of **3.5:1 to 4.5:1**.

### 1.3 Classification of Gearboxes Serviced in the Lab

| Gearbox Type | Construction | Vehicles | Engagement Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sliding Mesh** | Gears slide on splined shafts | Vintage vehicles (e.g., old Ambassador, Fiat) | Direct sliding engagement |
| **Constant Mesh** | All gears always in mesh; dog clutches select | Commercial vehicles, older trucks | Dog clutch collar |
| **Synchromesh** | All gears always in mesh with synchronizers | Modern passenger cars (Maruti, Hyundai) | Synchronizer ring + dog clutch |
| **Epicyclic / Planetary** | Sun, planet, ring gears | Automatic transmissions | Hydraulic band/clutch |

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Reality Check:** KTU colleges most commonly dismantle a **synchromesh gearbox (e.g., from Maruti 800 / Alto / Omni / Tata Nano)** because of its availability, simplicity, and clean construction suitable for student learning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Manual Gearbox

A manual gearbox operates on the principle of **gear ratio selection** through driver-controlled engagement of gear pairs. The fundamental kinematic relationships are:

1. **Torque Conversion** — The gearbox multiplies engine torque by the gear ratio while reducing output speed proportionally (neglecting losses).
2. **Power Transmission** — Power input (at the crankshaft/clutch) approximately equals power output (at the propeller shaft) minus frictional losses (typically **2–5%** efficient loss).
3. **Direction Reversal** — Achieved through an **idler (intermediate) gear** in the reverse gear train.

### 2.2 Gear Ratio Theory — The Mathematical Backbone

#### 2.2.1 Simple Gear Train (Two Gears)

For two meshing external spur gears, the **velocity ratio** or **gear ratio (i)** is given by:

$$i = \frac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{T_{\text{driven}}}{T_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{D_{\text{driven}}}{D_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{driver}}}{\omega_{\text{driven}}}$$

Where:

- $N$ = Number of teeth (subscripts: driven, driver)
- $T$ = Number of teeth (alternate notation)
- $D$ = Pitch circle diameter
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s or rpm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Gear Ratios:** In any pair of meshing gears, the **product of teeth × speed is constant**. If the driver has **20 teeth** at **1000 rpm**, and the driven has **40 teeth**, then driven speed = **500 rpm**, but driven torque = **2× the driver torque** (neglecting friction).

#### 2.2.2 Compound Gear Train

In a compound train, more than two gears share a common shaft. The **overall ratio** is:

$$i_{\text{overall}} = \frac{N_2 \times N_4 \times N_6 \cdots}{N_1 \times N_3 \times N_5 \cdots}$$

Where odd subscripts = driver teeth, even subscripts = driven teeth.

#### 2.2.3 Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train

Used in automatic transmissions. The ratio is computed using the **Willis Equation (Kinematic)**:

$$\frac{N_{\text{ring}} + N_{\text{sun}}}{N_{\text{sun}}} = 1 + i_{\text{carrier}}^{\text{ring}}$$

Or the tabular method:

$$\frac{\omega_{\text{sun}} - \omega_{\text{carrier}}}{\omega_{\text{ring}} - \omega_{\text{carrier}}} = -\frac{N_{\text{ring}}}{N_{\text{sun}}}$$

#### 2.2.4 Reverse Gear Ratio

In reverse, an **idler gear** is inserted between driver and driven:

$$i_{\text{reverse}} = \frac{N_{\text{reverse\,driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}} \times \frac{N_{\text{idler}}}{N_{\text{idler}}}$$

The idler's teeth cancel out (since it meshes with both), but it **reverses the direction of rotation**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gear Ratio (simple pair) | $i = N_{\text{driven}} / N_{\text{driver}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Output Speed | $N_{\text{out}} = N_{\text{in}} / i$ | rpm |
| 3 | Output Torque | $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \times i \times \eta$ | N·m; $\eta$ = efficiency (~0.95–0.98) |
| 4 | Compound Train Ratio | $i = (T_2 \cdot T_4) / (T_1 \cdot T_3)$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v = \pi \times D \times N / 60$ | m/s |
| 6 | Module (Gear Tooth Size) | $m = D / T$ | mm (standard: 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4) |
| 7 | Center Distance (Simple Pair) | $C = (D_1 + D_2) / 2 = m(T_1 + T_2)/2$ | mm |
| 8 | Module (Spur Gear) | $m = P / \pi$ (P = circular pitch) | mm |
| 9 | Addendum | $a = 1 \times m$ | mm |
| 10 | Dedendum | $b = 1.25 \times m$ | mm |
| 11 | Tooth Clearance | $c = b - a = 0.25 \times m$ | mm |
| 12 | Reverse Gear Ratio | $i_R = T_{\text{reverse\,driven}} / T_{\text{reverse\,driver}}$ | Idler cancels in magnitude |
| 13 | Overall Vehicle Ratio | $R_{\text{total}} = i_{\text{gearbox}} \times i_{\text{final\,drive}}$ | For traction calculation |
| 14 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | rad/s |
| 15 | Power | $P = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000$ | kW (when N in rpm, T in N·m) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Pipe Symbol in Tables:** In handwritten/typed exam answers, students often confuse **gear ratio direction sign**. Always remember: for **external gears**, rotation reverses at each mesh. For **internal (ring) gears**, rotation is in the **same direction** as the pinion (sun).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The gear ratio calculations performed in this lab directly relate to:

1. **Vehicle Performance Design:** Selecting 1st gear ratio so that the vehicle can start from rest on a **30% gradient** (typical Indian hill-road requirement).
2. **Fuel Economy Calibration:** Choosing top gear ratio (often overdrive < 1:1) so engine cruises at **~2000 rpm** at **80 km/h**.
3. **Towing Capacity:** Lower numerical ratios (numerically high ratios like 4.5:1) allow towing heavy loads.
4. **Diagnostics:** After dismantling, comparing **measured tooth count** against **OEM service manual** confirms whether the gearbox has been tampered with or replaced.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Compound Gear Train Gear Ratio

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Lab Task):**
A constant-mesh gearbox has the following gears on the **main shaft (output)** and **layshaft (countershaft)**:

| Gear | Location | Number of Teeth |
|---|---|---|
| 1st gear (main) | Output shaft | **40 T** |
| 1st gear (lay) | Layshaft | **15 T** |
| 2nd gear (main) | Output shaft | **30 T** |
| 2nd gear (lay) | Layshaft | **20 T** |
| 3rd gear (main) | Output shaft | **25 T** |
| 3rd gear (lay) | Layshaft | **25 T** |
| 4th gear (main) | Output shaft | **20 T** |
| 4th gear (lay) | Layshaft | **30 T** |

**Engine (input) rpm = 3000 rpm.** Calculate all gear ratios and output speeds.

---

**Step 1 — Establish the Reference Equation:**

For any pair of meshing gears, the linear velocity at the pitch point is identical:

$$v_{\text{pitch}} = \pi \cdot D_{\text{driver}} \cdot N_{\text{driver}} = \pi \cdot D_{\text{driven}} \cdot N_{\text{driven}}$$

This is because at the contact point, both gears have zero relative slip (pure rolling assumption for involute teeth).

---

**Step 2 — Derive the Ratio Formula:**

Dividing both sides by $\pi$ and $D_{\text{driver}}$:

$$D_{\text{driver}} \cdot N_{\text{driver}} = D_{\text{driven}} \cdot N_{\text{driven}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{D_{\text{driver}}}{D_{\text{driven}}} = i$$

Since $D = m \cdot T$ (module × number of teeth), we substitute:

$$i = \frac{m \cdot T_{\text{driver}}}{m \cdot T_{\text{driven}}} = \frac{T_{\text{driver}}}{T_{\text{driven}}}$$

> **Note on notation:** The above uses $N$ for rotational speed (rpm) and $T$ for tooth count to avoid confusion. Some textbooks reverse the subscripts; always state your convention.

---

**Step 3 — Apply to 1st Gear:**

The layshaft gear drives the main shaft gear (power flow: input → layshaft → main shaft → output).

$$i_{1} = \frac{T_{\text{main}}}{T_{\text{lay}}} = \frac{40}{15} = 2.667$$

$$N_{\text{out,1}} = \frac{N_{\text{in}}}{i_1} = \frac{3000}{2.667} = 1125.0 \text{ rpm}$$

---

**Step 4 — Apply to 2nd Gear:**

$$i_{2} = \frac{T_{\text{main}}}{T_{\text{lay}}} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.500$$

$$N_{\text{out,2}} = \frac{3000}{1.500} = 2000.0 \text{ rpm}$$

---

**Step 5 — Apply to 3rd Gear:**

$$i_{3} = \frac{T_{\text{main}}}{T_{\text{lay}}} = \frac{25}{25} = 1.000$$

$$N_{\text{out,3}} = \frac{3000}{1.000} = 3000.0 \text{ rpm}$$

---

**Step 6 — Apply to 4th Gear (Top Gear / Direct Drive):**

$$i_{4} = \frac{T_{\text{main}}}{T_{\text{lay}}} = \frac{20}{30} = 0.667$$

$$N_{\text{out,4}} = \frac{3000}{0.667} = 4500.0 \text{ rpm}$$

This is an **overdrive** — output shaft rotates faster than input.

---

**Step 7 — Reverse Gear Derivation:**

Reverse gear adds an idler between the lay gear and main gear:

$$i_{\text{reverse}} = \frac{T_{\text{reverse\,main}} \times T_{\text{idler}}}{T_{\text{reverse\,lay}} \times T_{\text{idler}}} = \frac{T_{\text{reverse\,main}}}{T_{\text{reverse\,lay}}}$$

Suppose $T_{\text{reverse\,main}} = 35$, $T_{\text{reverse\,lay}} = 15$, $T_{\text{idler}} = 18$:

$$i_R = \frac{35}{15} = 2.333$$

The idler's tooth count (18) appears in both numerator and denominator → cancels out → only **direction reversal** is its contribution.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — KTU Lab Calculation Summary

| Gear | Driver Teeth | Driven Teeth | Ratio (i) | Output rpm @ 3000 input | Torque Multiplier (×) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | 15 (lay) | 40 (main) | **2.667** | 1125 | 2.667 | Start / Hill climb |
| 2nd | 20 (lay) | 30 (main) | **1.500** | 2000 | 1.500 | Slow city driving |
| 3rd | 25 (lay) | 25 (main) | **1.000** | 3000 | 1.000 | Cruising city |
| 4th | 30 (lay) | 20 (main) | **0.667** | 4500 | 0.667 | Highway / Overdrive |
| Reverse | 15 (lay) | 35 (main) | **2.333** | 1286 | 2.333 | Reverse (direction reversed) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Measurement in Lab:** Students physically count teeth using a tooth gauge or by stamping (most gears have tooth count stamped on the web). The count is then compared with the OEM service manual to verify originality.

### 3.3 Module and Center Distance Verification

For 1st gear: $D_{\text{lay}} = m \times 15$, $D_{\text{main}} = m \times 40$.

Center distance between layshaft and main shaft:

$$C = \frac{D_{\text{lay}} + D_{\text{main}}}{2} = \frac{m(15 + 40)}{2} = 27.5\,m$$

For all gear pairs, the **center distance must be identical** (this is the geometric constraint of a fixed-shaft gearbox). Hence:

$$\frac{m(T_{\text{lay},1} + T_{\text{main},1})}{2} = \frac{m(T_{\text{lay},2} + T_{\text{main},2})}{2} = \cdots$$

This is achieved by **backlash adjustment via shims** between bearing races.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Manual Synchromesh Gearbox

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Engine Crankshaft]:::input --> nodeB[Clutch Assembly]:::input
    nodeB --> nodeC[Gearbox Input Shaft]:::shaft
    nodeC --> nodeD[Constant Mesh Gears]:::gear
    nodeD --> nodeE[Synchronizer Hub]:::sync
    nodeE --> nodeF[Gearbox Output / Main Shaft]:::shaft
    nodeF --> nodeG[Propeller Shaft]:::shaft
    nodeG --> nodeH[Differential Final Drive]:::shaft
    nodeH --> nodeI[Drive Wheels]:::output
    nodeJ[Shift Lever / Fork]:::control --> nodeE
    nodeK[Reverse Idler]:::gear -.-> nodeE

    classDef input fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef shaft fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef gear fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef sync fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Dismantling Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Drain Gearbox Oil]:::step --> nodeB[Step 2: Remove Gearbox from Engine]:::step
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Clean Exterior]:::step
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Mark Housing Halves]:::step
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Remove Bolts in Star Pattern]:::step
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Separate Clutch Housing]:::step
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 7: Extract Input Shaft Assembly]:::step
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 8: Extract Layshaft Assembly]:::step
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 9: Remove Shift Forks and Selector]:::step
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 10: Remove Synchronizers]:::step
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 11: Inspect All Components]:::step
    nodeK --> nodeL[Step 12: Clean in Kerosene Bath]:::step
    nodeL --> nodeM[Step 13: Measure Wear and Backlash]:::step
    nodeM --> nodeN[Step 14: Replace Defective Parts]:::step
    nodeN --> nodeO[Step 15: Reassemble in Reverse Order]:::step
    nodeO --> nodeP[Step 16: Refill with Specified Oil]:::step

    classDef step fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Component Inspection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Component Visual Inspection]:::start --> nodeB{Tooth Pitting / Spalling?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[REPLACE GEAR]:::reject
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD{Synchronizer Ring Wear Groove > 0.2 mm?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[REPLACE SYNC RING]:::reject
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF{Bearing Roughness / Noise?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[REPLACE BEARING]:::reject
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH{Shift Fork Groove Wear?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[REPLACE FORK]:::reject
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ[SEAL OIL ON SHAFT]:::approve
    nodeJ --> nodeK[REUSE COMPONENT]:::approve

    classDef start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef reject fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef approve fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Layout Schematic — Synchromesh Gearbox (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Shaft Cluster]
        A1[Input Shaft] --> A2[3rd Gear Pinion 18T]
        A1 --> A3[4th Gear Pinion 22T]
    end

    subgraph Counter[Layshaft Cluster]
        B1[Layshaft] --> B2[1st Gear 32T]
        B1 --> B3[2nd Gear 28T]
        B1 --> B4[3rd Gear 18T Drive]
        B1 --> B5[4th Gear 22T Drive]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Shaft Cluster]
        C1[Output Shaft] --> C2[1st Gear Driven 40T]
        C1 --> C3[2nd Gear Driven 36T]
        C1 --> C4[3rd Gear Synchronizer Sleeve]
        C1 --> C5[4th Gear Synchronizer Sleeve]
    end

    A2 -.meshes.-> B4
    A3 -.meshes.-> B5
    B2 -.meshes.-> C2
    B3 -.meshes.-> C3
    C4 --> C5

    classDef inputCluster fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef counterCluster fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef outputCluster fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Module 2]`**
**Define a synchromesh gearbox. List its main advantages over a constant-mesh gearbox. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **synchromesh gearbox** is a type of manual transmission in which all gears on the main and layshafts are **permanently in mesh**, and gear changes are made possible by **synchronizer units** (consisting of a brass/bronze synchronizer ring, hub, and dog clutch sleeve) that **equalize the speeds of the gear to be engaged and the shaft**, before locking the dog teeth into engagement.

**Advantages over constant-mesh gearbox:**
1. **Smooth and clash-free gear changes** — no grinding of gears during shifting.
2. **No double-declutching required** — simplified driver operation.
3. **Reduced driver fatigue** — especially in heavy traffic conditions.
4. **Less wear on dog clutches and gear teeth** — synchronizer rings absorb the shock.

*(Examiner's note: 3 points × 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Module 2]`**
**What is meant by "gear ratio"? A gearbox gives 3000 rpm at input and 1000 rpm at output in 1st gear. Calculate the gear ratio and torque amplification (assume 100% efficiency). (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Gear Ratio** is the ratio of the **angular speed of the driving gear to that of the driven gear**, or equivalently, the ratio of the **number of teeth on the driven gear to that on the driving gear**.

$$i = \frac{N_{\text{in}}}{N_{\text{out}}} = \frac{3000}{1000} = 3.0 \;:\; 1$$

**Torque Amplification** (assuming 100% efficiency, i.e., power in = power out):

$$\frac{T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}}} = i = 3.0$$

So if engine supplies **100 N·m**, the output shaft delivers **300 N·m** at 1/3rd the speed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]`**

**A gearbox has the following gear data:**

| Gear Pair | Driver Teeth (Layshaft) | Driven Teeth (Main Shaft) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | 14 | 42 |
| 2nd | 20 | 35 |
| 3rd | 25 | 30 |
| 4th | 30 | 25 |
| Reverse | 14 | 35 (with idler 18T) |

**The input shaft rotates at 3500 rpm.**

**(a)** Calculate the gear ratio and output speed for **1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th gears**. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the **reverse gear ratio**, explain the role of the idler, and describe the **dismantling procedure** of a synchromesh gearbox in **eight sequential steps**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Using the formula:

$$i = \frac{T_{\text{driven}}}{T_{\text{driver}}}, \qquad N_{\text{out}} = \frac{N_{\text{in}}}{i}$$

**1st Gear:**

$$i_1 = \frac{42}{14} = 3.0, \qquad N_{\text{out,1}} = \frac{3500}{3.0} = 1166.67 \text{ rpm}$$

**2nd Gear:**

$$i_2 = \frac{35}{20} = 1.75, \qquad N_{\text{out,2}} = \frac{3500}{1.75} = 2000.00 \text{ rpm}$$

**3rd Gear:**

$$i_3 = \frac{30}{25} = 1.20, \qquad N_{\text{out,3}} = \frac{3500}{1.20} = 2916.67 \text{ rpm}$$

**4th Gear (Overdrive):**

$$i_4 = \frac{25}{30} = 0.833, \qquad N_{\text{out,4}} = \frac{3500}{0.833} = 4200.00 \text{ rpm}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Reverse Gear Ratio:**

$$i_R = \frac{T_{\text{reverse,main}}}{T_{\text{reverse,lay}}} = \frac{35}{14} = 2.5$$

$$N_{\text{out,R}} = \frac{3500}{2.5} = 1400 \text{ rpm}$$

**Role of the Idler (18T):** The idler gear meshes between the layshaft reverse gear and the main shaft reverse gear. It **does not change the magnitude** of the gear ratio (its teeth appear in both numerator and denominator and cancel), but it **reverses the direction of rotation** of the output shaft — essential for moving the vehicle backward.

**Eight-Step Dismantling Procedure:**

1. **Drain the gearbox oil** by removing the drain plug into a clean container; inspect for metal particles.
2. **Remove the gearbox** from the vehicle using appropriate lifting equipment after disconnecting the clutch linkage, propeller shaft, and electrical connections.
3. **Clean the exterior** with kerosene/diesel and compressed air to prevent dirt ingress.
4. **Mark the housing halves** using a center punch or paint marker to ensure correct reassembly orientation.
5. **Remove the clutch housing bolts in star pattern**; separate the bell housing from the main casing.
6. **Extract the input shaft assembly** by tapping gently with a brass drift; note the shim and bearing locations.
7. **Remove the layshaft**, reverse idler, synchronizer assemblies, and shift forks; place all parts on a clean, organized tray in dismantling order.
8. **Clean each component** in kerosene, inspect for wear (gear tooth pitting, synchronizer ring wear grooves > 0.2 mm, bearing roughness), measure backlash, and replace defective parts before reassembly in reverse order.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]`**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **construction and working of a synchromesh gearbox**. What is the function of the **brass synchronizer ring**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List the **tools and equipment** required for dismantling a gearbox in the lab. Explain the **servicing procedure** for **gear teeth, bearings, and oil seals**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of a Synchromesh Gearbox:**

A synchromesh gearbox consists of the following major components:

1. **Input Shaft (Primary Shaft)** — Splined to receive clutch disc; carries pinion gears.
2. **Layshaft (Countershaft)** — Carries gears permanently meshed with input shaft gears.
3. **Main / Output Shaft** — Splined; carries synchronizer assemblies and driven gears.
4. **Synchronizer Assembly** — Comprises:
   - **Synchronizer Hub** (splined to output shaft)
   - **Synchronizer Sleeve** (slides on hub)
   - **Synchronizer Rings** (brass/bronze, with internal splines and external cone friction surface)
   - **Shift Keys and Springs**
5. **Shift Forks and Selector Mechanism** — Actuated by gear lever.
6. **Bearings** (Ball/Roller) — Support input, layshaft, and main shafts.
7. **Housing** — Cast iron/aluminum casing with bell housing and rear cover.

**Working Principle:**

When the driver pushes the gear lever toward a gear (e.g., 2nd gear):

1. The shift fork pushes the synchronizer sleeve toward the desired gear.
2. The cone surface of the synchronizer ring contacts the cone surface of the gear — **friction torque** is generated.
3. This friction **accelerates/decelerates the synchronizer hub** (which is at output shaft speed) to match the speed of the target gear.
4. Once speeds are **synchronized**, the dog teeth on the sleeve engage smoothly without clash.
5. Power flows from input → layshaft → main shaft → output → propeller shaft.

**Function of the Brass Synchronizer Ring:**

The brass synchronizer ring serves **two primary functions:**

1. **Friction Element:** Its internal cone surface creates the **friction torque** that brings the rotating masses to equal speed before dog-clutch engagement.
2. **Anti-Wear Element:** Being made of softer **brass / sintered bronze**, it acts as a **sacrificial wear surface** — it wears out faster than the harder steel gear, protecting the expensive gear teeth. When wear exceeds **0.2 mm depth**, it must be replaced.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Tools and Equipment Required:**

| Category | Tools |
|---|---|
| **Hand Tools** | Spanners (8–24 mm), Socket set, Screwdrivers (flat & Phillips), Pliers, Hammers (ball-peen & rubber) |
| **Special Tools** | Brass drift, Gear puller, Bearing puller, Snap ring pliers (internal & external), Torque wrench (¼" & ½" drive) |
| **Measuring** | Vernier caliper, Micrometer (0–25 mm), Dial indicator, Feeler gauge, Tooth gauge |
| **Cleaning** | Kerosene tank, Stiff brush, Compressed air gun, Parts tray |
| **Lifting** | Hydraulic jack, Engine hoist / chain block |

**Servicing Procedures:**

**A. Gear Teeth:**
- Visual inspection for **pitting, spalling, chipping, or wear** on tooth flanks.
- Measure tooth thickness with **gear tooth vernier** (or compare with new gear). Replacement if wear > 0.1 mm.
- Check for **scoring marks** indicating inadequate lubrication.

**B. Bearings:**
- Rotate by hand; **listen for roughness, rumbling, or clicking** — indicates fatigue.
- Check **axial and radial play** with dial indicator: typically should be < 0.05 mm.
- Wash in kerosene; **repack with grease** (for tapered roller) or replace (for sealed bearings).
- Replace any bearing showing discoloration (blueing = overheating).

**C. Oil Seals:**
- Inspect lips for **cracks, hardening, or tearing**.
- Always replace with **new seals during reassembly** — never reuse old seals.
- Apply a thin film of grease on the lip before installation.
- Ensure proper **sealant** is applied to housing joints.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing gear ratio direction:** Writing "i = N_driver / N_driven" without defining which is driving. Always state power flow.
> 2. **Missing the role of the idler:** Students write reverse gear ratio without explaining that the idler only reverses direction — it doesn't amplify torque differently.
> 3. **Skipping dismantling precautions:** Failing to mention "mark housing halves" or "drain oil first" — these are standard procedural marks.
> 4. **Forgetting backslash in tables:** Using pipe `|` symbols inside markdown tables breaks the rendering.
> 5. **No mention of backlash:** Backlash (typically 0.10–0.15 mm for automotive gears) is essential for thermal expansion and lubrication film.
> 6. **Skipping units:** Always write `N·m`, `rpm`, `mm` — bare numbers lose 0.5 mark per answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Gear Ratio Definition:** $i = T_{\text{driven}} / T_{\text{driver}}$ (dimensionless).
- ✅ **Torque Multiplier:** Output torque = Input torque × $i$ × $\eta$ (efficiency).
- ✅ **Idler Gear Function:** Reverses direction only; tooth count cancels in ratio.
- ✅ **Overdrive:** Gear ratio < 1:1; output shaft spins faster than input.
- ✅ **Module Formula:** $m = D/T$; standard modules 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4 mm.
- ✅ **Center Distance Constraint:** Must be equal for all gear pairs on a fixed-shaft gearbox.
- ✅ **Synchronizer Ring Material:** Brass / sintered bronze (sacrificial soft element).
- ✅ **Synchronizer Ring Replacement Threshold:** Wear groove depth > **0.2 mm**.
- ✅ **Dismantling Order:** Drain → External Clean → Mark Housing → Bolt Removal (star pattern) → Shaft Extraction.
- ✅ **Bearing Play Tolerance:** Axial/radial play < **0.05 mm** (passenger car).
- ✅ **Oil Seal Rule:** Always replace; never reuse on reassembly.
- ✅ **Dismantling Precautions:** Use brass drift (not steel) to avoid damaging shafts; organize parts sequentially.
- ✅ **Inspection Methods:** Visual, dimensional (gear tooth gauge, micrometer), operational (rotate bearings by hand).
- ✅ **Standard Gearbox Oil Grade:** SAE 75W-90 or 80W-90 GL-4 (varies by manufacturer).
- ✅ **Backlash Specification:** Typically **0.10 – 0.15 mm** for automotive spur gears.
- ✅ **Epicyclic Use Case:** Found in automatic transmissions (planetary gear sets).
- ✅ **Compound Gear Train:** Power flows through intermediate shaft; ratio = product of stage ratios.
- ✅ **Sliding Mesh vs. Constant Mesh vs. Synchromesh:** Evolution of gearboxes — sliding (oldest, no sync) → constant mesh (dogs) → synchromesh (modern, smooth).
- ✅ **Reverse Gear:** Always has an idler; ratio numerically between 1st and 2nd gear typically.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** Gearbox efficiency typically **95–98%**; assume 100% in basic KTU problems unless specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of steering system, servicing of steering gear box, checking for end play in shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of steering system, servicing of steering gear box, checking for end play in shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Steering System — Formal Definition

A **steering system** in an automobile is a mechanical (or electro-mechanical) assembly that converts the rotational input from the driver at the steering wheel into a precisely controlled angular displacement of the front (and sometimes rear) road wheels, thereby governing the directional path of the vehicle. The system is mandated to provide:
- **Mechanical advantage** (effort reduction via the gear box).
- **Self-centring tendency** (caster angle geometry).
- **Positive feedback and feedback feel (road feel)**.
- **Absorption of road shocks** before they reach the driver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAUL308):** The experiment is a *hands-on laboratory study* and is assessed through **continuous evaluation (CE)** and an **end-semester lab examination** based on procedure, viva-voce, and result interpretation. The student must demonstrate physical understanding of the *layout*, *gear types*, *servicing sequence*, and *metrology of end play*.

### 1.2 Steering Gear Box — Formal Definition

A **steering gear box** (also called the *steering gear assembly* or *steering reducer*) is the heart of the steering system. It is a torque-amplifying device that:
1. Receives torque from the steering column (input shaft).
2. Reduces the rotational speed and increases the torque.
3. Converts the rotation into either **linear motion** (rack-and-pinion) or **rocker-arm motion** (recirculating-ball/worm-and-roller type).

> [!NOTE]
> The overall **steering ratio** of a passenger car typically lies in the range of **15:1 to 20:1**, while heavy commercial vehicles use ratios as high as **28:1** to reduce driver effort.

### 1.3 End Play — Formal Definition

**End play** in a shaft is the *smallest axial (longitudinal) free movement* of a shaft within its bearing or housing when no external axial load is applied — but the shaft is otherwise free to rotate. It is expressed in millimetres (mm) or micrometres (µm).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab manuals, end play is also called **axial play** or **axial clearance**. It must be distinguished from **radial play** (side-to-side movement, perpendicular to the axis).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Steering System

Imagine you are steering a **traditional wooden row-boat** using a long oar pinned at the back (the *pivot*). To turn the boat left, you push the oar handle right. This is exactly what a steering system does:
- The **steering wheel = oar handle** (driver's input).
- The **steering column = oar shaft** (transmits input).
- The **steering gear box = the pivot/pin** (amplifies force, changes direction of motion).
- The **steering linkage (tie rods, drop arm) = the oar blade in water** (transmits amplified force to the wheels).
- The **road wheels = the boat itself** (turn in the desired direction).

For **end play**, think of a **spinning top** on a stick — there is a tiny gap (the end play) between the top of the stick and the spinning top. If the gap is too small, the top binds and stops; if too large, the top wobbles. The right end play is the "sweet spot."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Steering Systems

Modern automotive steering systems fall into three principal families:

#### A. Manual Steering (Mechanical)
The driver provides **100% of the steering effort** through purely mechanical linkages.
- **Rack-and-Pinion Type** — most common in modern passenger cars.
- **Recirculating-Ball / Worm-and-Roller Type** — used in older/larger vehicles.
- **Worm-and-Sector Type** — heavy commercial vehicles (LCVs, trucks).

#### B. Power-Assisted Steering (Hydraulic)
- **Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS)** — engine-driven pump + hydraulic cylinder.
- **Electro-Hydraulic Power Steering (EHPS)** — electric pump.

#### C. Electric Power Steering (EPS)
- **Column-assist EPS (C-EPS)**.
- **Pinion-assist EPS (P-EPS)**.
- **Rack-assist EPS (R-EPS)**.
- **Steer-by-Wire (SbW)**.

### 2.2 Major Components of a Steering System

| S.No | Component | Function | Typical Material |
|------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Steering Wheel | Receives driver input, sets hand position | Magnesium alloy / PU-foam-rimmed steel |
| 2 | Steering Column | Transmits rotation; may include universal joints | Mild steel tube |
| 3 | Steering Gear Box | Reduces speed, amplifies torque | Cast iron housing, hardened steel gears |
| 4 | Drop Arm / Pitman Arm | Converts gear output to angular motion | Forged steel |
| 5 | Drag Link / Centre Link | Transmits motion to both sides | Seamless steel tube |
| 6 | Tie Rod (Inner + Outer) | Connects centre link to steering knuckle | EN8/EN24 steel |
| 7 | Steering Knuckle / Hub | Holds the wheel and pivots on kingpin | Forged medium-carbon steel |
| 8 | Kingpin / Ball Joints | Allow wheel pivot (steering + suspension) | Hardened alloy steel |

### 2.3 Rack-and-Pinion Steering Geometry

In a rack-and-pinion system, the **pinion gear** is mounted on the lower end of the steering column shaft. As the pinion rotates, its teeth engage a straight-toothed bar — the **rack** — that moves linearly left or right. The rack is connected to the tie rods, which turn the wheels via the steering knuckles.

**Key geometric parameters:**

- **Pinion diameter, d_p** (mm)
- **Rack module, m** (mm)
- **Steering ratio, i_s = (steering wheel turns) / (road wheel turns)**
- **Rack travel, L_r** (mm) per full pinion revolution = $\pi \cdot d_p$

### 2.4 Recirculating-Ball Steering Gear

This system uses a **worm gear (or roller)** and a **sector gear** with a series of ball bearings recirculating through a closed circuit. The balls act as a low-friction medium, reducing wear and improving efficiency to roughly **70–80%**, compared to 50% for plain worm-and-sector.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for the ESE viva and lab record.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{r_p}$ | Tangential force at the pinion radius $r_p$ from input torque $T$ | N |
| 2 | $F_{rack} = \dfrac{T \cdot \eta}{r_p}$ | Axial force on the rack (η = gear efficiency) | N |
| 3 | $i_s = \dfrac{\theta_{wheel}}{\theta_{pinion}} = \dfrac{N_{rack}}{N_{pinion}}$ | Steering ratio (also expressed as turns lock-to-lock) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L_r = \pi \cdot d_p$ | Rack linear travel per pinion revolution | mm |
| 5 | $\delta = R \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Lateral displacement of wheel centre for steer angle α and wheelbase-derived R | mm |
| 6 | $e_p = \dfrac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{2}$ | End play (half of total axial free travel) — mean axial clearance | mm or µm |
| 7 | $e_p = A_{load} - A_{no\,load}$ | End play measured as difference in dial-indicator readings with and without preload | mm or µm |
| 8 | $Ackermann\,\% = \left(\dfrac{\delta_o}{\delta_i} - 1\right) \times 100$ | Ackermann compliance percentage (δ_o outer, δ_i inner wheel steer angle) | % |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Mechanical efficiency of steering gear | % |
| 10 | $T_{output} = T_{input} \times i_s \times \eta$ | Output torque on the drop arm / rack | N·m |

> **Note on units and notation:** Use `\vert` for absolute value, e.g., $\vert x \vert$ in equations outside tables.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Steering System

- **Driver safety** — predictable directional control at all speeds.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** — proper end play minimises rattles and steering wander.
- **Fuel economy** — EPS eliminates parasitic losses of hydraulic pumps (saves ~3–5% fuel).
- **ADAS compatibility** — modern Steer-by-Wire enables Lane Keep Assist, Auto-Parking.
- **Steering feel tuning** — luxury car manufacturers tune end play and gear ratio for "feel."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steering Ratio from Wheel Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is frequently asked in KTU viva and university examination theory papers.

**Statement:** For a rack-and-pinion system with a pinion of pitch diameter $d_p$ and a steering wheel of $D$ revolutions to move the rack by a linear travel $L_r$ (one full pinion rotation = one rack pitch travel), derive the steering ratio.

**Given:**
- Pinion pitch diameter: $d_p$ (mm)
- Pinion rotates by 1 full turn → rack moves linearly by $L_r = \pi \cdot d_p$.
- Steering wheel turns lock-to-lock: $N_{total}$ (driver input turns)
- Wheels turn lock-to-lock: $M_{total}$ (output turns of road wheel)

**Step 1 — Linear rack travel per pinion revolution**

The pinion is a circular gear of pitch diameter $d_p$. In one full revolution (angle = $2\pi$ rad), the arc length rolled out on the pitch circle equals the circumference:

$$L_r = 2\pi \cdot r_p = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{d_p}{2} = \pi \cdot d_p$$

**Step 2 — Wheel angular displacement from rack travel**

When the rack moves by $L_r$ (mm), it pulls the tie rod, which rotates the steering knuckle. If the effective steering arm radius (from kingpin to tie-rod connection) is $r_k$ (mm), the wheel turns by an angle $\theta_w$:

$$\theta_w = \dfrac{L_r}{r_k} \;\; \text{rad}$$

**Step 3 — Input-to-output ratio**

If the driver turns the steering wheel by $\theta_d$ rad for one rack travel, the steering ratio is:

$$i_s = \dfrac{\theta_d}{\theta_w} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\theta_w} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot r_k}{\pi \cdot d_p} = \dfrac{2\,r_k}{d_p}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example**

Take a Maruti Swift rack-and-pinion: $d_p = 30$ mm, $r_k = 220$ mm.

$$i_s = \dfrac{2 \times 220}{30} = 14.67$$

**Step 5 — Verify lock-to-lock**

If the steering wheel has 3.3 turns lock-to-lock and road wheels turn ~45° each side:

$$i_s = \dfrac{3.3 \times 360°}{2 \times 45°} = \dfrac{1188°}{90°} = 13.2$$

The small difference between calculated and design ratio is due to internal linkage tolerances, tyre slip and compliance.

### 3.2 Derivation: End Play from Dial-Indicator Readings

**Setup:** A dial indicator (least count = 0.01 mm) is mounted on the housing such that its plunger presses against the end face of the shaft. A gentle axial load $F_{pre}$ is applied to remove the slack on one side, and a reverse load on the other side. The two extreme dial readings are $A_1$ (max) and $A_2$ (min).

**Total axial free travel (peak-to-peak):**

$$\Delta A = A_1 - A_2$$

**End play (mean axial clearance):**

$$e_p = \dfrac{\Delta A}{2} = \dfrac{A_1 - A_2}{2}$$

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation:**

Suppose the readings taken are:

| Trial | $A_1$ (mm) | $A_2$ (mm) |
|-------|------------|------------|
| 1 | 4.52 | 4.46 |
| 2 | 4.51 | 4.47 |
| 3 | 4.53 | 4.45 |

**Step 1 — Mean of $A_1$**

$$\overline{A_1} = \dfrac{4.52 + 4.51 + 4.53}{3} = \dfrac{13.56}{3} = 4.520 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Mean of $A_2$**

$$\overline{A_2} = \dfrac{4.46 + 4.47 + 4.45}{3} = \dfrac{13.38}{3} = 4.460 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Total axial travel**

$$\Delta A = 4.520 - 4.460 = 0.060 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — End play**

$$e_p = \dfrac{0.060}{2} = 0.030 \text{ mm} = 30 \;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with manufacturer specification**

For a typical passenger-car steering input shaft, the allowed end play is **0.05 mm to 0.15 mm** (50–150 µm). A reading of 30 µm would indicate the shaft is *too tight* (binding risk) and a shim adjustment is required.

### 3.3 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Ackermann condition is essential for the *theoretical* part of the ESE lab exam.

To avoid tyre scrub, the **instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR)** of the vehicle must lie on the line extending the rear axle. The inner wheel must therefore steer through a **larger angle** than the outer wheel.

$$\cot(\delta_i) - \cot(\delta_o) = \dfrac{W}{L}$$

Where:
- $\delta_i$ = steer angle of **inner** wheel
- $\delta_o$ = steer angle of **outer** wheel
- $W$ = track width (mm)
- $L$ = wheelbase (mm)

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1** — Let the ICR lie on the extended rear axle, at a distance $R$ from the rear axle centre. Then, from the rear axle:

$$R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta_i)} = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta_o)} + W \cdot \text{geometry correction}$$

**Step 2** — Standard derivation using intersection of perpendiculars at the wheel contact patches:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance to inner wheel from ICR:} \quad R_i &= \sqrt{L^2 + (R - W/2)^2} \\
\text{Distance to outer wheel from ICR:} \quad R_o &= \sqrt{L^2 + (R + W/2)^2}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3** — Steer angles from the geometry of the steering trapezoid:

\begin{aligned}
\cot(\delta_i) &= \dfrac{R_i \cdot \cos(\delta_i) - R}{L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{after substitution and simplification}, \\
\cot(\delta_o) &= \dfrac{R - R_o \cdot \cos(\delta_o)}{L}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4** — Final compact form:

$$\cot(\delta_i) - \cot(\delta_o) = \dfrac{W}{L}$$

This is the **Ackermann condition** — the gold-standard formula for KTU board exams.

### 3.4 Servicing Steps of a Recirculating-Ball Steering Gear Box (Analytical Matrix)

| Step No. | Operation | Tool Used | Acceptance Criterion |
|----------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Drain old lubricant from gear box | Drain pan, spanner | All oil removed |
| 2 | Remove the gear box from vehicle | Ratchet set, torque wrench | Bolts torqued in reverse order on reassembly |
| 3 | Disassemble — remove sector shaft cover | Allen key, puller | No damage to mating flange |
| 4 | Inspect worm and ball nut for pitting | Magnifier, surface gauge | No visible scoring; replace if pitting > 0.5 mm² |
| 5 | Inspect recirculating-ball guide tubes | Visual + feeler gauge | Tubes clear, no metal flakes |
| 6 | Inspect sector gear teeth | Profile projector (if available) | Tooth contact > 60% face width |
| 7 | Measure sector-shaft end play | Dial indicator (LC = 0.01 mm) | $e_p \le 0.10$ mm (typical spec) |
| 8 | Check worm-shaft bearing preload | Spring balance + torque wrench | 0.5–1.5 N·m breakaway |
| 9 | Clean all parts in kerosene | Solvent tank | No debris; parts dry before reassembly |
| 10 | Lubricate with EP-90 or manufacturer-specified gear oil | Grease gun / oil can | All wear surfaces coated |
| 11 | Reassemble in reverse order | — | Tighten bolts to specified torque (e.g., 35 N·m) |
| 12 | Adjust sector mesh preload | Shim + adjuster screw | Sector-shaft free play $\le$ 0.2 mm at mid-position |
| 13 | Road test for centred steering | Test track | No wandering, no binding, return within ¼ turn |

### 3.5 Why End Play is Critical — Engineering Reasoning

- **Excessive end play** → knocking noise, steering wander, lost motion (dead-band > 5°), driver fatigue.
- **Insufficient end play** → binding, overheating, premature bearing failure, increased steering effort.
- **Asymmetric end play** → pull to one side, uneven tyre wear.

The **industry target** for steering-shaft end play is **0.05–0.15 mm**, with a tolerance band of $\pm 0.02$ mm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Steering System

This block diagram maps the energy/signal flow from the driver's hands to the road contact patches.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DRIVER INPUT\nSteering Wheel Torque] --> B[STEERING COLUMN\nUniversal Joints]
    B --> C[STEERING GEAR BOX\nRack and Pinion\nor Recirculating Ball]
    C --> D[DROP ARM and DRAG LINK\nSector Shaft Output]
    D --> E[TIE ROD ASSEMBLY\nInner and Outer]
    E --> F[STEERING KNUCKLE\nand BALL JOINTS]
    F --> G[FRONT WHEELS\nTyre Contact Patch]
    G --> H[ROAD REACTION FORCES\nFeedback to Driver]
    H -.feedback.-> A

    subgraph POWER_ASSIST [Optional Power Assist Loop]
        C2[HYDRAULIC PUMP or EPS MOTOR] --> C
    end

    subgraph TOLERANCE [Quality Control]
        T1[End Play Check] --> C
        T2[Preload Adjustment] --> C
        T3[Lubrication Schedule] --> C
    end
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Servicing Procedure for Steering Gear Box

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START] --> S1[Receive Gear Box Assembly]
    S1 --> S2[Visual Inspection for Leaks and Damage]
    S2 --> S3[Drain Lubricant]
    S3 --> S4[Disassemble Carefully]
    S4 --> S5{Clean Parts}
    S5 --> S6[Inspect Worm Gear]
    S5 --> S7[Inspect Sector Gear]
    S5 --> S8[Inspect Ball Nut and Balls]
    S5 --> S9[Measure Sector Shaft End Play]
    S9 --> S10{End Play within Spec}
    S10 -->|No| S11[Replace Bearings or Adjust Shims]
    S11 --> S9
    S10 -->|Yes| S12[Inspect Seals and Replace if Worn]
    S12 --> S13[Lubricate with EP90 Gear Oil]
    S13 --> S14[Reassemble]
    S14 --> S15[Adjust Sector Mesh Preload]
    S15 --> S16[Measure Total Free Play at Steering Wheel]
    S16 --> S17{Total Free Play less than 30 mm}
    S17 -->|No| S15
    S17 -->|Yes| S18[Mount on Test Bench and Verify Smooth Rotation]
    S18 --> S19[END]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — End Play Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Shaft on V-Block Fixture] --> A2[Apply Axial Load F Pre on Side 1]
    A2 --> A3[Dial Indicator Reads A1]
    A3 --> A4[Reverse Axial Load F Pre on Side 2]
    A4 --> A5[Dial Indicator Reads A2]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Total Travel = A1 minus A2]
    A6 --> A7[End Play = Total Travel divided by 2]
    A7 --> A8{Within Manufacturer Spec}
    A8 -->|Yes| A9[ACCEPT]
    A8 -->|No| A10[REJECT - Adjust Shims or Replace Bearing]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Ackermann Steering Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VEHICLE_TOP [Top View of Vehicle]
        direction LR
        REAR[REAR AXLE] --- C[Centre of Rear Axle]
        WL[WHEELBASE L]:::geom
        TW[TRACK WIDTH W]:::geom
        IW[INNER WHEEL Steer angle delta_i]:::wheel
        OW[OUTER WHEEL Steer angle delta_o]:::wheel
        ICR[INSTANTANEOUS CENTRE OF ROTATION]:::icr
        REAR -. extension .-> ICR
    end

    classDef geom fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef wheel fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef icr fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Process Flow — End Play Diagnostic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Shaft Removed from Assembly] --> P1[Clean Shaft and Housing]
    P1 --> P2[Mount on V-Blocks]
    P2 --> P3[Set Dial Indicator on Shaft End Face]
    P3 --> P4[Zero the Dial Indicator]
    P4 --> P5[Push Shaft Axially Forward - Record A1]
    P5 --> P6[Pull Shaft Axially Backward - Record A2]
    P6 --> P7[Repeat 3 Times for Average]
    P7 --> P8[Compute End Play = A1 minus A2 divided by 2]
    P8 --> P9{End Play within Spec}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Mark as ACCEPTABLE]
    P9 -->|No - High| P11[Shim Adjustment - Reduce Clearance]
    P9 -->|No - Low| P12[Re-machine Spacer - Increase Clearance]
    P11 --> P3
    P12 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Used:** Since physical drawings of a steering system involve complex 3D geometries (e.g., steering gear cross-section), the diagrams above present the **functional architecture and decision-tree flow** as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for non-renderable schematics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define end play in a shaft. State its typical acceptable range for a steering input shaft.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** End play is the small axial (longitudinal) free movement of a shaft within its bearing or housing, measured along the axis of rotation, with the shaft free to rotate but unloaded axially.
- (1 mark) **Distinction:** It is *axial* and not radial; it is the *gap* between two thrust faces.
- (1 mark) **Acceptable range:** Typically **0.05 mm to 0.15 mm (50–150 µm)** for steering input shafts, depending on the manufacturer specification.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*List any three types of steering gear boxes and state one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **(1 mark) Rack-and-Pinion:** Simple, compact, light, high efficiency (≈ 75–80%), direct steering feel.
2. **(1 mark) Recirculating-Ball (Worm-and-Roller):** High reduction ratio in a small package, smooth operation at high loads, used in heavy commercial vehicles.
3. **(1 mark) Worm-and-Sector:** Rugged and inexpensive, suited to older trucks and LCVs; however, lower efficiency (~50%).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE module-level lab theory questions follow the **internal choice** pattern. **Attempt either Question A or Question B in full.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Lab-Based Theory]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional layout of an automobile steering system**. List the major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, in detail, the **step-by-step procedure to service a recirculating-ball steering gear box**. Include inspection checkpoints, tools used, lubrication specification, and final adjustment procedure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional layout (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) Block diagram of the steering system (Driver → Wheel → Column → Gear Box → Drop Arm → Drag Link → Tie Rod → Knuckle → Wheel). Reference the diagram in Section 4.1.
- (3 marks) Components and functions:
  - **Steering wheel** – receives driver torque input.
  - **Steering column** – transmits rotation; may include a flexible coupling and collapsible mechanism for crash safety.
  - **Steering gear box** – reduces speed and amplifies torque; converts rotation to linear/angular output.
  - **Drop arm (Pitman arm)** – converts gear output to angular motion of the drag link.
  - **Drag link** – transmits motion to both sides of the front axle.
  - **Tie rod (inner and outer)** – connects the steering knuckle to the drag link.
  - **Steering knuckle and ball joints** – allow the wheel to pivot.
  - **Steering damper** (in some systems) – absorbs road-induced oscillations.
- (2 marks) Mention of **steering ratio** (15:1–20:1 typical for passenger cars) and **Ackermann geometry** for tyre scrub minimisation.

**(b) Servicing procedure (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Safety precautions: vehicle on level ground, battery disconnected, wheels chocked.
- (1 mark) Remove the gear box from the vehicle; tag all parts.
- (1 mark) Disassemble systematically; record shim sizes and positions.
- (1 mark) Clean all parts in kerosene; inspect worm, ball nut, recirculating balls, sector gear and bearings.
- (1 mark) Measure sector-shaft **end play** using a dial indicator (LC 0.01 mm) — accept/reject criterion $\le 0.10$ mm.
- (1 mark) Replace worn seals, balls and bearings; lubricate with **EP-90 gear oil** as per manufacturer spec.
- (1 mark) Reassemble, set sector mesh preload, check total steering wheel free play ($\le 30$ mm), and conduct a bench/road test.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Lab-Based Theory]*

**(a)** Explain the **Ackermann steering geometry** with a neat sketch. Derive the condition for no tyre scrub. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **end play** in shafts. With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **procedure to measure end play** using a dial indicator. State two consequences of (i) excessive and (ii) insufficient end play. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ackermann geometry (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) Definition: Ackermann condition ensures that the **instantaneous centre of rotation** of the vehicle lies on the extension of the rear axle, thus eliminating tyre scrub during a turn.
- (1 mark) Geometry: when turning, the inner wheel must steer through a larger angle than the outer wheel.
- (3 marks) Derivation (as in Section 3.3):
  - $\cot(\delta_i) - \cot(\delta_o) = \dfrac{W}{L}$
  - where $W$ = track width, $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta_i$ = inner-wheel steer angle, $\delta_o$ = outer-wheel steer angle.
  - Steps: draw the rear-axle extension line → set the ICR on it → drop perpendiculars from inner and outer wheel contact patches → derive cotangent relationship.
- (1 mark) Numerical example: For a car with $W = 1.5$ m, $L = 2.5$ m and $\delta_i = 20°$, compute $\delta_o$:
  - $\cot(20°) - \cot(\delta_o) = \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.6$
  - $\cot(20°) = 2.747 \Rightarrow \cot(\delta_o) = 2.747 - 0.6 = 2.147 \Rightarrow \delta_o \approx 24.97°$

**(b) End play (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** end play is the axial free movement of a shaft within its bearing/housing when no external axial load is applied.
- (1 mark) **Distinction** between axial and radial play.
- (2 marks) **Measurement procedure** (see Section 3.2):
  - Mount shaft on V-blocks.
  - Place dial indicator (LC = 0.01 mm) on shaft end face.
  - Apply axial load $F_1$, read $A_1$.
  - Reverse load, read $A_2$.
  - $e_p = (A_1 - A_2)/2$.
  - Repeat thrice and take mean.
- (3 marks) **Consequences:**
  - (i) **Excessive end play:** knocking noise, steering wander, dead-band at steering wheel, premature bearing wear, driver fatigue, road-test failure.
  - (ii) **Insufficient end play:** shaft binding, overheating, seizure under thermal expansion, increased steering effort, loss of self-centring.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (from KTU Board Examiner Perspective):**
> 1. **Confusing end play with radial play** — many students write "backlash" instead of "end play" and lose 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the dial-indicator least count** — examiners expect this explicitly (e.g., LC = 0.01 mm).
> 3. **Skipping the lubrication specification** — EP-90 (or equivalent) must be named in servicing answers.
> 4. **Drawing a poor-quality Ackermann diagram** — the ICR line on the rear-axle extension **must** be clearly marked; otherwise full marks are not awarded.
> 5. **Not mentioning tyre scrub** in the Ackermann answer — tyre scrub is the *reason* Ackermann geometry matters and earns an extra 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversion** — $30 \,\mu\text{m} \neq 0.3$ mm; double-check your decimal places.
> 7. **Skipping the "Why"** — always state the *engineering reason* (e.g., *why* end play is measured, *why* Ackermann is needed) — KTU 2024 scheme follows outcome-based education (OBE) and tests *understanding*, not rote.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam!):**

- ✅ A **steering system** converts driver rotation into wheel angular displacement via a **gear box + linkage**.
- ✅ Major components: **steering wheel, column, gear box, drop arm, drag link, tie rod, knuckle, kingpin/ball joint**.
- ✅ Three gear-box families: **rack-and-pinion, recirculating-ball, worm-and-sector**.
- ✅ **Steering ratio** for passenger cars ≈ **15:1 to 20:1**; for heavy vehicles up to **28:1**.
- ✅ **Ackermann condition:** $\cot(\delta_i) - \cot(\delta_o) = W/L$ — inner wheel steers more than outer.
- ✅ **End play** is **axial** free travel, measured using a **dial indicator** on the shaft end face.
- ✅ **Formula:** $e_p = (A_1 - A_2) / 2$.
- ✅ **Acceptance range for steering shafts:** typically **0.05–0.15 mm (50–150 µm)**.
- ✅ **Excessive end play** → noise, wander, dead-band; **insufficient** → binding, overheating, seizure.
- ✅ **Servicing sequence:** drain → disassemble → clean → inspect (worm, balls, sector, bearings) → measure end play → replace worn parts → lubricate with **EP-90** → reassemble → set mesh preload → road test.
- ✅ **Steering wheel free play** (lock-to-lock slack) should not exceed **30 mm** at the rim.
- ✅ **Mechanical efficiency** of rack-and-pinion ≈ **75–80%**; recirculating-ball ≈ **70–80%**; worm-and-sector ≈ **50%**.
- ✅ Always specify **least count**, **tolerance**, and **lubricant grade** in lab records (KTU record-evaluation criteria).
- ✅ **Total steering wheel free play** is checked at the rim with the front wheels on the ground.
- ✅ **Power-steering variants** (HPS, EHPS, EPS) reduce driver effort by **30–70%**.
- ✅ **Lab viva questions to expect:** "What happens if you over-tighten the sector-mesh adjuster?" (Answer: steering becomes hard and bearings overheat.) "Why is end play not zero?" (Answer: thermal expansion, lubrication film, manufacturing tolerance.)

---

**End of Notes — Module Experiment 2, PCAUL308 Vehicle Systems Lab, KTU 2024 Scheme.**

*Prepared in alignment with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol — NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education (OBE) compliant.*
