# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer Type)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the meaning of "205/55 R 16 91 V" printed on the sidewall of a passenger car tyre.**

**Model Answer:**
The string **205/55 R 16 91 V** is the standardised P-metric tyre identification code. It decodes as follows:
- **205** — Section Width = **205 mm** (the linear width of the inflated tyre).
- **55** — Aspect Ratio = **55%** (the sidewall height is 55% of the section width).
- **R** — Construction = **Radial** (plies run radially across the tread).
- **16** — Rim Diameter = **16 inches** (must match the wheel rim).
- **91** — Load Index = **91**, corresponding to a maximum load of **615 kg** per tyre.
- **V** — Speed Symbol = **V**, corresponding to a maximum sustained speed of **240 km/h**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four defects to be checked during a routine tyre inspection in a vehicle service workshop.**

**Model Answer:**
During routine tyre inspection, a service technician must check for:
1. **Cuts or gashes** in the sidewall or tread, especially deeper than 6 mm or exposing the body plies.
2. **Bulges or blisters** (raised areas on the sidewall indicating internal ply separation).
3. **Excessive tread wear** — measured against the **legal minimum of 1.6 mm** in India.
4. **Uneven wear patterns** (e.g., shoulder wear indicating under-inflation, centre wear indicating over-inflation).
5. **Embedded foreign objects** (nails, glass, stones).
6. **Cracking or ozone damage** to the sidewall rubber (also called *weather-checking*).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch (or functional block diagram), describe the **construction of a pneumatic tyre changer**. Explain the role of **compressed air** in the bead-seating operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car tyre is marked **225/45 R 17 94 W**.
- (i) Decode the code and state the section height, load index, and speed rating. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the **overall diameter** of the inflated tyre in mm. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) Convert the speed rating to **m/s**. **(2 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Pneumatic Tyre Changer Construction

A pneumatic tyre changer consists of the following major sub-assemblies (see Section 4.2 for the block diagram):
1. **Base and Turntable:** A heavy steel frame supporting a rotating turntable driven by a **pneumatic air motor** (typically 0.75–1.5 kW). The turntable rotates clockwise and counter-clockwise, with two jaws that clamp the wheel rim from the centre.
2. **Bead-Breaker Arm:** A pivoting arm (manually operated or air-cylinder actuated) that presses a flat blade between the tyre bead and the rim flange to break the bead seat.
3. **Dismounting/Mounting Head:** A vertical column with a swivelling head fitted with a steel mounting/dismounting tool (often a duck-head or roller type).
4. **Bead-Seating Air Jet:** A high-velocity compressed-air nozzle directed at the gap between the tyre bead and the rim. When activated, it forces the bead to "pop" against the rim flange by suddenly pressurising the air trapped between the rim and tyre.

**Role of Compressed Air in Bead Seating:**
After mounting the second bead onto the rim, a **bead-sealing pressure of 2.5 to 3.5 bar** is required to seat the bead homogeneously against the rim flange. The air jet (using shop air at 8–10 bar) achieves this in a fraction of a second, avoiding the need for a slow, dangerous manual inflation that risks bursting the tyre. The shop air is regulated down via a **pressure regulator** mounted on the machine.

#### Part (b) — Decoding 225/45 R 17 94 W

**Step (i) — Decoding:**
- Section Width: $W = 225 \, \text{mm}$
- Aspect Ratio: $AR = 45$
- Construction: $R$ (Radial)
- Rim Diameter: $d = 17 \, \text{in}$
- Load Index: $LI = 94$ → from ETRTO chart, $L_{max} = 670 \, \text{kg}$
- Speed Symbol: $W$ → $v_{max} = 270 \, \text{km/h}$

Section Height:

$$
H = 225 \times \frac{45}{100} = 101.25 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Overall Diameter:**

$$
d_{mm} = 17 \times 25.4 = 431.8 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
D = 2 \times 101.25 + 431.8 = 202.5 + 431.8 = 634.3 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step (iii) — Speed in m/s:**

$$
v_{max} = 270 \times \frac{5}{18} = 75.0 \, \text{m/s}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** List and explain **any six** criteria used for inspecting a tyre for serviceability in a workshop. Mention the legal minimum tread depth applicable in India. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tyre originally has **9.0 mm** tread depth when new. After running **55,000 km**, the measured tread depth is **2.6 mm**. The legal minimum is **1.6 mm**.
- (i) Calculate the **total wear** in mm. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the **wear rate** in mm per 10,000 km. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) Predict the **additional service life** in km before reaching the legal minimum. **(3 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Six Tyre Inspection Criteria

| # | Criterion | Explanation |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | **Tread Depth Measurement** | Measured with a tread depth gauge. The legal minimum in India (per MV Act) is **1.6 mm** for four-wheelers, measured in the central 75% of the tread width. |
| 2 | **Sidewall Cuts and Gashes** | Any cut exposing the body plies is a rejection criterion. Cuts shallower than 6 mm may be acceptable if not on the sidewall flex zone. |
| 3 | **Bulges and Blisters** | Raised bulges on the sidewall indicate broken or separated body plies. **Always reject** — risk of catastrophic blowout. |
| 4 | **Embedded Foreign Objects** | Nails, screws, glass, or stones lodged in the tread. Remove and check for inner-liner puncture using a soap-and-water bubble test. |
| 5 | **Uneven Wear Patterns** | Indicates vehicle issues: shoulder wear (under-inflation), centre wear (over-inflation), one-sided wear (misalignment). |
| 6 | **Cracking / Weather Checking** | Small surface cracks from UV/ozone exposure. If confined to the outer rubber, may be acceptable; deep cracks require rejection. |
| 7 | **Bead and Rim Condition** | Inspect the bead for damage and the rim for dents or corrosion. Damaged rims must be repaired before remounting. |

#### Part (b) — Tread Wear Calculations

**Step (i) — Total Wear:**

$$
W_t = 9.0 - 2.6 = 6.4 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Wear Rate:**

$$
R_w = \frac{6.4}{55{,}000} \times 10{,}000 = \frac{6.4 \times 10{,}000}{55{,}000}
$$

$$
R_w = 1.164 \, \text{mm per 10{,}000 \, \text{km}
$$

**Step (iii) — Additional Service Life:**

Remaining wear allowed before legal limit:

$$
W_{rem} = 2.6 - 1.6 = 1.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

Additional distance until legal limit:

$$
S = \frac{1.0}{1.164} \times 10{,}000 = 8592 \, \text{km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Conversion of inches to mm:** Many students write *1 inch = 25 mm* (rounded). The *exact* value is **25.4 mm** (per ISO). Examiners deduct marks for the rounded value in Part (b) calculations.
> 2. **Aspect Ratio interpretation:** Students often confuse "55" with the *height in mm*. Always remember: 55 is a **percentage**, not an absolute dimension. The sidewall height must be **computed**, not extracted.
> 3. **Speed Symbol trap:** The speed symbol is a *sustained* rating. A "V" rated tyre at 240 km/h means it can sustain 240 km/h, not that it cannot exceed it momentarily.
> 4. **Bead-seating safety:** The most dangerous step in a pneumatic tyre changer is the **bead-seating air jet**. Examiners specifically look for the mention of *removing the valve core first* and *standing clear* during inflation. A student who omits these will lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Wear rate units:** Always quote the wear rate as **mm per 10,000 km**, not as a raw fraction. This is the convention used in ETRTO service manuals and is the format expected by KTU examiners.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as a 5-minute revision guide the night before the exam.

- [x] **Tyre Code Format:** `P/Width/AR R/Inch LI SS` — e.g., `P 205/65 R 15 91 H`
- [x] **Section Width** is in **mm** (not inches).
- [x] **Aspect Ratio** is a **percentage** of the section width, **not an absolute height**.
- [x] **Section Height** $H = W \times (AR/100)$.
- [x] **Rim Diameter** is in **inches** and must **convert to mm** for any diameter calculation.
- [x] **Overall Diameter** $D = 2H + (d \times 25.4)$ — all in **mm**.
- [x] **Load Index** is decoded using the **ETRTO chart** — common values: 89 = 580 kg, 91 = 615 kg, 94 = 670 kg, 100 = 800 kg.
- [x] **Speed Symbol** decoding — common values: H = 210 km/h, V = 240 km/h, W = 270 km/h, Y = 300 km/h.
- [x] **Speed conversion to m/s:** multiply km/h by **5/18**.
- [x] **Legal minimum tread depth in India:** **1.6 mm** for four-wheelers, **1.0 mm** for two-wheelers.
- [x] **TWI bars** are **1.6 mm** high; when flush with the tread, the tyre is at the legal limit.
- [x] **Pneumatic Tyre Changer** uses compressed air for: (i) turntable rotation, (ii) bead-seating air jet, (iii) optional bead-breaker cylinder.
- [x] **Bead-seating pressure** is **2.5 to 3.5 bar**; shop air pressure is typically **8–10 bar** (regulated down).
- [x] **Bulges and blisters** = **immediate rejection** (ply damage).
- [x] **Cuts deeper than 6 mm** exposing the body plies = **immediate rejection**.
- [x] **Always deflate the tyre fully** (remove valve core) before dismounting.
- [x] **Pneumatic pressure formula:** $P = F/A$; contact patch $A_c = L_{max} / P_{inf}$.
- [x] **ETRTO** is the European standard authority for tyre dimensions; **DOT** is the American authority.
- [x] **Aspect ratio of 50 or less** is called a *low-profile* tyre.
- [x] **Aspect ratio of 70 or more** is called a *high-profile* tyre.
- [x] **TWI = Tread Wear Indicator**, not "Tread Wear Index" (a common student error).
- [x] **Wear rate unit** for KTU answers: **mm per 10,000 km** (not mm/km).
- [x] **Tyre code reading** is part of KTU PCAUL308 CO2: *Dismantle and inspect vehicle sub-systems*.
- [x] **Workshop safety:** always use a **tyre inflation cage** for bead-seating, never inflate a tyre without the cage if it is unmounted.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Tyre Codes, Inspection, and Pneumatic Tyre Changer**
*(Course: PCAUL308 – Vehicle Systems Lab, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel balancing: balancing of wheels by computerized wheel balancing machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wheel balancing: balancing of wheels by computerized wheel balancing machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wheel Balancing** is the automotive service procedure of equalizing the mass distribution around the axis of rotation of a pneumatic tyre-wheel assembly so that it spins without causing unwanted centrifugal vibrations, uneven tread wear, and steering component stress. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (aligned to the AUR (Automotive Workmanship) and Vehicle Maintenance lab outcomes), the operation is executed on a **Computerized Wheel Balancer (CWB)** — an electromechanical diagnostic instrument that measures the magnitude and angular position of the residual unbalance and numerically guides the technician to the optimal counter-weight placement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> "Balancing of wheels by computerized wheel balancing machine — to determine the amount and location of unbalance and to rectify it using recommended counter-weights."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine spinning a **laundry washing machine drum with a single wet sock stuck on one side**. The drum hammers violently against the cabinet, producing violent shaking. The same physical principle — *uneven mass distribution about the rotation axis generating centrifugal force* — is what makes an un-balanced car wheel cause:

- Steering wheel vibration above ~60 km/h
- Premature and patchy tyre tread wear
- Fatigue failure of wheel bearings and suspension bush
- Driver fatigue and reduced ride comfort

A **Computerized Wheel Balancing Machine** is the modern equivalent of placing an *opposite wet sock* (a precisely calculated metallic weight) at the *exact angular position* diametrically opposite to the heavy spot, so the rotation axis passes through the geometric center of mass and the wheel spins smoothly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metrics to Remember:**
> - Service rotation speed for measurement: **~100–200 rpm** (slow spin, not driving speed)
> - Drive highway speed reference: **~80–120 km/h** where imbalance symptoms become noticeable
> - Counter-weight tolerance: typically **±5 g** for passenger car wheels
> - Residual permissible unbalance: **≤ 5 g per wheel** (OEM standard)

### 1.3 Why "Computerized"?

Earlier mechanical balancers used purely spring-displacement gauges. The modern CWB uses **piezo-electric / strain-gauge force sensors** at the spindle housing, **optical encoders** (or magnetic pick-ups) to detect the angular position of the wheel, and an **onboard microcontroller** to:

1. Sample the centrifugal force vector **magnitude and phase**,
2. Resolve it into **two correction planes (inner & outer rim flanges)**,
3. Compute exact gram + angle values,
4. Display on LCD/LED with **LED positioning indicators** (often 8–16 LEDs around the wheel showing where to clip the weight).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory of Wheel Unbalance

When a tyre-wheel assembly rotates about its true axis, two independent sources of unbalance may exist:

### 2.1.1 Static Unbalance (Single-Plane / Force Unbalance)
- The **center of gravity (CG)** of the wheel is displaced from the geometric axis of rotation by a small radial distance **e** (eccentricity).
- A single heavy spot exists. Balancing requires a **single counter-weight** at 180° opposite.
- Detected even when wheel is stationary on a knife-edge or static balancer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static unbalance** is the simplest form — the wheel is "heavier on one side" but the heavy spot still lies on the central plane.

### 2.1.2 Couple Unbalance (Dynamic / Two-Plane Unbalance)
- Mass is unevenly distributed in **two parallel planes** perpendicular to the spin axis.
- CG lies on the axis, but the **principal axis of inertia is tilted** relative to spin axis.
- Cannot be corrected by a single weight; requires **two weights in two separate planes** (typically inner & outer flanges of the rim).
- This is **why modern cars always use computerized dynamic balancing** — static-only is obsolete for tubeless alloy wheels at high speeds.

### 2.1.3 Combined Unbalance
- The realistic case: both static AND couple unbalance co-exist. The CWB resolves this into two independent static corrections in the two rim planes.

## 2.2 The Unbalance Vector

The unbalance in a wheel is treated as a **vector quantity** in each correction plane:

$$\vec{U} = m \cdot r \;\; \text{[gram · millimetre]}$$

where:
- $m$ = offending mass (grams)
- $r$ = radial distance from rotation axis (mm)
- Direction of $\vec{U}$ points from the axis to the heavy spot.

## 2.3 The Centrifugal Force Generated by Unbalance

When the wheel spins at angular velocity $\omega$, the unbalance produces a rotating centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

Expressing in terms of rotational speed N (rpm):

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \;\; \text{[rad/s]}$$

$$\boxed{\,F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \right)^2\,}$$

This force rotates with the wheel, exciting the steering and suspension at the rotation frequency — felt as steering-wheel shimmy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Lab Viva & ESE:**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Unbalance vector | $U = m \cdot r$ | g·mm | Vector quantity (magnitude + angular position) |
| 2 | Centrifugal force | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | N (Newton) | Rotating disturbing force |
| 3 | Force in terms of rpm | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \right)^2$ | N | Substitute N in rpm |
| 4 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | N in revolutions per minute |
| 5 | Balancing mass | $m_c = \frac{m_u \cdot r_u}{r_c}$ | g | Counter-weight at chosen radius |
| 6 | Permissible residual | $U_{residual} \le 5 \text{ g per wheel}$ | g | OEM service limit |
| 7 | Trial weight method | $U_{corr} = m_t \cdot r_t$ | g·mm | Trial weight × trial radius |
| 8 | Plane separation (couple) | $M_c = U_1 \cdot d$ | g·mm² | Couple unbalance moment |

> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (3) is the *master equation* — it reveals why imbalance at 100 km/h (~1666 rpm) is **64× worse** than at standstill. Doubling speed quadruples force; this is why even 10 g of unbalance is catastrophic at highway speed yet invisible at parking speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Counter-Weight Mass Required for Static Balance

**Problem Setup:** A wheel of mass $M$ rotating at $N$ rpm has its CG offset by eccentricity $e$ from the spin axis. A counter-weight of mass $m_c$ is to be fixed at radius $r_c$ on the opposite side. Determine $m_c$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Unbalance Vector
The original unbalance, expressed as a mass–radius product, is:

$$U_{orig} = m_{off} \cdot e$$

where $m_{off}$ is the offending (excess) mass located at distance $e$ from the CG-axis.

### Step 2 — Apply the Static Balance Condition
For static balance, the **moment of the heavy spot about the rotation axis must equal the moment of the counter-weight** about the same axis, but acting in the **opposite sense**:

$$m_{off} \cdot e = m_c \cdot r_c$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Counter-Weight Mass

$$\boxed{\,m_c = \frac{m_{off} \cdot e}{r_c}\,}$$

This is the fundamental static-balance equation used in every service manual.

## 3.2 Derivation: Centrifugal Force at Service Speed

**Problem:** A wheel has an unbalance of $U = 100$ g·mm. Compute the centrifugal force at $N = 3000$ rpm.

### Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = 100 \pi \approx 314.159 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Force Equation

$$F_c = U \cdot \omega^2 = (100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg·m}) \cdot (314.159)^2$$

Wait — convert units carefully: $U = 100$ g·mm $= 100 \times 10^{-3}$ kg $\times 10^{-3}$ m $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m

$$F_c = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (314.159)^2$$

$$F_c = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 98696$$

$$\boxed{\,F_c \approx 9.87 \;\; \text{N}\,}$$

### Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation
A mere 100 g·mm (less than a 5-rupee coin of unbalance at 10 mm radius) generates ~10 N of rotating force at 3000 rpm — sufficient to excite visible steering-wheel vibration in a typical 1200 kg sedan.

## 3.3 Derivation: Trial-Weight Method (Used by Computerized Balancers)

The CWB uses an iterative trial-weight method to "calibrate" its own reading to a known reference. The procedure:

### Step 1 — Initial Measurement
Spin the wheel on the balancer spindle. The machine reads an apparent unbalance $U_{apparent}$ in the chosen plane.

### Step 2 — Apply a Known Trial Weight
Clip a known trial weight $m_t$ (e.g., 50 g) at a known trial radius $r_t$ on the wheel rim. Re-spin.

### Step 3 — New Measurement
The machine now reads $U_{new}$ — a vector sum of original unbalance and the trial-weight unbalance.

### Step 4 — Vector Subtraction
The "true" unbalance is the **vector difference**:

$$U_{true} = \vec{U}_{new} - \vec{U}_{trial} = \vec{U}_{new} - m_t \cdot r_t \;\; \text{(vector at known angle)}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Correction

$$m_c = \frac{U_{true}}{r_c}$$

This is what the CWB microprocessor does in milliseconds and displays on its LCD.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A passenger car wheel of total mass 12 kg is found to have its CG offset by 0.8 mm from the geometric center. Calculate:
(a) The unbalance in g·mm.
(b) The centrifugal force at 90 km/h (assume wheel rolling radius makes $\omega_{wheel} = 670$ rpm).
(c) The mass of counter-weight required if placed at $r_c = 150$ mm from the center.

### Part (a) — Unbalance in g·mm
The off-center mass equals the entire wheel mass (all 12 kg is shifted), so:

$$U = M \cdot e = 12 \times 10^3 \text{ g} \times 0.8 \text{ mm} = 9600 \text{ g·mm}$$

### Part (b) — Centrifugal Force
Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 670}{60} = 70.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

Compute force:

$$F_c = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2 = 12 \times 0.0008 \times (70.16)^2$$

$$F_c = 0.0096 \times 4922.4 = 47.25 \text{ N}$$

### Part (c) — Counter-Weight Mass
Using the static balance equation:

$$m_c = \frac{M \cdot e}{r_c} = \frac{12 \times 0.0008}{0.150} = 0.064 \text{ kg} = 64 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answer:** Apply a **64 g weight** at the **diametrically opposite location** on the rim at 150 mm radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state **both magnitude AND angular position** when specifying counter-weights. Stating only the mass loses half the marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computerized Wheel Balancer — Block Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **subsystem-level functional architecture** of a typical Computerized Wheel Balancing Machine (CWB), showing the data and signal flow from the spinning wheel to the technician's display and weight-clinching action.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[WHEEL_TYRE_ASSEMBLY] --> A2[SPINDLE_SHAFT]
    A2 --> A3[DRIVE_MOTOR_BELT_COUPLED]
    A2 --> A4[STRAIN_GAUGE_PIEZO_SENSORS_X4]
    A4 --> A5[SIGNAL_CONDITIONER_AMPLIFIER]
    A5 --> A6[ANALOG_TO_DIGITAL_CONVERTER_ADC]
    A6 --> A7[MICROCONTROLLER_DSP_UNIT]
    A8[OPTICAL_ENCODER_ANGULAR_POSITION] --> A7
    A7 --> A9[VECTOR_DECOMPOSITION_INNER_OUTER_PLANE]
    A9 --> A10[LCD_NUMERIC_DISPLAY_GRAMS_AND_DEGREES]
    A9 --> A11[LED_RING_INDICATOR_16_LEDS]
    A9 --> A12[CALCULATION_OF_COUNTER_WEIGHT_MASS_AND_ANGLE]
    A12 --> A13[TECHNICIAN_READS_DISPLAY]
    A13 --> A14[SELECT_WEIGHED_CLIP_FROM_RACK]
    A14 --> A15[CLIP_WEIGHT_AT_INDICATED_RIM_FLANGE]
    A15 --> A16[RE_SPIN_AND_VERIFY_RESIDUAL_UNDER_5G]
    A10 --> A13
    A11 --> A13
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The wheel is clamped to the **spindle shaft** (centre node of measurement).
- The shaft is suspended on **piezo-electric / strain-gauge load cells** (4 sensors typically arranged at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270° around the housing).
- The **optical encoder** at the shaft end sends a 1-pulse-per-revolution trigger, allowing the DSP to lock the unbalance-vector angular position to a fixed reference on the wheel.
- The **DSP/MCU** applies the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to extract the once-per-revolution (1×) component — this isolates the imbalance signal from random vibration noise.
- The vector is **decomposed into two rim planes** (inner flange & outer flange) by the resolver algorithm.
- Display panel shows: **LEFT plane: 25 g @ 142° | RIGHT plane: 30 g @ 318°** (example readout).
- The **LED ring** lights up the LED nearest the heavy-spot angle for fast manual indication.

## 4.2 Unbalance Classification — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[WHEEL_PLACED_ON_BALANCER] --> B2{STATIC_TEST_ON_KNIFE_EDGE}
    B2 --> B3{STATIC_UNBALANCE_PRESENT}
    B2 --> B4[STATIC_BALANCED]
    B3 --> B5[SINGLE_PLANE_CORRECTION_SINGLE_WEIGHT]
    B4 --> B6[SPIN_ON_CWB_DYNAMIC_TEST]
    B6 --> B7{COUPLE_UNBALANCE_DETECTED}
    B7 --> B8[TWO_PLANE_CORRECTION_INNER_AND_OUTER_WEIGHTS]
    B7 --> B9[FULLY_DYNAMIC_BALANCED]
    B5 --> B10[VERIFY_RESIDUAL_5G_OR_LESS]
    B8 --> B10
    B9 --> B10
```

## 4.3 Rim-Flange Anatomy & Weight Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[OUTER_RIM_FLANGE] --> C2[INNER_RIM_FLANGE]
    C2 --> C3[DROP_CENTRE_WELL]
    C3 --> C4[BEAD_SEAT]
    C1 -.OUTER_PLANE_CORRECTION.-> C5[CLIP_WEIGHT_OUTER]
    C2 -.INNER_PLANE_CORRECTION.-> C6[CLIP_WEIGHT_INNER]
    C5 --> C7[TUBELESS_ALLOY_RIM_SHOWN]
    C6 --> C7
```

**Practical Note for Students:**
- **Alloy wheels**: Use **adhesive (stick-on) weights** on the inside of the rim (hidden from view).
- **Steel wheels**: Use **spring-clip (hammer-on) weights** on the flange edge.
- Always fit the **smaller weight** to the **outer plane** and the **larger** to the **inner plane** when one plane is dominant — this minimises couple residual.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]
**Define wheel balancing. Distinguish clearly between static and dynamic (couple) unbalance.**

**Model Answer:**
*Wheel balancing* is the procedure of distributing the mass of a tyre-wheel assembly uniformly about its axis of rotation so as to eliminate unwanted centrifugal forces during spin.

| Feature | Static Unbalance | Dynamic (Couple) Unbalance |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | CG displaced from rotation axis | CG on axis, but inertia axis tilted |
| **Detection** | Wheel rolls to one side on knife edge | Only detectable when spinning |
| **Plane** | Single plane | Two parallel planes |
| **Correction** | One weight at 180° opposite | Two weights in two planes |
| **Modern relevance** | Rare in tubeless tyres | Dominant in modern cars |
| **Symbol** | $U = m \cdot r$ | $M_c = U \cdot d$ |

**(3 marks)** — Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]
**With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a computerized wheel balancing machine.**

**Model Answer:**
A computerized wheel balancing machine consists of:
1. A **spindle shaft** on which the wheel is clamped.
2. A **drive motor** (or hand-spin knob) to rotate the wheel at low speed.
3. **Piezo-electric / strain-gauge force sensors** at the spindle housing that detect the centrifugal force created by unbalance.
4. An **optical/encoder sensor** to determine the angular position of the heavy spot.
5. A **microcontroller** that resolves the force into two rim planes and computes the required counter-weight mass and angle.
6. A **digital display** (LCD/LED) showing the magnitude in grams and angle in degrees, and an **LED ring** indicating heavy-spot position.

The unbalance is computed using $U = m \cdot r$ and corrected by clipping the appropriate weight on the indicated flange.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for listing components, 1 mark for working, 1 mark for the formula $U = m \cdot r$.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-based question with internal choice. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** is to be answered. Each carries 14 marks split across two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam, Module 2 Choice Pattern]

**(a)** A passenger car wheel of mass 14 kg is found to have a CG eccentricity of 1.2 mm from the geometric axis of rotation. The wheel rotates at a speed corresponding to a vehicle speed of 100 km/h, with effective wheel rpm of 700. Calculate:
   (i) the static unbalance in g·mm,
   (ii) the centrifugal force generated at 700 rpm,
   (iii) the mass of the counter-weight required if placed at a radius of 175 mm from the centre. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram the functional components and working of a computerized wheel balancing machine. How is the residual unbalance verified after correction? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**(i) Static Unbalance:**

$$U = M \cdot e = 14 \times 10^3 \text{ g} \times 1.2 \text{ mm} = 16800 \text{ g·mm}$$

**(ii) Centrifugal Force:**

Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 700}{60} = 73.30 \text{ rad/s}$$

Substitute into $F_c = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$:

$$F_c = 14 \times 0.0012 \times (73.30)^2 = 0.0168 \times 5372.89$$

$$F_c \approx 90.26 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Counter-Weight Mass:**

$$m_c = \frac{M \cdot e}{r_c} = \frac{14 \times 0.0012}{0.175} = 0.096 \text{ kg} = 96 \text{ g}$$

**Final:** Apply a **96 g weight** at the diametrically opposite side at 175 mm radius.

**Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

A CWB contains the following functional blocks (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram):

1. **Wheel mounting spindle** with cone adapters and quick-nut clamp.
2. **Drive system** — typically a single-phase AC motor with belt or direct drive, running the wheel at ~100–200 rpm.
3. **Force sensors** — four piezo or strain-gauge sensors placed radially around the spindle housing, sensing the centrifugal force vector.
4. **Angular position sensor** — an optical encoder producing 1 pulse/revolution (1× trigger) so the DSP knows "where the heavy spot is" in space.
5. **Signal conditioner + ADC** — amplifies the sensor millivolt signals and digitises them.
6. **DSP / Microcontroller** — applies FFT, extracts 1× component, decomposes into inner & outer plane vectors, solves for the two counter-weights.
7. **Display module** — LCD showing numeric values and LED ring for visual angle indication.
8. **Calibration weights & accessories tray.**

**Residual Verification Procedure:**
- After clipping both indicated weights, re-spin the wheel.
- The CWB displays new unbalance values. The acceptable residual limit is **≤ 5 g per plane** for passenger cars.
- If exceeded, the technician repeats the procedure; if the residual is acceptable, the wheel is removed and re-fitted to the vehicle.
- Road test at 80–100 km/h confirms absence of steering-wheel vibration.

**Mark Split:** 4 marks for diagram + listing, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for residual verification.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Derive the expression for the centrifugal force generated due to wheel unbalance. A wheel has an unbalance of 250 g·mm. Compute the disturbing force when it rotates at 1500 rpm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a labelled sketch, explain the procedure to balance a four-wheeler wheel on a computerized wheel balancer. Mention the types of counter-weights used and where each is mounted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Derivation:**

Let a wheel of mass $M$ have CG displaced by eccentricity $e$ from the axis. The effective unbalance mass at radius $e$ is $M$ itself (since the whole assembly is shifted).

The centripetal acceleration required to keep this displaced mass in circular motion is:

$$a_c = \omega^2 \cdot e$$

By Newton's second law, the centripetal (and hence equal-and-opposite centrifugal) force is:

$$F_c = M \cdot a_c = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$$

Expressing in terms of rpm N:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,F_c = M \cdot e \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \right)^2\,}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $U = M \cdot e = 250$ g·mm $= 250 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m $= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m; $N = 1500$ rpm.

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$F_c = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (157.08)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 24674$$

$$\boxed{\,F_c \approx 6.17 \text{ N}\,}$$

**Mark Split:** 4 marks for derivation, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer with units.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Procedure for Computerized Wheel Balancing:**

1. **Wheel Removal:** Loosen the wheel lug nuts, lift the car with a jack, remove the wheel.
2. **Cleaning:** Remove old weights, stones, and debris from the wheel. Check for damaged rim or bulged tyre sidewall — replace if necessary.
3. **Mounting on Balancer:** Select the correct cone adaptor to match the wheel's centre bore. Place the wheel on the spindle, push it against the back cone, and tighten the quick-release nut with adequate clamping force (≈ 25–30 N·m).
4. **Enter Parameters:** Key in wheel dimensions — **diameter (in inches)**, **width (in inches)**, and **offset/distance from spindle to rim flange** for both inner and outer planes (this allows the machine to mathematically resolve the 1× force into two plane vectors).
5. **First Spin:** Lower the wheel hood/safety guard and initiate spin. The CWB measures the unbalance and displays:
   - **Inner plane: X g at angle θ₁**
   - **Outer plane: Y g at angle θ₂**
6. **Apply Inner-Plane Weight:** Identify the LED position for inner plane. Select a weight of mass X grams. Clip or stick it on the **inner rim flange** at the indicated angle.
7. **Apply Outer-Plane Weight:** Repeat for outer plane with Y-gram weight on the **outer rim flange**.
8. **Verification Spin:** Re-spin. The CWB should now display residual ≤ 5 g per plane. If not, repeat from step 6.
9. **Dismount and Refit:** Remove wheel, refit to vehicle, torque lugs in star pattern.

**Types of Counter-Weights:**

| Type | Mounting | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Spring-clip (hammer-on)** | Clipped on rim flange edge | Steel wheels |
| **Adhesive (stick-on)** | Peel-and-stick on inside of rim | Alloy wheels (cosmetic) |
| **Tape weights** | Adhesive strip with lead segments | Truck/bus wheels |
| **Fe weights** | Eco-friendly adhesive | Modern lead-free regulations |

**Mark Split:** 4 marks for procedure, 2 marks for types of weights, 1 mark for locations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the conversion $1 \text{ g·mm} = 10^{-6} \text{ kg·m}$** when computing force in Newtons — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Stating only the magnitude of the counter-weight, not the angular position** — examiner deducts 1 mark minimum; for 7-mark sub-parts, the position is worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing rpm of engine with rpm of wheel** — at vehicle speed $V$ km/h with rolling radius $R$ m, wheel rpm is $N_{wheel} = \frac{V \times 1000}{2 \pi R \times 60}$. Students who use engine rpm by mistake get wildly wrong force values.
> 4. **Not mentioning residual verification ≤ 5 g** — for 7-mark procedure questions, omitting this loses 1 full mark.
> 5. **Using the word "balancing" loosely without mentioning "static" or "dynamic"** — always specify which type you are addressing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Wheel balancing** = uniform mass distribution about the rotation axis.
- [x] **Unbalance vector:** $U = m \cdot r$ with units **g·mm** (vector — needs magnitude + angle).
- [x] **Static unbalance:** CG off-axis; corrected by **one** weight at 180°.
- [x] **Dynamic / Couple unbalance:** inertia axis tilted; corrected by **two** weights in two rim planes.
- [x] **Centrifugal force equation (master equation):** $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \right)^2$ — quadratic in N.
- [x] **Counter-weight formula:** $m_c = \frac{m_u \cdot r_u}{r_c}$.
- [x] **Computerized balancer components:** Spindle, force sensors (piezo/strain-gauge), optical encoder, ADC, DSP/MCU, LCD + LED ring.
- [x] **DSP method:** FFT extracts the 1× (once-per-revolution) component to isolate unbalance.
- [x] **Two-plane resolution:** inner flange (closer to hub) and outer flange (rim edge) planes.
- [x] **Residual acceptance limit:** **≤ 5 g per plane** (passenger car standard).
- [x] **Counter-weight types:** Spring-clip (steel wheels) and Adhesive (alloy wheels).
- [x] **Always specify both magnitude and angular position** of counter-weight in answers.
- [x] **Unit conversion check:** 1 g·mm = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m; 1 rpm = $\frac{2 \pi}{60}$ rad/s.
- [x] **Service spin speed** is low (~100–200 rpm), but real-world disturbance occurs at driving speed.
- [x] **Trial-weight calibration** is used by CWBs to verify their own accuracy.
- [x] **Practical tip:** Always remove old weights and clean the rim before rebalancing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel alignment: checking the camber, caster, king pin inclination, toe in and toe out with computerized wheel alignment machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL308 - VEHICLE SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Wheel alignment: checking the camber, caster, king pin inclination, toe in and toe out with computerized wheel alignment machine # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Wheel Alignment **Wheel Alignment** is the process of adjusting the angles of the wheels of a vehicle to the manufacturer's specifications. It is a critical automotive maintenance procedure that ensures the wheels are set to run straight and true, optimizing tire wear, fuel efficiency, handling, and overall driving safety. In a typical KTU lab setting (PCAUL308 - Vehicle Systems Lab), this is performed using a **computerized wheel alignment machine** which uses precision optical sensors (CCD cameras) or laser technology to measure wheel angles with an accuracy of **± 0.01°**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wheel alignment is NOT the same as wheel balancing. Alignment adjusts the *angles* of the suspension, while balancing corrects the *mass distribution* of the tire/wheel assembly. ## 1.2 The Five Critical Wheel Alignment Parameters ### 1.2.1 Camber **Camber** is the tilt of the wheel from the vertical when viewed from the front of the vehicle. - **Positive Camber:** Top of the wheel tilts *outward* (away from the vehicle centerline). - **Negative Camber:** Top of the wheel tilts *inward* (toward the vehicle centerline). - **Zero Camber:** Wheel is perfectly vertical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Value:** Passenger cars typically have **-0.5° to +0.5°** of camber. Race cars use aggressive negative camber (up to **-3°**) for cornering grip. **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of camber like a person's stance when standing. If your toes point straight ahead, that's zero camber. If you roll your feet outward (duck-footed), that's positive camber. If you roll them inward (pigeon-toed), that's negative camber. ### 1.2.2 Caster **Caster** is the tilt of the steering axis from the vertical when viewed from the side of the vehicle. It is analogous to the angle of a motorcycle's steering fork. - **Positive Caster:** Steering axis tilts *backward* (top toward rear of vehicle). Most modern cars use this. - **Negative Caster:** Steering axis tilts *forward* (top toward front of vehicle). Used in older vehicles and some heavy trucks. - **Zero Caster:** Steering axis is perfectly vertical. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Value:** Modern passenger cars have **+2° to +5°** of positive caster. This provides directional stability - the "self-centering" feel of the steering wheel. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a shopping cart wheel. The fork that holds the wheel leans backward. This is positive caster. Now push the cart - it naturally wants to go straight. This is exactly what caster does for your car. ### 1.2.3 King Pin Inclination (KPI) / Steering Axis Inclination (SAI) **King Pin Inclination (KPI)**, also known as **Steering Axis Inclination (SAI)**, is the tilt of the steering axis from the vertical when viewed from the front of the vehicle. In modern vehicles, the king pin is replaced by the **MacPherson strut** or **double wishbone** suspension geometry, so the term **SAI** is more commonly used. - KPI tilts *inward* at the top (toward the vehicle centerline) and outward at the bottom. - **Standard Value:** Typically **+10° to +14°** for most passenger cars. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why KPI Exists:** KPI causes the front wheels to lift slightly when turned, creating a self-centering torque and reducing the tendency to wander. It also reduces the scrub radius. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine leaning a bicycle fork inward at the top. When you turn the handlebars, the wheel naturally wants to return to center. That's KPI at work. ### 1.2.4 Toe (Toe-in and Toe-out) **Toe** is the direction the wheels point relative to the vehicle's centerline when viewed from above. - **Toe-in:** Front of the wheels point *toward* each other. The wheels are "pigeon-toed." - **Toe-out:** Front of the wheels point *away* from each other. The wheels are "duck-footed." - **Zero Toe:** Wheels point perfectly straight ahead. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Value:** Most rear-wheel-drive cars use **0° to +0.2°** (slight toe-in) at the front wheels. Front-wheel-drive cars typically use **0° to -0.2°** (slight toe-out). **Intuitive Analogy:** Stand up and look down at your feet. If your toes point inward, you have toe-in. If they point outward, you have toe-out. Toe-in makes the car more stable in a straight line; toe-out improves turn-in response. ### 1.2.5 Computerized Wheel Alignment Machine A **computerized wheel alignment machine** is a precision diagnostic tool that uses **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) cameras**, **infrared sensors**, or **laser beams** to measure the geometric angles of the wheels. The machine consists of: - **Sensor heads** (mounted on each wheel) - **Wheel clamps** (with reflectors/targets) - **Computer console** (with vehicle-specific database) - **Calibration plates** and **turn plates** (for steering angle measurement) > [!NOTE] > **Accuracy:** Modern alignment machines measure to **± 0.01°** accuracy, far exceeding manual string-and-plumbob methods (± 1°). ## 1.3 Summary Callout Box > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Big Picture - Why Alignment Matters:** > - **Safety:** Prevents pulling to one side > - **Tire Life:** Even wear, prevents premature tire replacement > - **Fuel Economy:** Reduces rolling resistance > - **Handling:** Optimal steering response and stability > - **Suspension Life:** Reduces stress on bushings and joints # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Analysis of Each Parameter ### 2.1.1 Camber - In-Depth Theory Camber affects the **contact patch** (the portion of the tire touching the road) in the following ways: **Effects of Excessive Positive Camber:** - Outer edge of tire wears out faster - Vehicle pulls toward the side with more camber - Reduced cornering grip **Effects of Excessive Negative Camber:** - Inner edge of tire wears out faster - Steering feel becomes heavy - Can improve high-speed cornering in performance applications **Mechanical Reasoning:** A tilted wheel has a different effective slip angle on the inner and outer edges during cornering, which is why camber must be balanced with the vehicle's primary use. ### 2.1.2 Caster - In-Depth Theory Caster is the key to **steering stability** and **self-centering** behavior. The physics behind it: 1. When the wheel is turned, the contact patch moves behind the steering axis intersection with the ground 2. This creates a **mechanical trail** (or simply "trail") - the distance between the steering axis intersection and the tire contact patch 3. The trail creates a moment arm that generates a self-centering torque > [!IMPORTANT] > **Caster Trail vs. Mechanical Trail:** > - **Mechanical Trail** = perpendicular distance from steering axis to contact patch > - **Caster Trail** = the same as mechanical trail, measured at the ground ### 2.1.3 KPI/SAI - In-Depth Theory KPI works in conjunction with **camber** to define the **included angle** and **scrub radius**. **Included Angle = KPI + Camber** The **scrub radius** is the distance at the road surface between the king pin axis intersection and the tire contact patch center. A positive scrub radius means the contact patch is inside the steering axis; negative means outside. ### 2.1.4 Toe - In-Depth Theory Toe is the most sensitive alignment parameter - even **0.1°** deviation can cause noticeable tire wear. There are two main types: 1. **Static Toe:** Measured with the vehicle stationary on a level surface 2. **Dynamic Toe:** The actual toe angle during driving (affected by suspension compliance) **Toe causes "opposite wear patterns"** - excessive toe-in causes feathering on the outer edge; excessive toe-out causes feathering on the inner edge. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Standard Range (Passenger Car) | Engineering Use | |-----------|---------|-----------|-------------------------------|-----------------| | Camber | $\theta_c$ | $\theta_c$ = camber angle | $-0.5°$ to $+0.5°$ | Determines tire wear pattern and cornering grip | | Caster | $\theta_{cs}$ | $\theta_{cs}$ = caster angle | $+2°$ to $+5°$ | Steering self-centering torque | | KPI/SAI | $\theta_{kpi}$ | $\theta_{kpi}$ = king pin angle | $+10°$ to $+14°$ | Reduces scrub radius, stabilizes steering | | Included Angle | $\theta_i = \theta_{kpi} + \theta_c$ | $\theta_i$ = included angle | Sum of KPI and camber | Used to detect bent parts (KPI stays constant, camber changes) | | Toe (per wheel) | $\alpha_t = \arctan(\frac{\Delta x}{L_w})$ | $\Delta x$ = toe difference, $L_w$ = wheelbase | $\pm 0.2°$ | Steering stability vs. turn-in response | | Total Toe | $\alpha_{tot} = \alpha_{L} + \alpha_{R}$ | $\alpha_L$ = left toe, $\alpha_R$ = right toe | $0°$ to $+0.4°$ | Combined effect on vehicle direction | | Scrub Radius | $r_s = r_{wheel} \cdot \sin(\theta_{kpi})$ | $r_{wheel}$ = tire radius | $\pm 25 \text{ mm}$ | Brake pull, steering effort | | Mechanical Trail | $t_m = r_{wheel} \cdot \sin(\theta_{cs})$ | $r_{wheel}$ = tire radius | $20 \text{ mm}$ to $50 \text{ mm}$ | Self-centering torque | | Setback | $\Delta_s = x_{L} - x_{R}$ | $x_L, x_R$ = wheelbase offsets | $\vert \Delta_s \vert \leq 1°$ | Detects bent axle/cradle | | Thrust Angle | $\theta_{th} = \theta_{R,r} - \theta_{L,r}$ | $\theta_{R,r}, \theta_{L,r}$ = rear individual toe | $\vert \theta_{th} \vert \leq 0.2°$ | Detects bent rear axle | > [!NOTE] > **All angles in the formula sheet are measured in DEGREES unless specified. Modern alignment machines display all parameters in real-time with digital precision.** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In the automotive industry, wheel alignment is performed: - **At the factory** as part of quality control (every car rolling off the line is aligned) - **At every tire replacement** (preventive maintenance) - **After any suspension repair** (corrective maintenance) - **After collision damage** (safety-critical) - **At periodic service intervals** (typically every 10,000 km or annually) **Industry Standard:** The **Vehicle Manufacturers Association (VMA)** publishes alignment specifications for every make/model, and these are stored in the alignment machine's database for instant recall. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Scrub Radius The scrub radius is a critical geometric parameter derived from the interaction between KPI and camber. Let's derive it step by step. ### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup Consider a wheel with: - Tire radius $r$ - King pin inclination angle $\theta_{kpi}$ - Camber angle $\theta_c$ The king pin (steering axis) intersects the ground at a point offset from the wheel centerline. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the tire radius vector. The tire contact patch center is at distance $r$ below the wheel center. $$r_{cp} = r \quad \text{(vertical distance from wheel center to contact patch)}$$ **Step 2:** Define the steering axis tilt. The steering axis tilts inward at angle $\theta_{kpi}$ from vertical. $$\Delta_{horizontal} = r \cdot \sin(\theta_{kpi})$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the steering axis intersection with the ground. The steering axis, if extended to the ground, intersects at horizontal distance $\Delta_{horizontal}$ from the wheel center. **Step 4:** Apply the definition of scrub radius. Scrub radius is the horizontal distance between the tire contact patch center and the steering axis intersection with the ground, projected along the wheel's centerline. $$r_s = r \cdot \sin(\theta_{kpi}) - r \cdot \sin(\theta_c)$$ **Step 5:** Simplify the expression. $$\boxed{r_s = r \cdot \big[ \sin(\theta_{kpi}) - \sin(\theta_c) \big]}$$ **Step 6:** For small angles (where $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ in radians), this simplifies to: $$r_s \approx r \cdot (\theta_{kpi} - \theta_c) \quad \text{(radians)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - If $r_s > 0$: Positive scrub radius (contact patch is inward of steering axis). Found in most rear-wheel-drive cars. > - If $r_s = 0$: Zero scrub radius. Found in many FWD cars (MacPherson strut). > - If $r_s < 0$: Negative scrub radius. Found in some heavy trucks and FWD cars. ## 3.2 Derivation of Mechanical Trail from Caster **Step 1:** Set up the side view geometry. Consider the wheel viewed from the side. The steering axis tilts at caster angle $\theta_{cs}$ from vertical. **Step 2:** Identify the contact patch location. The contact patch is at the bottom of the wheel, at radius $r$ below the wheel center. **Step 3:** Project the steering axis to ground level. The steering axis, projected down to ground level, intersects at a horizontal distance from the contact patch: $$t_m = r \cdot \tan(\theta_{cs})$$ **Step 4:** For small angles ($\tan(\theta) \approx \theta$ in radians): $$t_m \approx r \cdot \theta_{cs} \quad \text{(radians)}$$ $$\boxed{t_m = r \cdot \tan(\theta_{cs})}$$ **Step 5:** Numerical example. For a tire with $r = 0.3 \text{ m}$ and caster $\theta_{cs} = 4° = 0.0698 \text{ rad}$: $$t_m = 0.3 \times 0.0698 = 0.0209 \text{ m} \approx 21 \text{ mm}$$ This matches the typical **20 mm to 50 mm** mechanical trail range for passenger cars. ## 3.3 Derivation of Toe Angle from Linear Measurement In manual alignment, toe is often measured as a linear distance difference between the front and rear of the wheels. Let's derive the relationship. **Step 1:** Define the measurement. Let $\Delta x$ be the difference in distance between the front of the wheels and the rear of the wheels (measured at the tire tread, across the wheel diameter $d$). **Step 2:** Apply the small angle approximation. For small angles, the toe angle $\alpha_t$ (in radians) equals the difference in arc length divided by the wheel diameter: $$\alpha_t = \frac{\Delta x}{d}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to degrees: $$\alpha_t \quad [°] = \frac{\Delta x}{d} \times \frac{180°}{\pi}$$ **Step 4:** Numerical example. If $\Delta x = 2 \text{ mm}$ and $d = 600 \text{ mm}$ (typical wheel diameter): $$\alpha_t = \frac{2}{600} \times 57.296 = 0.191° \approx 0.19°$$ This is a typical toe-in value for a passenger car. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Parameter on Vehicle Behavior | Parameter | Tire Wear | Steering Pull | Steering Feel | Cornering Grip | Fuel Economy | |-----------|-----------|---------------|---------------|----------------|--------------| | Camber (Positive) | Outer edge wear | Pulls outward | Light | Reduced | Slight decrease | | Camber (Negative) | Inner edge wear | Pulls inward | Heavy | Increased (in turns) | Slight decrease | | Caster (Excess Positive) | Even | None (if equal) | Heavy, slow return | Improved stability | Slight decrease | | Caster (Negative) | Even | Pulls both directions | Light, twitchy | Reduced stability | Slight decrease | | KPI (Excessive) | Inner edge | Pulls to side with less KPI | Heavy | Reduced | Slight decrease | | KPI (Insufficient) | Outer edge | Pulls to side with more KPI | Light | Reduced | Slight decrease | | Toe-in (Excess) | Outer edge feather | Pulls outward | Stable straight-line | Reduced turn-in | Decreased | | Toe-out (Excess) | Inner edge feather | Pulls inward | Sharp turn-in | Unstable straight-line | Decreased | > [!NOTE] > **The analytical matrix is the examiner's favorite** - it tests whether you can correlate geometric misalignment with on-road symptoms. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Wheel Alignment Parameter Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Wheel Alignment System] --> B[Primary Angles] A --> C[Secondary Angles] A --> D[Diagnostic Angles] B --> B1[Camber] B --> B2[Caster] B --> B3[Toe - Toe In / Toe Out] C --> C1[King Pin Inclination KPI] C --> C2[Steering Axis Inclination SAI] C --> C3[Included Angle] D --> D1[Scrub Radius] D --> D2[Mechanical Trail] D --> D3[Setback] D --> D4[Thrust Angle] B1 --> E1[Vertical Wheel Tilt Front View] B2 --> E2[Steering Axis Tilt Side View] B3 --> E3[Wheel Direction Top View] C1 --> F1[Steering Axis Tilt Front View] C2 --> F2[Modern Equivalent of KPI] C3 --> F3[Sum of KPI and Camber] D1 --> G1[Brake Pull and Steering Effort] D2 --> G2[Self Centering Torque] D3 --> G3[Detects Bent Axle] D4 --> G4[Detects Bent Rear Axle] ``` ## 4.2 Computerized Wheel Alignment Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: Vehicle Preparation] --> P2[Step 2: Wheel Clamp Installation] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Sensor Head Mounting] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Vehicle Selection in Database] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Level Compensation] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Wheel Runout Compensation] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Initial Measurement] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Cross Reference with Specs] P8 --> P9{All Within Spec?} P9 -->|Yes| P10[Step 9: Generate Report and Sign Off] P9 -->|No| P11[Step 10: Identify Adjustment Points] P11 --> P12[Step 11: Adjust Camber Caster and Toe] P12 --> P13[Step 12: Re-measure and Verify] P13 --> P14{Within Spec Now?} P14 -->|Yes| P10 P14 -->|No| P12 ``` ## 4.3 Front Suspension Geometry Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Suspension_Components SPR[Spring and Strut] CNT[Control Arm] TIE[Tie Rod] BJ[Ball Joint] WH[Wheel and Tire] end subgraph Adjustment_Points A1[Camber Adjustment
via Strut Bolts or Camber Plate] A2[Caster Adjustment
via Cradle Bolts] A3[Toe Adjustment
via Tie Rod Sleeve] end SPR --> WH CNT --> WH TIE --> BJ BJ --> WH A1 --> SPR A2 --> CNT A3 --> TIE ``` ## 4.4 Wheel Alignment Parameter Visual Reference Table | Parameter | View Direction | Measurement Type | Adjustment Location | |-----------|----------------|------------------|---------------------| | Camber | Front view | Angle from vertical | Strut bolts, camber plates, eccentric bolts | | Caster | Side view | Angle from vertical | Cradle bolts, control arm bushings, camber bolts | | KPI/SAI | Front view | Angle of steering axis | Non-adjustable (defined by suspension design) | | Toe | Top view | Angle from centerline | Tie rod sleeves, eccentric cam bolts | | Included Angle | Front view | Sum of KPI and camber | Diagnostic (detects bent parts) | | Scrub Radius | Front view | Horizontal distance | Non-adjustable (function of KPI/camber) | | Mechanical Trail | Side view | Horizontal distance | Non-adjustable (function of caster) | | Thrust Angle | Top view | Rear axle symmetry | Non-adjustable (detects bent rear) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Block Diagram Insight:** Of the 5 main parameters, only **3 are adjustable** in most modern vehicles: Camber, Caster (in some designs), and Toe. KPI, SAI, and scrub radius are defined by the suspension geometry and cannot be field-adjusted (only corrected by replacing bent components). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define camber and explain its effect on tire wear. (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** **Model Answer:** **Camber** is the tilt of the wheel from the vertical when viewed from the front of the vehicle. It is measured in degrees and can be positive (wheel tilts outward at the top), negative (wheel tilts inward at the top), or zero (perfectly vertical). **Effect on tire wear:** - **Excessive positive camber** causes wear on the **outer edge** of the tire tread, as the wheel is effectively "leaning out." - **Excessive negative camber** causes wear on the **inner edge** of the tire tread, as the wheel is "leaning in." - **Zero camber** (ideal) ensures even wear across the entire tread surface. The standard camber value for passenger cars is **-0.5° to +0.5°**. Any deviation beyond this range leads to premature tire wear, typically noticed as one-sided tread wear after 5,000 to 10,000 km of driving. **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tire wear effects]** ### Question 2: What is the difference between KPI and SAI? Why is KPI typically non-adjustable? (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** **Model Answer:** **KPI (King Pin Inclination)** is the tilt of the king pin (the pivot axis of the wheel) from vertical, measured from the front view. It was used in vehicles with physical king pins in the steering knuckle. **SAI (Steering Axis Inclination)** is the modern equivalent of KPI, referring to the tilt of the steering axis (defined by the strut or control arm pivots) from vertical, also measured from the front view. **Why KPI is non-adjustable:** KPI/SAI is defined by the **physical geometry of the suspension components** - specifically, the relative positions of the upper and lower ball joints (or strut mount and lower pivot). Changing KPI would require: 1. Replacing bent components (control arms, knuckles, struts) 2. Using offset bushings (which is a major repair, not a routine adjustment) Since KPI directly affects the included angle and scrub radius, the vehicle manufacturer sets it during the design phase and does not provide field adjustment. **Any deviation from specification indicates a bent or damaged component** that must be replaced. **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark for KPI, 1 mark for SAI, 1 mark for non-adjustability reason]** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice) ### Question A: Wheel Alignment Parameter Analysis (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a) Explain with neat sketches the following wheel alignment parameters: (i) Camber, (ii) Caster, (iii) King Pin Inclination. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Camber (2.5 Marks):** Camber is the vertical tilt of the wheel when viewed from the front of the vehicle. It is the angle between the wheel's centerline and the true vertical. - **Positive Camber:** Top of wheel tilts outward - **Negative Camber:** Top of wheel tilts inward - **Zero Camber:** Wheel is vertical **Sketch description (ASCII representation):** ``` Front View of Vehicle | | | | (Zero Camber) | | | | | | _____/ \_____ | Left Right | | Car | \____ _________/ ``` ``` Positive Camber \ / \/ (Top tilted out) /\ / \ ``` **Effect:** Affects tire wear pattern, cornering grip, and steering pull. **(ii) Caster (2.5 Marks):** Caster is the tilt of the steering axis from the vertical when viewed from the side of the vehicle. It is the angle between the steering axis and the vertical plane. - **Positive Caster:** Steering axis tilts backward (top toward rear of vehicle) - **Negative Caster:** Steering axis tilts forward (top toward front of vehicle) **Sketch description:** ``` Side View of Front Wheel Positive Caster Negative Caster | / | / | (steering axis) / (steering axis) | \ / | \ / |___\_______________/___ Contact Patch ``` **Effect:** Positive caster provides steering self-centering and directional stability. Standard value: **+2° to +5°**. **(iii) King Pin Inclination (2 Marks):** KPI is the tilt of the king pin (or steering axis) from the vertical when viewed from the front of the vehicle. The king pin is angled inward at the top. **Sketch description:** ``` Front View \ / Steering axis (tilted inward at top) \/ /\ / \ Wheel KPI angle: angle between steering axis and vertical ``` **Effect:** KPI works with camber to reduce scrub radius and provides steering returnability. Standard value: **+10° to +14°**. --- **(b) A vehicle has the following alignment readings: Left camber = -0.8°, Right camber = +0.2°, Left caster = +3.5°, Right caster = +3.7°, Left toe = +0.1°, Right toe = +0.3°. Calculate: (i) Cross camber, (ii) Cross caster, (iii) Total toe, and (iv) Indicate which parameters are within specification (standard values: Camber ±0.5°, Caster +2° to +5°, Toe 0° to +0.4°). (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Cross Camber (2 Marks):** Cross camber is the difference between left and right camber angles, calculated by subtracting the more negative (or less positive) from the more positive (or less negative). $$\text{Cross Camber} = \theta_{c,R} - \theta_{c,L}$$ $$\text{Cross Camber} = (+0.2°) - (-0.8°) = 0.2° + 0.8° = +1.0°$$ **Cross Camber = +1.0°** (Left is more negative than right) **(ii) Cross Caster (2 Marks):** Cross caster is the difference between left and right caster angles. $$\text{Cross Caster} = \theta_{cs,R} - \theta_{cs,L}$$ $$\text{Cross Caster} = (+3.7°) - (+3.5°) = +0.2°$$ **Cross Caster = +0.2°** **(iii) Total Toe (2 Marks):** Total toe is the sum of individual toe angles. $$\alpha_{tot} = \alpha_{L} + \alpha_{R}$$ $$\alpha_{tot} = (+0.1°) + (+0.3°) = +0.4°$$ **Total Toe = +0.4°** (Slight toe-in) **(iv) Specification Check (1 Mark):** | Parameter | Measured | Specification | Status | |-----------|----------|---------------|--------| | Left Camber | -0.8° | -0.5° to +0.5° | **OUT OF SPEC** | | Right Camber | +0.2° | -0.5° to +0.5° | Within spec | | Left Caster | +3.5° | +2° to +5° | Within spec | | Right Caster | +3.7° | +2° to +5° | Within spec | | Left Toe | +0.1° | 0° to +0.4° | Within spec | | Right Toe | +0.3° | 0° to +0.4° | Within spec | | Cross Camber | +1.0° | $\vert \text{Cross} \vert \leq 0.5°$ | **OUT OF SPEC** | | Cross Caster | +0.2° | $\vert \text{Cross} \vert \leq 0.5°$ | Within spec | | Total Toe | +0.4° | 0° to +0.4° | Within spec (borderline) | **Conclusion:** The **left camber** and **cross camber** are out of specification. The vehicle requires camber adjustment, typically achieved by replacing or shimming the strut mount, using camber bolts, or adjusting the control arm position. --- ### Question B: Computerized Wheel Alignment Procedure (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question - Alternative Choice]** **(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the procedure for performing wheel alignment using a computerized wheel alignment machine. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Computerized Wheel Alignment Procedure:** **Step 1: Vehicle Preparation (1 Mark)** - Park the vehicle on a level alignment rack (level within ± 1 mm/m) - Check tire pressure and inflate to manufacturer specifications - Inspect tires for uneven wear, damage, or improper sizes - Verify that the steering wheel is centered - Check that the vehicle is at curb weight (no excess load) - Ensure the fuel tank is at least half full - Bounce the suspension to settle it (release stored energy) **Step 2: Wheel Clamp and Sensor Installation (1 Mark)** - Mount wheel clamps on each of the four wheels - Ensure clamps are tight and centered on the wheel - Attach sensor heads (CCD cameras or laser units) to the clamps - Connect sensors to the alignment computer via cable or wireless - Mount the steering wheel holder to prevent the wheel from moving **Step 3: Vehicle Specification Selection (1 Mark)** - Select the vehicle make, model, and year in the computer database - Choose between standard specifications or customer preferences - Print the target specification sheet for reference **Step 4: Compensation Procedures (1 Mark)** - **Level Compensation:** Drive the vehicle onto level plates or use the alignment rack level sensor - **Wheel Runout Compensation:** Rotate each wheel 180° to compensate for tire/wheel runout (eccentricity) **Step 5: Measurement Phase (1 Mark)** - The computer automatically measures: - Camber (front and rear) - Caster (front only) - Toe (front and rear) - KPI/SAI (front only) - Included angle, scrub radius, mechanical trail (calculated) - Thrust angle and setback (calculated) - Results are displayed as live readings and compared to specifications - Color-coded: Green (in spec), Yellow (borderline), Red (out of spec) **Step 6: Adjustment Phase (1 Mark)** - Adjust camber via strut bolts, camber plates, or eccentric bolts - Adjust caster via cradle bolts or control arm eccentric bolts - Adjust toe via tie rod sleeves or eccentric cam bolts - Make small adjustments and re-measure after each change **Step 7: Verification and Sign-off (1 Mark)** - Perform final measurement to verify all parameters are within specification - Print the before-and-after alignment report - Attach the report to the job card - Return the vehicle to the customer with the report **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Vehicle on Alignment Rack] --> B[Pre-alignment Inspection] B --> C[Mount Wheel Clamps and Sensors] C --> D[Select Vehicle in Computer Database] D --> E[Perform Level and Runout Compensation] E --> F[Measure All Alignment Angles] F --> G{All Parameters in Spec?} G -->|No| H[Identify Out of Spec Parameters] H --> I[Adjust Camber Caster or Toe] I --> F G -->|Yes| J[Print Final Report] J --> K[End: Return Vehicle to Customer] ``` --- **(b) A tire has a radius of 0.32 m. If the caster angle is +4.5° and KPI is +12°, calculate: (i) Mechanical trail, (ii) Scrub radius, and (iii) Explain why the scrub radius is critical for braking stability. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Tire radius $r = 0.32 \text{ m}$ - Caster angle $\theta_{cs} = +4.5° = 0.0785 \text{ rad}$ - KPI $\theta_{kpi} = +12° = 0.2094 \text{ rad}$ **(i) Mechanical Trail (2 Marks):** The mechanical trail is calculated using the formula: $$t_m = r \cdot \tan(\theta_{cs})$$ $$t_m = 0.32 \times \tan(4.5°)$$ $$t_m = 0.32 \times 0.0787 = 0.0252 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{t_m = 25.2 \text{ mm}}$$ This is within the typical range of **20 mm to 50 mm** for passenger cars, providing good steering self-centering. **(ii) Scrub Radius (3 Marks):** For a wheel with zero camber (or neglecting camber for this calculation): $$r_s = r \cdot \sin(\theta_{kpi})$$ $$r_s = 0.32 \times \sin(12°)$$ $$r_s = 0.32 \times 0.2079 = 0.0665 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{r_s = 66.5 \text{ mm}}$$ This is a **high positive scrub radius** (typical positive scrub radius is 0 to 50 mm). A scrub radius of 66.5 mm would result in: - Heavy steering effort - Significant brake pull during uneven braking - Steering disturbance from road irregularities > [!NOTE] > For a more accurate calculation including camber effect: $r_s = r \cdot [\sin(\theta_{kpi}) - \sin(\theta_c)]$. If camber is -0.3°, then $r_s = 0.32 \times [\sin(12°) - \sin(-0.3°)] = 0.32 \times [0.2079 + 0.0052] = 0.0682 \text{ m} = 68.2 \text{ mm}$. **(iii) Importance of Scrub Radius for Braking Stability (2 Marks):** Scrub radius is critical for braking stability because of the following reasons: 1. **Brake Pull Effect:** When the vehicle brakes, braking force is applied at the contact patch. If the contact patch is offset from the steering axis (non-zero scrub radius), the braking force creates a **torque** about the steering axis. This torque causes the wheel to "scrub" or pivot, leading to **brake pull** to one side. 2. **Uneven Braking:** If the left and right wheels have different brake friction (e.g., due to a stuck caliper or worn pad), the resulting torques are unequal, causing the steering wheel to pull toward the side with more friction. This effect is **directly proportional to the scrub radius** - larger scrub radius means more pull. 3. **Steering Disturbance:** Any road irregularity that creates a side force at the contact patch will generate a steering disturbance proportional to the scrub radius. A smaller scrub radius provides better straight-line stability and reduces driver fatigue. 4. **Disc Brake Rotor Thermal Effects:** In disc brakes, heat can cause one rotor to expand more than the other, creating uneven braking forces. With a large scrub radius, this would cause dangerous steering pull. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning - Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing positive and negative scrub radius:** Remember - positive scrub radius means the contact patch is *inboard* of the steering axis (toward the vehicle centerline). Negative means *outboard*. > 2. **Mixing units:** Use consistent units (convert degrees to radians for trigonometric functions, or use degrees with a calculator set to degree mode). > 3. **Forgetting the small angle approximation:** If using $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ (radians), state this assumption clearly. > 4. **Not including camber in scrub radius:** For more accurate results, always include the camber term. > 5. **Confusing KPI and caster:** KPI is measured from the **front**, caster is measured from the **side**. Students often swap these in answers. ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Exam - Wheel Alignment - Mark Loss Hotspots:** > > 1. **Marks lost for not drawing sketches (2-3 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly allocate 1.5 to 2 marks for neat sketches/diagrams in 14-mark questions. Always draw the front view for camber/KPI and side view for caster. > > 2. **Marks lost for incorrect sign convention:** Toe-in is positive (+), toe-out is negative (-). Students often mix up the convention. Always state the convention at the start of your answer. > > 3. **Marks lost for not stating units:** Always write "°" (degrees) after angle values. For linear measurements, use "mm" or "m" consistently. > > 4. **Marks lost for missing effect on tire wear:** For any alignment parameter, the examiner expects at least one sentence on its effect on tire wear. This is worth 1-2 marks. > > 5. **Marks lost for incorrect formulas:** The most commonly confused formulas are: > - Mechanical trail: $r \cdot \tan(\theta_{cs})$ (NOT $\sin$) > - Scrub radius: $r \cdot \sin(\theta_{kpi})$ (NOT $\tan$) > - Toe angle: $\arctan(\Delta x / d)$ or $\Delta x / d$ for small angles > > 6. **Marks lost for not specifying the view direction:** Always state "viewed from the front," "viewed from the side," or "viewed from above" when defining alignment parameters. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Quick-Reference Bullet Checklist: - **Wheel Alignment:** Adjustment of suspension angles to manufacturer specifications; affects tire wear, handling, safety, and fuel economy. - **Camber:** Wheel tilt from vertical (front view). Standard: **-0.5° to +0.5°**. Positive = top out, Negative = top in. Affects tire edge wear. - **Caster:** Steering axis tilt from vertical (side view). Standard: **+2° to +5°** (positive). Provides self-centering. Mechanical trail = $r \cdot \tan(\theta_{cs})$. - **KPI/SAI:** Steering axis tilt from vertical (front view). Standard: **+10° to +14°**. Inward tilt at top. Non-adjustable in field. - **Toe:** Wheel direction relative to centerline (top view). Standard: **0° to +0.2°** (slight toe-in for RWD). Toe-in positive, toe-out negative. Most sensitive parameter. - **Included Angle:** KPI + Camber. Diagnostic tool for detecting bent parts (KPI stays constant, camber changes when parts bend). - **Scrub Radius:** Horizontal distance from steering axis to contact patch. $r_s = r \cdot \sin(\theta_{kpi})$. Critical for brake pull. Target: small positive or zero. - **Mechanical Trail:** Horizontal distance from steering axis intersection to contact patch (side view). $t_m = r \cdot \tan(\theta_{cs})$. Provides self-centering torque. - **Setback:** Difference in wheelbase between left and right wheels. Detects bent axle/cradle. $\vert \text{Setback} \vert \leq 1°$. - **Thrust Angle:** Angle of rear axle centerline vs. vehicle centerline. Detects bent rear axle. Target: $\leq 0.2°$. - **Computerized Alignment Machine:** Uses CCD cameras or lasers for ±0.01° accuracy. Steps: prep, clamp, sensor mount, spec select, compensate, measure, adjust, verify, report. - **Adjustable vs. Non-Adjustable:** Camber, Caster (some), Toe are field-adjustable. KPI, SAI, scrub radius, mechanical trail are design-defined. - **Tire Wear Patterns:** Outer edge = positive camber or toe-in. Inner edge = negative camber or toe-out. Feathering = toe problem. Both edges = underinflation. - **Cross Camber/Caster:** Difference between left and right. Maximum allowable: **0.5°** for most vehicles. Causes pull if excessive. - **Total Toe:** Sum of left and right toe. Specification: typically **0° to +0.4°**. - **Alignment Sequence:** Always adjust **rear first**, then front. Adjust **camber before toe**. Check **caster last** (least frequent adjustment). - **Pre-Alignment Checks:** Tire pressure, tire condition, suspension play, wheel bearings, steering linkage. Aligning a worn suspension is wasteful. - **KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, expect a 7+7 split: definition + sketch (7 marks) and calculation/analysis (7 marks). Always carry units in calculations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Exam Note:** Wheel alignment is a high-scoring topic in the Vehicle Systems Lab. Focus on the relationships between parameters, the effects of misalignment, and the correct adjustment procedures. Always include neat sketches in your descriptive answers - they are worth 2-3 marks by themselves.
